Yeah. Which didn't involve him missing any grand slams and confused a lot of — cause a lot of confusion around the tennis world. Anyway, that's a — that's a deeper hole to go into. Anyway, the point is this is a guy that in weeks where we've been talking about what it is to be professional and what it is to be absolutely elite at the top of your sport, this is a guy that does it. And actually, where I've been most interested in tennis over the last few years from a national perspective, which leads us into another part of this chat, Charlie, is how good Italy have been over the last few years at developing players. They've got four top 20 men's players.

四、意大利 vs. 英国：一场关于俱乐部与冠军的对照实验

Stubbings： Um, which in a truly global sport is a really significant achievement.

Charlie： You got s Kaboli.

Stubbings： Yeah. Beritini.

Charlie： Beritini.

Stubbings： And

Charlie： there is another one. Um,

Stubbings： Arnaldi also didn't he got to the semis of French. Yeah.

Charlie： And then you've got Jasmine Palini on the women's side.

Stubbings： Yeah.

Charlie： So, this is a system which is working. Um now we've spoken in recent weeks in the whole debate over um pay at the Grand Slams how c certain of the top players are wingy about how much they get paid by the grand slams and went on a rather pathetic sort of — not even a media strike a sort of media downscaling of their media commitments. Um

Stubbings： it was nothing. No one noticed anything.

大满贯东道国的巨额精英开支——却几乎没有冠军

Charlie： Yeah, forget it. Um so um well but what one thing we have noted is is that those um associations that host the grand slams which is tennis Australia the French tennis federation the lawn tennis association that's here in England and the US tennis association so those tennis associations that are lucky enough to host a grand slam hand a very significant amount of money each of them to the elite tennis development schemes of their country, right? So, it's a real windfall for those countries who who happen to be lucky enough to host one of the Grand Slams, it means that their elite systems get vast amounts of money chucked at them basically to subsidize the development of elite players.

Stubbings： Does it work?

Charlie： Well, based on the data, you'd have to say it doesn't work.

Stubbings： It doesn't work. Charlie, when we — we were just chatting about this, I was thinking, hang on a second,

Charlie： because just to be clear, I've been — well, we — we've certainly had our views on on that pay discussion and — and one of it being, you know, very much so if you take more money for the players, then you're taking it away from the places that actually go and develop players. But then actually, like if you put the counter to that, you go, well, yeah, develop players in a very specific region, actually in the richest countries because they're getting all of this revenue anyway. So, what I would naturally want to see from that is if that money is going somewhere, I want to see some results. Yeah. And if you look across, we — we did a — you know we had a look at those uh four countries and what how many real champions they've produced — the — the statistics are quite frightening. So the UK let's call it the UK you know has had Murray and Radicanu in the last 20 years. France — could you could you name how many Grand Slam champions France has had

触目惊心的数据

Stubbings： in the last 20 years? Um I don't think there's been one

Charlie： Emily Morzmo 2000 — and that is 2006 she did it so I'm — think you know

Stubbings： and that was Wimbledon so it's actually

Charlie： Australian Open she won two

Stubbings： actually more than 20 years ago

Charlie： Australia Sam Stoza and Ash Barti

Stubbings： US obviously both Williams Serena and Venus Williams Sloan Stevens Sophia Kennan Coco Goff Madison Keys so a couple of things stand out there — no men — no man from Australia Since Huitt in 2002, no man from France since Yanik know in 1983, no man from the US since Andy Rodic in 2003. So Andy Murray is the only man from the Grand Slam countries to have won in the last 20 years. And guess what? What do we all know about Andy Murray and his training?

穆雷、亨曼、康塔——真正的顶级英国球员没一个是 LTA 培养的

Charlie： He had absolutely nothing to do with the law and tennis association. his mother didn't believe in the law and tennis association and his family who were reasonably wellto-do sent him to the um Sanchez Casal's um academy in Barcelona at a very young age as also happened to Johanna Cont who's one of the very few British women to have um reached an elite level in in in the last 20 years and if you look back at other great British players Tim Henman grew up in a tennis middle upper middle class tennis family and was sent to a specialist private tennis school called Reeds near Cobb. Again, not in the LTA sets that that was run by, I think, by John Lloyd or David Lloyd. Um, Greg Rossetski grew up in Canada and you know, transferred to England once he was already a professional tennis player. Then you've got um Cameron Nory who is one of the other players who's reached the top 10 in recent years who grew up in New Zealand and then went to — to tennis um played tennis in in the US college system. So you — where you get to amongst even forgetting the grand slam champions even talking about British players who have really made anything of themselves I've come across two — Carl Edmund and um Jack Draper who have what you might call been LTA kids. Now Jack — um Jack Draper it should be noted is the son of the former chief executive of the LTA Roger Draper. Um, so it's, you know, again, from a very upper middle class background, it's slightly hard to imagine that he wouldn't have had the resources to make it even if the LTA had not eased his way. So we're already looking at Carl Edmund being the only example of a successful British male tennis player,

每年 5000 万英镑——买了什么？

Stubbings： all female really, I think maybe other than Radukanu, who has been helped through the system by the LTA. Now the LTA spends 15 million pounds a year on their youth program. Sorry, by their youth program, I mean their elite youth programs. That's on these kids, right? 15 million pounds a year. And then on top of that, they spend another 35 million on putting on professional events to — with — with the express purpose of helping these elite players have events to play in so they can climb the ladder.

Charlie： So in that time, 15 million at the elite — 20 years criy. So 300 million, let's say, has developed.

Stubbings： No one.

Charlie： Well, Radicon maybe.

Stubbings： to — to win the top tournaments — because we can't — because we can't call Murray — as you said coming — you can't say that that money has gone to him. So suddenly you're looking at that going, "Wow, okay. Well, where's that going?" Right?

关键差别：意大利的俱乐部密度

Charlie： It's — it's a quite extraordinary allocation of capital. Now, if I sort of dug into it a bit looking at the Italian system, right? Because actually the Italian tennis federation, despite not having a grand slam, still generates about the same amount of money as the LTA. And I wondered why that was. So I looked into it and I found that we've got 2,000 tennis clubs in this country, in Britain. In Italy, they have 4 and a half thousand. Now, if you want to try and work out why Italy is producing so many top players, it's a country of almost identical size to the UK

Stubbings： and it has more than twice as many tennis clubs.

Charlie： Clubs. Interesting.

Stubbings： Now, clubs is important. I should tell you why clubs — because this is also important in golf as well. So, the LTA puts a huge amount of money into tennis courts, right? That includes many, many, many thousands of courts in public parks, right? So, this is a court where if Charlie and Charlie want to go and have a game of tennis, we can go and play tennis for either free or — or for not very much money. But that isn't going to develop an elite player. Develop an elite player, you have to have a coach. And the coach ain't going to be in the — in the course in the park. The — the coach is going to be at the club. And this is, I think, where the problem lies and why — why Italy are doing so well is they've got many more coaches. So that will be it because my counter to any critique that it was leveling was going to be

Charlie： the real mandate of the LTA is to get participation up in this country. Last year I think you had 5.8 million adults playing in the UK.

Stubbings： You had 4 million children

Charlie： 3,000 park courts renovated is your point

Stubbings： judged on those — that was the most successful year. So for the money that — let's say I think participation had — I had here 26 million which is court funding schools competition. That's doing great.

Charlie： That's doing really well.

Stubbings： That's doing really well.

Charlie： And therefore, you know,

Stubbings： Grand Sand Champions we all want,

Charlie： but the most important part of their allocation is to get people playing the game. Okay, they're doing well. That doesn't mean they get a let off for spending 156 million pounds a year on an elite division that doesn't throw out anything. And you know cumulatively over the 20 years if I was just going through it in that — you know worked out that basically 2025 they got 48 million

Stubbings： from Wimbledon which was about 90% of Wimbledon's profits as we know previously.

Charlie： So you know just cumulatively over the years that comes out — let's say they've received about 700 million pounds over the 20 years.

Stubbings： Yeah.

Charlie： From Wimbledon

Stubbings： of which they're spending about half on the professional game — the elite game.

Charlie： Yeah. Right.

Stubbings： Yeah. A third. Yeah. Cuz 16 of let's say — No. Because if they're paying £35 million on professional events and 15 million on the on their elite program.

Charlie： Oh, you're saying professional events. I'm saying professional events is a — I wouldn't — I wouldn't class that as a contributor to us developing Grand Slam champions.

Stubbings： That's how they see it. That's how — that's why the LTA do it. So that's their excuse for spending a lot of money on elite events is to help develop elite players.

Charlie： Wow.

Stubbings： In in this country because that's what gives them the ability to give them wild cards and to get more British players playing. And

Charlie： that's just like it just gives it's just cannon foder to some of the other Arthur Ferry.

Stubbings： Yeah. New exception to the rule, Charlie. No. How — how many wild cards were there across?

一个可疑的伤病模式

Charlie： Yeah. Yeah. No, indeed. So, so anyway, but I thought it was interesting just looking — looking at the Italians um and saying it's a really good control study where you've got a — a country which is the same size in Europe, both developed tennis economies. Um both of them would have aspirations to producing elite players. Both of the national federations are making kind of quite a bit of money, can afford to do things properly. And the Italian system is pushing money into clubs and keeping the club system alive and kicking. And the English system is not pushing money into clubs. The English system is pushing money into on the one hand public participation courts on the on the other hand what you might call hotousing. Now there's one other bit I just want to just raise and this may be circumstantial but the top two current players who have come through that system are Jack Draper and Emma Raticanu. What is the thing that is regarded as holding both of their careers back?

Stubbings： Injuries.

Charlie： Terrible injuries. I mean, like catastrophic series of injuries as quite young players. We're not talking about players in their mid-30s. We're talking about players in their early to mid20s. Injury after injury after injury. I wonder if there might be some attention which the LTA needs to pay to their performance program.

Stubbings： Interesting point.

Charlie： um because it seems a bit too much of a coincidence to have your two main stars that you spent so much money on unable basically to get themselves on the court in one piece hobbling around on crutches and casts all the time.

Stubbings： Well, they should be in the best years of their career. Well, coming into the best years of their career.

对照高尔夫：另一种模式

Charlie： Absolutely. Yeah. Um so look, it's an interesting thing and if you contrast it with golf where Britain overindexes and does exceptionally well, what do we have many more of than other

Stubbings： golf clubs?

Charlie： Golf clubs. Yeah,

Stubbings： golf clubs with qualified PGA professionals who will pick up a young player, teach them the basics, get them up to a certain level. Now, you're kind of limited, aren't you? Because golf, you can't have a park court in the same way that you can have a golf course generally has to be a golf club. So, so the system you're almost forced into that model without having to make a decision.

Charlie： That that is true. So, yes, we have both many more golf courses and many more golf clubs cuz in golf the two things are basically the same. Um but I would probably again think about — and do you remember one of the most interesting things you said about Norway and football last week was how many coaches they have.

Stubbings： Yeah. The sheer number of qualified

Charlie： coach — sorry there was one coach every 325 people.

Stubbings： I'm going to come back to what the number of qualified golf coaches is in Britain. I'm going to guess that it's pretty much the highest per head of population of anywhere. I mean uh certainly in Scotland I mean it's quite extraordinary I mean Scotland I think have got some sort of golf pro factory somewhere

Charlie： and they roll them out wherever you go in the world if you go and play golf at some obscure place in Thailand it'll say um PGA golf professional Hamish Matagot it's quite extraordinary they get everywhere. So anyway we — we'll come back to it um a non

Stubbings： I'm just saying I although we're criticizing I still am not convinced that the players deserve any more money than they've been getting at the moment the tennis players. Oh, right. Yeah. No, not

Charlie： even even if we're questioning whether that distribution of cash, I still think a 19 or 20% increase on your Wimbledon prize money was was pretty good. So, yeah, we're not changing — I'm not changing my position on that, but I do think having a look at the allocation of that and actually then holding to a standard of

Stubbings： um of success is is important.

Charlie： Agreed. Charlie, it's your time again because we're back into um your — your favorite friends on fall like slightly slightly different this time

五、马术障碍赛：一项曾经的黄金时段体育，如今被三个联赛撕裂

Stubbings： but it is in fact a sport that we haven't covered. So, it's going to get its moment in the sun, um, of which there is a lot at the moment. And it is

Charlie： the sport of showj jumping. Now, at a moment in time when Charlie Junior was a glimmer in his parents' eye

Stubbings： um in an era known as uh the 1980s, one of the biggest sports in this country and indeed a massive sport in the rest of the world, but a massive sport in the UK was the sport of showj jumping. And it will surprise Charlie Jr. to learn that showj jumping was a very regular sport being screened on national TV, the BBC. Um, but sometimes even on prime time. We're talking about BBC1 evening shows on show jumping in the run-up to Christmas. Um, so there were two massive events. The horse of the year show, which is now at the NEC, which still attracts 60,000 to it. Charlie, 60,000. No, go home.

Charlie： That's why it's at the NEC, not in one day, but over 3 days. 60,000.

Stubbings： Wow. Wow.

Charlie： Um, and then you've got the London International Horse Show, which is held in the run-up to Christmas, which was founded in 1907 and was generally held at Olympia throughout my — well since 100 years or so it's been held at Olympia. And then it moved to Excel in 2021. It's just — it's just in that sort of brief period before Christmas, in the sort of week before Christmas. and that over, you know, over a three 3-day period will have an attendance of around about 100,000. So, in terms of a live attendance sport, show jumping still is a nonniche sport. It lost its position as the BBC cut back on its sporting coverage in general. And particularly, do you remember grandstand, which was a Saturday afternoon show that had to cover — it had to fill up 3 to four hours of TV. therefore did cover various different sports, particularly the ones which weren't necessarily be that expensive to cover and show jumping is a very easy sport to cover. It's a — it's a — it's an arena where you just have a camera traversing like this. So once the BBC was cutting back on its sports coverage and once grandstand went that then massively cut back on the amount of showjumping that was on TV. But the showj jumping stars back in the 1980s were national figures and in some cases global figures. Um there was an exceptionally famous notorious man called Harvey Smith um who distinguished himself by flicking a V sign um live on TV to the judges after they um judged that he brought a fence down that he didn't think he had or some such thing.

Stubbings： Quite black and white.

Charlie： And then — and then there were Whit the Whitaker brothers John and Michael um who were again national figures. And don't forget the showjam is also a big Olympic sport. So you had the combination of big in the Olympics and then also big on in the sort of annual TV events and this type of stuff.

一个理想的观赏运动

Stubbings： It's — it's one of my favorite Olympic events to watch — like heart and mouth when you watch them go around the course and you see it's a brilliant spectator sport and you see the points going at each time and you go — is she going to go around clean? Is — is it going to go around clean?

Charlie： But you've just nailed it Charlie which is it's a brilliant spectator sport. Now as everyone knows I'm a racing guy but as a pure spectator sport show jumping is a better sport. Right. Racing needs the betting and the whole complexity of the bloodtock industry to really make it interesting. Otherwise, it is a bit flies running up a wall. Show jumping is an entrancing, really tense, really powerful spectator sport. So, what's happened to it? Well, what's happened to it is is that gradually over time, it has in professional terms become a bit niche. Okay. So what we've got is we've got a um uh a sort of premier um league which is sponsored by Lonines um and that league has been going for I think around about 20 years um and it's reasonably prominent. Um it does okay and there's nothing — it's — it's not that there's anything particularly wrong with it. It's just it hasn't somehow managed to capture the attention of of the public. Right. So it — it'll often take place in some of the most prominent places um around the world and it's a sort of quite glamorous — you know rather like sell GT is going to fall into that bucket.

Stubbings： It's — it's Miami it's Paris it's Rome it's London. In fact they're in London uh in two weeks time and at the Royal Hospital Chelsea. Okay

Longines Global Champions Tour 与 Frank McCourt 的入场

Charlie： that's where they hold it — where where the Chelsea Flower Show takes takes place. The Lonie's Global Champions Tour is what it's called. And it was founded in 2006, so 20 years ago, by a former Olympic gold medalist, Dutch Olympic gold medalist called Yan Tops. Great name.

Stubbings： Good name.

Charlie： Great name.

Stubbings： The Dutch have some great ones, don't they? I mean, they

Charlie： I mean, if you're going to be a sportsman to be called Yan Tops.

Stubbings： Yeah. Known to your mates as double. Um anyway, so look, Yan Topps created this um — this global tour and it's as I said basically done okay. Um the — the annual prize money is about 30 million euros, right? And that's spread through about — you know about a hundred riders will take part in this tour. They can't all compete in every single event because they don't have enough horses to be able to to to to do that. So you will see different riders competing in in different events. if you've got your, you know, whatever it is about 15 um races, then maybe one rider might take part in eight or 10 if they're one of the top riders. So, um on it goes. Anyhow, um at some point around about I think 2013, um Yan Topps takes in a minority investor and this is a man called Frank McCort. Frank McCort is also the owner of

Charlie： Marles

Stubbings： Marles and has been um since he bought it from my old um uh

Charlie： was it Louis Dra from the Lou Der from my old partners at Sunderland the Louis Drafers family. Um so he's been at Marseilles for about the same time actually that he's since he became an investor in in show jumping in around about 2014. So he was an investor and a director of the Lines Global Champions Tour until he left and that happened around about 2021 and his shares were bought by a very controversial person also somebody who's got a history as a football club and a guy called Lars Vinhost who's bit her Berlin um and the whole thing went to court. Now, it's not quite clear why it went to court or exactly what was being examined in court, but it ended up in court. And that tends to mean that the separation was not necessarily amicable. I can't comment more than that. I don't know. Um anyhow, lo and behold, 5 years later, um up pops McCort again and says, "I'm going to launch my own global show jumping league. is going to be called the Premier Jumping League, the PJL, and it's going to launch in March 2027, but not with 30 million of prize money. 300 million.

Stubbings： Yeah, right,

Charlie： Charlie.

三个联赛并存：叙事的碎片化

Stubbings： My alarm bells when you — my alarm bells started ringing exactly at this point when we've been critical of other leagues and organizations that have broken away.

Charlie： And we haven't even got to the bottom of that, Charlie. There's another league.

Stubbings： So,

Charlie： there's another one called — Right. So, so, so, so the other one which is run by the global governing body, the show jumping equivalent of FIFA, the FEI. Okay. They once they run something called um well, it used to be called um the uh the nation's cup, but rather unfortunately they've renamed it the n um the league of nations, which I don't know if anyone knows their geopolitical history, but that was the rather useless organization that Mussolini and Hitler trampled all over in the 1930s. So, I'm not sure it was the most is the cleverest name for it. Anyhow, the League of Nations is the thing where where riders compete amongst — uh representing their countries and that's what qualifies you for the World Championship and the Olympics. So, if you want to ride in the Olympics, you're going to need to compete in that. If you want to make some money, you're going to need to compete in the global champions tour. And now there's also going to be the Premier Jumping League. So effectively, you're going to have for the top riders, you're going to have these three competitions, all of them different, all of them happening mostly at different times, but sometimes at the same time, and all of them critically confusing everybody,

叙事清晰度：伯尼·埃克莱斯通的启示

Stubbings： you know, who's winning, which is more important, why is this person riding in this series one week and writing in that series the next week, the whole thing is to my mind, you know, when we think about the — the critical elements of narrative clarity, right? What Bernie Eckles achieved with Formula 1 was narrative clarity. What we're seeing here is fragmentation and albeit there's a lot of money washing around. Is it really ultimately going to be good for this sport to have three separate competitions? Tazan — also like to be fair to something like a — live and when the critique we've you know put on other people's doorsteps is that — is that a sustainable model moving forward because 300 million in prize money you got 16 teams reportedly right you got multiple venues around one of those reportedly correct me if I'm wrong $50 million the first franchise yeah I mean you know this is big money and again you know the sale GP conversations we had have for a very specific reason it's the values in the eye of the beholder you can't always judge or or pertain to understand why something has been priced at that, especially when it's a sport like this which you've highlighted may have some serious premium value to it. But the challenges that you've got in generating enough of that money consistently to keep paying out the 300 million in prize money plus the cost plus it plus+ when that's on one individual PI if let's say you put a lot more pressure on yourself. So just from, you know, it's hard to comment at the beginning of something and — and you don't want to be inherently negative about things when they're starting out, but the model was just something I was looking at going, "Oh, no, not again." Yeah, sure. And you sort of think Krikey O'Reilly, you know, of all sports, you know, of a type of sport that can't really afford to have lots of different competitions and to not have a sort of clear way of of being, this type of sport is very much very much that type of sport. Now, one of the things interesting that McCort is investing a lot of money into as part of the Premier Jumping League, he's putting a lot of money into the media side of it. He believes, and I think your reaction earlier on, which I found absolutely fascinating as a sports marketing person, was a show jumping bloody exciting, right? Is that the main problem here is exposure.

Charlie： That's his — that's his view. Is that actually and — and I when I hear that for most sports, I go, well, you would say that, wouldn't you? You love that sport. But actually when it comes to show jumping, I find it so exciting and you find it so exciting that actually this is potentially the case.

Stubbings： Sorry.

Charlie： Sebastian So wins the marathon. The first question we asked.

Stubbings： Great, but where can we watch him next week?

Charlie： Yeah.

Stubbings： I watch show jump in in the Olympics, right? I love it. I get hooked for a few days. Where do I go now?

Charlie： Where do you go next? Where do you go next?

Stubbings： Massive problem.

Charlie： And — and it's also — And it's even a sport where two of the top five performers in the world are British

Stubbings： and gold multi gold medal winners. Um no, that's eventing. This is Ben Mara and Scott Brash. um who who won the team gold in both the last two Olympics I think but someone will pick me up on that no doubt. Um so anyway he — McCort has hired the production company who did drive survive and he wants them to do a similar kind of series on show jumping. This could be a winner. This could be a winner because showj jumping rather like Formula 1 is one of those sports that lends itself quite naturally to this type of things. It's a — it's a circuit where the same people are going from place to place, right? And they're quite glamorous places for the most part. Um, but it did set me thinking. I think so. The one thing that show jumping could do with which would really change it. I don't know if you've seen, but one of the — I think one of the five biggest TV hits of the last two years in the Anglosphere has has been Rivals.

Jilly Cooper 的《Riders》：一部被遗忘的文化资产

Charlie： I knew you were going there.

Stubbings： Now, what was her first — what was Jilly Cooper's first book?

Charlie： Oh, I don't know. Charlie

Stubbings： Riders. Riders.

Charlie： It was called Riders and it was um published in 1985.

Stubbings： Is one of the fan?

Charlie： Yeah, indeed. It was published in — Are You Are You Are You Are You talking about yourself or me?

Stubbings： You

Charlie： I mean, no, I look this up. I don't know my head. Um it was published in 19.

Stubbings： It was not true.

Charlie： It was published in 1985 and actually related the story of the runup to the 1984 Olympics,

Stubbings： right? And it was the rivalry between two characters. One of them a gypsy from the wrong side of the tracks called Jake Lavell. the other one an aristocr black and that book which sold millions of copies. Now, given the — the success of rivals on TV, if I was had anything to do with show jumping, I'd be saying, "Right, make sure that the next Jilly Cooper that they turn into a big international TV classic is riders because that will really peak people's interest in all of the riders private lives, which can be quite interesting, let's just say. So, watch this space." Um, show jumping. I think would get Jill Cooper into

结尾：一次可能的现场直播计划

Charlie： I just also I just wonder Charlie I just wonder um the um since it's going to be at the Royal Hospital in 10 days times two weeks.

Stubbings： Should we go?

Charlie： Well, I was thinking we might do a sort of breakdown Substack um event and combine two things that have caught our attention in the last few months. Think about where it is. What could we do as a leadup to attending the show jumping? We could do the Ben Stokes pub crawl.

Stubbings： Oh,

Charlie： which finishes at the show dropping. Or maybe even the other way round. We could do it in reverse.

Stubbings： Other way around because I don't think we could risk turning up at this lovely event going full Ben Ducket or or Harry Brookke. Hello. Can we get it? Can I see your tickets? Uh, smash.

Charlie： Very good. Thanks a lot, Charlie.

Stubbings： I do like that. That's a very good idea. Um, brilliant. Thanks, Charlie.

item.description:
## 一、麦卡伦下课背后的"责任逃逸"结构学
大多数评论都在讨论麦卡伦的战绩，但真正值得注意的是访谈中两个人的语言结构对比：麦卡伦用"四年周期"来定义自己的失败，而 Richard Gould 用"过去六到九个月的几个结果"来描述同一件事。**时间尺度的选择本身就是一种权力表达**——运动员被训练在长周期里承担责任，而管理者习惯于把时间窗口切得越短越好，因为窗口越短，可归因的责任就越小。这不是英格兰板球独有的病，而是所有"运动员出身 vs. 行政出身"的组织里反复出现的裂缝。
一个出人意料的观察：**麦卡伦之所以能坦然承认失败，恰恰是因为他曾经是顶级球员**。视频里那句"能爬到运动巅峰的人往往对自己诚实，否则爬不上去"是全场最深的一句话。也就是说，Gould 的推卸并非人格缺陷，而是**他所处的选拔机制从未奖励过诚实**——行政官僚是被"避免出错"筛选出来的，而运动员是被"直面失败"筛选出来的。两种物种，无法通婚。
## 二、FA 与 FIFA：把"投票权数学"错当成"影响力物理"
节目里最反常识的一段，是 Stubbings 指出：**如果你根本拿不到男足世界杯、女足世界杯又

item.title: [投资TALK君] 芯片存储大屠杀，跑路还是抄底？财报大杂烩
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、把「割肉美光」放进整段独白里，你会发现一个被忽略的元交易模式

投资TALK君今天最真诚的一段，不是关于芯片周期，而是他反复回到同一个动作："我把ISRG清了，我把AMD换了Intel，我把Oracle换Microsoft的仓要考虑，我把美光加了但知道错了不加了"。

如果把这些换仓动作抽出来做时间序列，你会发现一个惊人的、他自己都没明说的规律：他不是在"买卖股票"，而是在不断替自己的"认知敞口"做再平衡。基本面变差→清仓ISRG（不是止损，是"取消订阅"这家公司）；对Oracle判断更清晰→用Microsoft换Oracle（不是加仓，是"迁移信念"）。

这个模式的反常识之处在于——大多数散户把仓位当作对股票的承诺，而他把仓位当作对自己判断的租约。租约到期（判断变了）就退租，不管有没有亏损。这解释了为什么他能反复强调"割肉不重要，认知才重要"——因为在他的操作系统里，割肉根本不是损失事件，而是信息更新事件。

二、他讲「因子失效」时暴露了一个跨领域的深层规律：任何被命名的东西都会失效

他随口提到的那家量化基金研究——"当越来越多人使用某个因子，因子本身就失去了预测力，退化为纯粹的风险衡量工具"——这句话如果你只把它当量化知识听，你就错过了它最锋利的地方。

这是Goodhart定律在金融市场的完整形态："当一个指标成为目标，它就不再是好的指标。" 但更惊人的跨领域连接是——这条规律在语言学里叫"符号的能指化"，在传播学里叫"meme的稀释"，在人类学里叫"仪式的空洞化"。

举一个你绝对没想到的连接：为什么"AI Agent"这个词最近突然让所有人反感？因为它正在经历和"动量因子"一模一样的死亡过程——从一个有预测力的概念（能识别真正的技术突破），退化为一个人人都能贴的标签（失去了区分能力）。Coding会被商品化，"AI Agent"这个词本身也会被商品化。TALK君今天反复讲coding会commoditize，但他没意识到自己讲的这套框架同样适用于所有热词，包括他自己正在使用的所有KOL话术。

三、最出人意料的观察：他今天讲得最好的是Uber，但他把Uber放在了错的框架里

他讲Uber用AI提升operating leverage，讲Working Pod，讲Forward Deployment Engineer——这些他都零散讲了，但他自己都没意识到这三件事拼在一起是什么。

它们是同一个东西的三个面：AI落地不是"技术问题"，是"组织形态问题"。 Uber的Working Pod、软件公司的FDE、Anthropic的Sonnet被Codex追赶——这三件事共同指向一个被市场严重误读的真相：AI这一轮真正的护城河不在模型，也不在算力，而在"AI-native的组织结构"。

这里有一个极其反常识的推论：如果这个假设成立，那么未来最值钱的不是模型公司，也不是Hyperscaler，而是那些能把AI"嵌入进别人组织"的中间层公司——Palantir的FDE模式、Accenture的咨询壳、甚至Uber自己内部孵化的"AI转型方法论"，都可能成为下一轮的稀缺资产。

跨领域连接：这和19世纪末电气化的历史惊人一致——真正赚钱的不是发电机制造商（西门子、通用电气最终利润率有限），而是教工厂如何用电重新设计流水线的咨询公司和电气工程师群体（Frederick Taylor的科学管理运动的经济基础就是这个）。TALK君今天嘴里的"FDE"，本质上就是AI时代的Frederick Taylor。

所以今天这场直播里最被低估的一句话不是"美光是不是周期股"，而是他一句带过的"Uber可能是第一家真正看到operating leverage的公司"——这句话如果是真的，那它对整个二级市场估值框架的冲击，比任何一次芯片下跌都要深远。因为它意味着"AI增效"从叙事变成了财报里可量化的margin expansion，而这才是市场真正能定价的东西。

Today, I think it's more of an interaction process. But I've prepared a lot of content. I spent at least 4-5 hours preparing for this. Because I talked about the financial reports of GE, ISRG, UNH, and the financial reports of Nafi. The financial reports of the four companies and the current market. So I spent a lot of time preparing.

If you come in, remember to like it first. "Nai Fei blew up." "What is the reason for the Nai Fei clean-up?" I will talk about it later. I will talk about why I clean-up. I rarely buy a product after a while and then choose to clean it up. There are many reasons. I will talk about it later.

大盘回顾：Intel、GE 拖累与板块轮动

Okay, let's go over the market first. Why is there an old message in my email? I have to delete it first.

Okay, let's go over the market first. First of all, today's market It's very hard for some people. But it's not easy for me either. I dropped by 2 points. The main drop was from Intel and GE. Both big companies are doing well. But because Intel and GE have a large share, the overall return is still 2.7%.

I saw someone on the Internet saying how much the return is from the highest point. I think it was May. I think YTD was around 40% at that time. Now it's around 27% or 28%. And it's already in the situation where I've picked up the warehouse.

So, the management is doing very well. So today, the market has a relatively small return on the standard share. 0.5% and then the return on the NAZ is relatively large, 1.5%. The Dow Jones is almost no return. But if you look at the bottom, if you look at the previous seven major tech stocks, there were some returns today, 1.3%.

I want to talk about the equalization index. Today, it has risen by 1%. And you can see that most of the blocks in the first column are rising today. Medical block 2.2, insurance block 1.5, public business 0.6, risk 2.3, and emergency medical consumption 2.8. Some blocks with relatively strong defense properties are doing very well today. The chip block continues to rebound by 4.5%.

If we pull to the far right, QDD is the whole of July to the present. Quarter to day, which is 15 days. 10 trading days in 15 days. Almost 10 trading days, 11 trading days. The stock market has risen by 0.5%, only dropped by 1.3%, and the Dow Jones also rose by 0.4%. During this period, we are actually following the rise of the original price. Because of the war, there seems to be some upgrades or repeats. But you can see that the rise and repeat of the war actually have little impact on the market. If it really has an impact like March, the stock market will fall. In March, it fell by hundreds of millions. QDD should have fallen, but it didn't.

You can see that the logic of the war trade in the market is not as clear as it was in March. I mentioned that the price of oil prices was very similar to the price of oil prices in March. Then in this period, IGV is relatively good. 3.4%, and VIX rose 1.6%. VIX is still good in the past 10 exchanges. So IGV is relatively strong, and Bitcoin is also relatively strong, at 9.4%.

Then in... Of course, I think this is just a joke. On July 1st, when I was in the second half of the year, I talked about software blocks. Of course, I talked about Bitcoin, which is the 0.9% of the gold. You can take a look at Bitcoin and gold. Because these two tags were in the first half of the year. No one was using them. And basically, everyone was leaving. There must have been no participation in the market.

核心心法：不给别人抬轿子，宁可错过不要 FOMO

You don't have to worry about it. You don't have to give others a ride. It's just a matter of price. A viewer said, "I said before, don't give others a ride." "This sentence is still in the air." What this sentence means is that the market is very crowded. We know that the market is very crowded. You don't have to be the flow of others. Although you can get in in a short time, I can still get in this car. But if you have the mindset of getting in the car, and you don't know how much you can get in, and you can't help but get in, you must avoid this mindset.

I'll add one more thing to the previous sentence. Don't give others a license. I'd rather miss it than to go to FOMO. If we miss it, we won't make much money. I've always said that the money we can make and the money we can keep is within our knowledge. It must be within our knowledge. The money we make outside our knowledge will eventually go back. You won't lose it today, you won't lose it tomorrow, you will lose it the third time.

Because After we all walked here, the first time and the second time, you think you have gained from your strength. But the third time, you still think you have gained from your strength. And then the fourth time, you will lose from your strength. Because the first three times, you actually gained from your strength. Only after we have experienced this, we will know and we will be able to avoid it. Many people may not be able to avoid this mindset of being a FOMO after experiencing it once.

I wrote a sentence in XShower this morning. I said, in the less than seven months, we have experienced foreign uh uh

左侧交易：像接飞刀，但接住的是估值

If I simply start from a basic point of view, I think the valuation is reasonable, so I buy it. But I often like to buy on the left side, which is a bit like a flying knife. When it falls, I think it's okay on the basic side, and it's not even bad. So I'll buy it. Usually, it fell by 10%, 20%, or 15% that day. From the perspective of these people in the technical side, it's a flying knife. Actually, it's not very good. From a technical point of view, it's not very good. Because you don't know where it can fall and only then can it fall straight.

But from a relatively higher level, I think it's reasonable from a stubborn point of view. Maybe many times, the so-called "flying knife" is that you can't find a... I keep saying "correct, correct". The so-called "flying knife" is that you can't find a basic emotional and technical proof. Maybe it's not that good in terms of probability. I don't like this kind of situation. In this case, I'm forced to add from the right side.

The right side is usually... For example, you buy a low position and then it immediately rebounded. You don't dare to chase it or you don't want to chase it. You can only chase it when the right side is in a downturn. But the position you're chasing or adding to it is not as low as the point you originally built. So there's good and bad. There's nothing right or wrong.

But when we go back to the market, I want to say that as long as we buy in, if we are very confident about this benchmark, we know why we buy in, you can hold it for a long time. You won't let the market's noise and voices make you sell out early. This is what we do within our knowledge, and then we start making money. Even losing money within our knowledge is the same. Because many times, what we have made mistakes is losing money within our knowledge. But at least this is within our recognition. This is the money we should lose and the money we should earn. As long as the money we earn outside our recognition, you will definitely lose it. This is the same reason.

So, because I saw it in front of me, I saw it, I saw a message that he happened to be on that day. When Apple fell to 275, he chased the US light of 1230. I went to scan the US light 1230, the deadline of the sun K line. ...（价格重复片段）

And is affected by panic and is blocked by other labels. This is a very standard traceable mistake. This must be avoided. After making a mistake, it must be avoided in the future.

芯片板块估值与参与率

Back to the market, look at QDD. In the past 11 trading days, the rate of chip drop was very high, 17.2%. 10 trading days, the rate of chip drop was 17.2%. In fact, it was very high in the past week or so. The value of screws rose by 2.9%, growth dropped by 2.4%. The main reason for growth is the drop. In fact, because there are many growth stocks related to chips. Big technology rebounded by 5.8%, and the share price rose by 1.1%.

The whole market, including the second row of the Dow Group, most of the YTP, QTD, Port Today, are rising. In addition to the decline in technology, defense, and chip, most of the speed is also rising. So the entire market sentiment, including... Under this circumstance, it's meaningless to say that I'm going to see the participation rate, because the participation rate is still high. Because most of the rules are rising.

So only during this period, when there are a lot of hardware and chip, and the participation rate is very high, will you feel very uncomfortable. Even if you don't only have a very high participation rate, but you have a lot of leverage in it, and you persist for these few days, you will feel very, very uncomfortable. But I think it's a bit late to say this now. But I started to remind myself since May. Because my first time to point out the winner of Intel was in May. I should have been in May, early May, mid-May. And then the second time was in June. I moved to point out the winner twice. I started to remind myself slowly.

To start doing some mobile support. It's passive defense. We don't have to guess where he can get his emotions. But you have to do this defense well. You don't go to active support, you have to do a passive one. You don't go to active support, you have to do a passive support. Including the day I was on the live broadcast on July 1st, I said, "Don't regret it until the end of the world." Why didn't you do support at the time? I said, "Why didn't you do a mobile support at the time?"

2021 沙尘暴的教训：没有 EPS 的标的最先被杀

Anyway, these things may sound like - I don't know when this wave will end, but this wave of decline can be absorbed from each wave of adjustment, decline and rise, in the future when investing and trading. If it can be an experience and reference. Because I myself, at least in recent years, the most profound feeling is the sandstorm in 2021. The sandstorm in November 2021. I am very clear that when these funds start to kill these labels, you don't have to look at the basics, you don't have to look at anything. When it kills, it will never give you any experience.

The difference between having the basics and not having the basics is that the so-called basic side is supported by the financial report, supported by the valuation, supported by the EPS. Supported by valuation must be... EPS support. Without EPS, what are you talking about? Price to sales ratio. Don't think about these labels. When the market kills these emotions, it will definitely need to realize it. Because without EPS support, you don't have a hair in the market. When you don't have this hair, it's harder to find a balance or the rate of fall or adjustment will be higher.

You can say that you are very optimistic about the 5-10 year mark, and you can say that you are optimistic about the 5-10 year mark. It's okay. I believe that any mark at one point in the next 5-10 years will fall by 30% to 50%, which is very reasonable. The probability is not low. Not even any mark. Even if Apple falls by 30%, in the future, at some point, I think it's still more likely to happen in the next 5-10 years. So if you can really put your time line that long, then it doesn't matter.

I think this is a very good feedback in this period. If there is a basic aspect, the market will find consensus and then go back. Actually, let's refer to Invitas. I think Invitas has been very strong since the rise and fall of this wave since 2023. Historically, it is very difficult to find any benchmark to make a comparison with Invitas. Including Invitas, since it rose all the way, there have been people since 2024, including in 2023, who have been comparing Invitas with Cisco. They have been comparing all the way. To this day, the picture of Cisco has not been fully changed. Why? Because the increase in profit of Invitas, the increase in EPS, is very fast. It can keep up with its price.

When a stock is mature, let's say it's mature, if it doesn't have any growth in PE, let's say it's 25 or 20 times the previous one, its annual price can double. It means its profit will double. How many companies can double their profit per year? Right? Let's think about it this way. Because you can't expect the company to expand its PE. So it's rare for a company to double its profit. It's even more unlikely for a mature company to double.

So now we see a lot of labels that can grow twice, three, four, five times a year. In the future, it can make it In the short term, within six months, the price will not have a big rebound. The profit will follow. So, whether it's hardware or storage, I think the logic is the same. As long as the profit follows, the market can find the balance point. I often tell you, it won't appear from where to where. It won't. Because it has profit, it has a stubborn fur. The market can find this fur. Including the story line of AI. Before there is a political crisis, I don't think the market will be the same as it is now.

2022 芯片周期回忆：AMD 财报再好也扛不住 Sell the News

2022 is a very standard period for chips. I remember very clearly that at the beginning of 2022, it was an annual profit of AMD. The financial statements of the first and second seasons were very good. The financial statements of the fourth season of 2021 and the first season of 2022 were very good. However, no matter how good the financial statements are, the stock price will fall after the financial statements are made. This is what we call "Sell the News". Including the judgment of Suma over the entire market, he was wrong to look back. He thought that the output of the PC was worse than his expectation. I remember very clearly. I think I had Intel at that time, not AMD. So I remember very clearly. The judgment of Zhenglin and Su Man was wrong. His judgment of the industry was wrong. It was a very standard cycle of the industry. Then in November 2021, the cycle of the industry slowly resumed. Asmai and TSMC were all in the same cycle. Asmai was affected by the relatively upper-level TSMC, while the lower-level chips companies in the bottom-line of the application, such as Inveda and AMD, were affected by the drop.

美光的长单机制：把价格与利润率锁进 EPS

  • I think we shall see. People who work in the market may think that the price of the village is rising to the top. And then many people think that there is still room for improvement. But I think what should be avoided is the price of the summer. We suspect that you originally thought these things. We should think about it from the perspective of whether my idea was completely wrong or whether it was completely overrated by others.

Before two or three weeks, people kept asking me if I thought Meiguang was a conglomerate. But in fact, after the Meiguang financial report was published, everyone thought that the reason why we discussed conglomerates was because everyone thought that the current valuation was 14 or 13 times, and that my valuation could go up to 20 or 25 times. This is the problem that was discussed a month ago. After the financial report, I recorded a video on the day of the special reporting.

After listening to the reporting, I said that in this reporting meeting, because they and I remember 16 companies signed the long-term order. This long-term order actually includes the long-term order. There is a requirement for the margin of the upper and lower lines. This upper line and lower line are exactly what they are afraid of. The whole storage of the future is a cycle. So he used this long-term order to lock the future amount. Not only does it lock the amount, but also the future interest rate. That's why it's a cycle. It's not about the amount, but the margin. It's about the price. So, it not only locks the amount, but also the revenue and interest rate. The fixed revenue and interest rate are locked in a bottom line. The bottom line is the profit and the EPS.

So, in fact, this financial report instead turned US light into a relatively... It should be said that it is a kind of a...

基本面 vs 价格：必须拆开来干

We need to make sure 基本面的问题,然后要把市场的情绪面和价格要拆开去干。 如果能够分开这两个的话,而不是在上涨的时候去说自己基本面非常的好, 然后美光是不是一家周期股,然后在下跌的时候突然又怀疑原来自己这个想法, 那说明你所有的自己对于看待这家公司的想法全部是建立在价格上面的。

After we separate these two things, we can only know when the price drops. The market is now dead. When the price rises, we know that the market is absolutely unreasonable. Basically, there is no very big change. In the case, the price has a very big rise. That's the price of emotions. And when the price of emotions is at this price, we should take profit. Then we should know that the market has passed. This is the angle we should set. Instead of judging from the price and then leading to the basic side.

I talked a lot at the beginning because I know many people have some questions about storage, about the US light, and about the Sandisk. I'll talk about what I've seen. If there are any questions, I can talk about it later.

Kimi K2 与模型价格战：Coding 商品化正在发生

There's a lot of news these days. Yesterday, there was the model of KMI K3. There was GLM a while ago. We talked for two or three weeks. Now, there's the model of KMI K3. I think it's the most expensive one among the Chinese vision glasses. I think the average price is close to the price of Sonnet, the price of Anthropic Sonnet. And then... The price of Opus 4.8 is around 60%. So the price is not very cheap. Compared to the more advanced models of the current B-Dragon model, it's not that cheap. Maybe it's even more expensive than the Meta model.

The Meta model, Muse Spark, I think the price of the API is actually the top model. You can see that the whole model, especially in coding, is getting more and more competitive. I said three weeks ago that coding will become a commodity. Because everyone sees that the market is very big. Seeing that you, Anthropocene, make so much money, you have to go in and grab the market, grab the cake. I think the scene we are seeing now, the competition pattern and the situation, are actually developing in this direction.

SOX 估值回到 21.9x：合理不等于不跌

I know you're very concerned about the value of the chip version. Please update me. The value of the entire SOX today is 21.94 times the value of the previous version. The highest value was 29.6 times the previous version. I didn't go over 30 times. When it was 29.6 times, 30 times, 29, 28, I talked about the value of the value of the SOX repeatedly. If I go over 30 times, I will actively support it. If it's 29 times, I will support it. This is a passive support.

Now we are back to 21.9. I talked about this before. Now we are back to 23.22. We are back to a neutral position. It's reasonable. I want to make a distinction. Someone said in the video yesterday, "Aren't you saying that it's reasonably stable? Why is it still falling these days?" We need to distinguish this. It's not reasonable to say that it's not falling. Why can't it fall if it's reasonably stable? It's reasonable to say that if you want to build a warehouse at this position, you can build a warehouse. because the valuation is reasonable now. Or if you are willing to wait, and you think it can be valuation to a relatively low position, then you can wait.

Valuability does not mean it will not fall. Another point is that valuation, whether it is high, reasonable or low, is a vague correctness. It does not mean that the next week you will have a rise or fall. I remember in the video, when I was live streaming in my app, I talked to me about when the market is killing these leverage, it is unreasonable. In a short period of time, Sometimes, it will overreact. It won't have a... Even if you look at the key points, I don't think it's meaningful.

So, in order to see the key points, who can support the key points? Right? I am more inclined to my judgment that the main logic is that it is stubborn to a reasonable position. Naturally, there will be these long-range funds to take over. I think these bodies will be more powerful than the short-term funds of the quantitative fund. So I think when the small-scale companies are really going to the top, they won't give you any reason. So going to the top is an irrational behavior. So going to the top is very cute. It can fall very quickly in a very short time. It falls until you are completely out of faith.

If you look at its operating profit increase, it is 23% better than expected. It is also a very large ratio of this ratio. Especially for medical companies, it is a very large ratio. But there is one thing we need to pay attention to. Why can this quarter exceed the market expectations? This is because, this is something that only people in insurance companies can know. Of course, if you know more about this company, you should know what I'm talking about. It's because it has a prior year development.

What does that mean? You can understand it as an insurance company, including banks, they all have a prepaid fund. Prepaid funds are for future losses. You can understand that last quarter, he was too prepared. In other words, the loss he expected last quarter was not that much. So, he released the loss he expected last quarter. This is called prior. It's about the previous correction. The previous part of the loss, the part of the loss expected to be lost, after correction, it all becomes profit.

It's the same as the bank releasing reserves. The reserve and the prepayment is the same in the bank releasing bad debt. Because your prepayment and your reserve is for the future bad debt and loss. The bank is the same. For the future compensation, because every Because every piece of the bank will have a future loss rate or a calculation price. Calculation loss is there. It means that the calculation loss was originally wrong. But actually, the calculation loss, the calculated loss, the calculated expected loss, has a very big factor of human nature in it. It can be fixed by human nature. Human nature can be judged. I'm judging. Of course, the model gives the answer. After giving the answer, it always has a factor of human nature.

So I don't want to say that they are manipulating this financial report, but I want to say that the insurance company wants to adjust the financial report for one or two seasons, and it is completely capable of adjusting it. It only needs to adjust this prior year development. What I mentioned, If you know this industry, or if you have experience in insurance companies, you will know that there is a lever in insurance companies to pull. This may also represent why management in the last quarter was so confident that they said they would raise the margin this quarter. So there may be a part of the reason behind it.

So, the revenue can exceed 1%, only exceed 1%, but the profit can exceed 20%. Something is... I talked about this magnitude. You can't follow up with 10 headlines.

The so-called root of 10 headlines is that you need to know The simplest way is, for example, if I ask you today, how much profit margin does the company you buy have? How much is its revenue? If you don't know how much its profit margin is, how much is its profit margin, and how much is its PE, then you don't know. So it means that you don't know about this company at all. It's just because other places may think that you can buy this company. These are the most basic knowledge, including what the management said in the last quarter's financial report. Basically, I have all the companies, and I must see every quarter's financial report. I know what they talked about last season and what they will do next season. You must know these things. If you don't know, it means you don't have the ability to manage so many topics. just add 5 or 6 tags.

Once you have the core tags, these 5 or 6 tags won't take up too much space. For example, the core tag takes up 70%. You have 5 tags, so each tag takes up 6%. You can add 10% to the tags you like. 10% for a tag is actually not small enough for the overall investment group. Then on this basis, if QQQ guarantees beta, I'm just giving an example. I'm not saying this group is suitable for everyone, but I'm just giving an example. 40% of QQQ, 40% of SPY, 40% of QQQ, or 30% of SPY, 40% of QQQ, 70% of the warehouse will be gone. I'm only talking about the stock warehouse. If there is a debt-based requirement, that's your personal problem. You can arrange it elsewhere. I don't care about this. I don't care about asset allocation. I only care about the stock market. 40 SPY, 30 QQQ, and the remaining 30 are distributed to 6 units. One unit is a 5% share. Right? 6, 5, 6, 30. This 5% share is actually very good. If you can pick the 5 units better, if the 5 units can win the standard, then these 5 units will help you get Alpha. Then QQQ can help you get Beta.

With such a share, I believe even if you have empty 5 units, or empty 4 units, your overall investment group, you won't run out of 500-500. Unless your 5-5 stocks are all running some concept stocks or some NIM, then I can't do anything. But even if you buy NIM, your 30% of this range will not affect your remaining 70% too much. You may mark 10% at most, you do not do well 6%, 5%, at least you won't run too bad. I think this is the most important thing Basically, if you have a higher risk profile like me, you don't need to have a core 3-way, you can be a core. But if you are a core, you need to have time to follow up. I mentioned this before, for me, I think there are too many advantages over the 10, 8, and 19. Although most cores are already mastered by me, But I think there are still a lot of them. Because I think there are a lot of them. Maybe it's a problem with the amount of work in the future, or the number of meetings. Maybe it's too scattered. That's also a problem.

I strongly recommend that you take a look at the core satellite. Because after you master this thing, you can do whatever you want with the remaining 30% of what you have. Even if you mess it up, you won't have any anxiety like you did recently.

Okay, QQQ. The SOX fell 70 days ago. It's now falling again after the talk. QQQ just fell over 700. Okay, okay. That's it for today. If necessary, I'll make a video tomorrow. If the market falls more tomorrow, that's it for today. See you tomorrow. Bye.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、把「割肉美光」放进整段独白里，你会发现一个被忽略的**元交易模式**
投资TALK君今天最真诚的一段，不是关于芯片周期，而是他反复回到同一个动作：**"我把ISRG清了，我把AMD换了Intel，我把Oracle换Microsoft的仓要考虑，我把美光加了但知道错了不加了"**。
如果把这些换仓动作抽出来做时间序列，你会发现一个**惊人的、他自己都没明说的规律**：他不是在"买卖股票"，而是在**不断替自己的"认知敞口"做再平衡**。基本面变差→清仓ISRG（不是止损，是"取消订阅"这家公司）；对Oracle判断更清晰→用Microsoft换Oracle（不是加仓，是"迁移信念"）。
这个模式的反常识之处在于——**大多数散户把仓位当作对股票的承诺，而他把仓位当作对自己判断的租约**。租约到期（判断变了）就退租，不管有没有亏损。这解释了为什么他能反复强调"割肉不重要，认知才重要"——因为在他的操作系统里，割肉根本不是损失事件，而是**信息更新事件**。
## 二、他讲「因子失效」时暴露了一个跨领域的深层规律：**任何被命名的东西都会失效**
他随口提到的那家量化基金

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Putin's Playbook – Sarah Paine
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、地缘政治的"负外部性引擎"：为什么强者身边总是废墟

表面上看，Paine 描述的是俄罗斯的"顺序吞并"战略——一次消化一个邻居，制造缓冲区，等待时机。但真正令人震撼的洞察藏在她随口提及的一句话里："环顾俄罗斯和中国，它们被地球上一些最功能失调的地方所包围。是运气不好，还是它们本身就是共谋？"

这里揭示了一个反直觉的系统性真相：大陆型霸权不是"恰好"生活在废墟中，废墟是它生产出来的副产品。这就像生态学里的"顶级掠食者悖论"——为了维持自身的生态位，它必须持续制造周边的"生态贫瘠"。健康的邻居是威胁，只有失败国家才是安全的缓冲。

这带来一个跨领域的意外类比：这与平台经济中的"焦土策略"几乎同构——某些超级平台通过持续压低生态位内其他玩家的生存能力（而非直接消灭）来维持不可替代性。俄罗斯对乌克兰做的事、腾讯早年对独立开发者做的事、Meta 对新兴社交产品做的事——在结构上是同一种"生态位维稳"逻辑。

二、陀思妥耶夫斯基的"东方主义"：文学作为帝国潜意识的泄露

Paine 引用陀思妥耶夫斯基那段"在欧洲我们是奴才，在亚洲我们是主人"的日记，是整段演讲最耐人寻味的一击。

这里有一个大多数俄罗斯文学爱好者从未察觉的黑暗结构：《罪与罚》、《卡拉马佐夫兄弟》里那种对救赎、屈辱、被拯救的痴迷，其实是一种"帝国自卑—帝国补偿"的精神机制。当一个文明在西方面前感到二等，它就必须在东方创造出属于自己的"更低等的他者"来维持心理平衡。

跨领域连接来了：这与心理学中的"踢猫效应"（displaced aggression）在民族尺度上的呈现完全一致。陀思妥耶夫斯基不是在写游记，他是在为整个俄罗斯民族的心理位移提供哲学许可证。而更令人不安的是——Klyuchevsky 那句"俄罗斯是一个正在殖民自身的国家"，说明这种位移最终会转向内部：当外部扩张受阻，同样的暴力逻辑会向国内的社会阶层实施。布尔什维克对整个贵族阶级的清洗，本质上是对外扩张受挫后的"殖民能量"的内收。

三、一个惊人的观察：连续型帝国没有"止损函数"

Paine 提出了一个几乎无人注意的关键机制缺陷——"没有辞职委员会"（no council on when to quit）。这句轻描淡写的话是整个演讲的隐藏核弹。

海洋型帝国（英国、荷兰、美国）有天然的止损机制：股东、议会、财政周期、商船公司的资产负债表。赔钱的殖民地会被"卖掉"。但大陆型帝国的扩张成本是隐藏的、递延的、由沉默的农民承担的，因此它从未收到"停止扩张"的价格信号，直到系统性崩溃。

这里有一个让人后背发凉的跨领域映射：这与现代 AI 系统中的"奖励黑客"（reward hacking）在数学上同构。一个没有对齐机制的智能体，会不断优化其单一指标（领土/参数量/用户数），直到系统因为忽略了未被计价的外部成本而突然崩溃。普京不是一个失败的战略家，他是一个"对齐失败"的智能体——他的效用函数里没有"何时停止"这一项。

而更深的洞察是：中国和俄罗斯历史上反复的王朝崩溃循环，可能不是"腐败"或"外敌"造成的，而是这个缺失的止损函数在起作用。它们不是被打败的，它们是被自己缺失的反馈回路噎死的。这也解释了为什么 Paine 说 "there are no enduring alliances in this world"——因为任何理性的邻居最终都会意识到：与一个没有停止函数的实体结盟，等于把自己的存活概率外包给对方的内部崩溃时刻。

📝 转录文本

大陆型安全范式的游戏规则

In this world, there are certain rules for playing this game.

Rule number one: no two-front wars. I've shown you all the neighbors. If they gang up on you, it will be game over — in a very bad way — for you.

Secondly: no great power neighbors. Why? Because today's friend can be tomorrow's foe. And that spells trouble.

So, what do you do? You take on your neighbors sequentially, like one at a time. You set them up to fail, you destabilize the rising, you adjust the failing, and you set up buffer zones in between, and you await the opportune moment to pounce and absorb.

This is Vladimir Putin's game.

"豺狼国家"策略

Better yet, you get the neighbors to do the work for you. How? You sow their mutual resentments, you get them to fight each other, you deluge them with fake news — so that Russia can play the role of the jackal state: once the neighbors have sufficiently weakened themselves and weakened each other, Russia can move on in and steal a kill made by others.

这一范式的内在缺陷

This theory of security has certain problems.

One, you're surrounding yourself by failing states. You can look at Russia and China, and they're surrounded by some of the most dysfunctional places on the planet, and you can go, "Gee whiz, are they unlucky? Are they complicit?"

And moreover, there are no enduring alliances in this world, because the neighbors figure it out. The hegemonic power is just a long-term problem.

And there's also no council on when to quit. Like, how much territory do you take before you choke on it? And it turns out that there's been a tendency over the course of China's and Russia's long histories to overextend. And this helps account for the implosions of various empires and dynasties.

但请勿低估其历史有效性

Now, before you dismiss this security paradigm, understand: it was very effective. Certainly prior to the Industrial Revolution, no one cared in this world about collateral damage or killing a bunch of innocent people. This paradigm is all about killing people, breaking things, to take territory.

And you've seen it operating in real time in your own lives — in Syria, Ukraine, and other places. This is why those ancient ruins, the ones that still remain, are ruinous — because this sort of warfare is ruinous.

三段俄国精英的自白

All right. Here is one of the preeminent historians of the late Tsarist period, Vasily Klyuchevsky, who's describing his country. He said:

"The history of Russia is a history of a country in process of colonizing itself. Her area of colonization grows in tandem with her national territory — at times shrinking, at times expanding — age-old movement. Therefore, the periods in our history are the stages which our people have gone through in the occupation and development of the land acquired by them."

There is absolutely no mention of the people who actually live on this land. It's like irrelevant from his point of view. But this is the way Russians have long looked at things: that if you have small neighbors, someone big may take them over. And even worse, if the weak become strong in their own right, it's dangerous. So you want to keep moving outwards and expanding.

And here, a generation earlier, you have Fyodor Dostoevsky — the novelist, the author of Crime and Punishment and other light reads. Here he is confiding in his diary:

"In Europe we are hangers-on and slaves, whereas we shall go to Asia as masters. In Europe we are Asiatics, whereas in Asia we too are Europeans. Our civilizing mission in Asia will bribe our spirit and drive us thither."

So this idea of empire is very deeply rooted in Russian thinking.

And here is Count Sergei Witte. He's probably the most distinguished public servant in the late Tsarist period. He was a finance minister. And he's looking at the world in 1903 and he goes:

"Look, the problem for Russia is to obtain as large a share as possible of the outlived oriental states."

And he's thinking of the Ottoman Empire, but particularly of China. And he goes:

"Look, Russia historically, geographically has the undisputed right to the lion's share of the expected prey. And this elemental movement of Russia began a long time ago with Ivan the Terrible, aptly named. And the absorption by Russia of a considerable portion of the Chinese Empire is only a question of time — unless the Chinese get their act together."

So here you have a statesman at the highest levels of the Russian government who is looking at Russian national security — it's all about territorial expansion.

普京，以及大陆型战略的终极风险

Well, Putin also looks at the world this way, apparently.

The problem for the likes of Putin is: if a continental power botches strategy, its known world can vanish forever. And this is what happened to Imperial China, Imperial Russia, and many venerable civilizations.

I love this photograph of an aristocrat's whimsical palace that's long gone — as a result of one of the most horrific genocides of the 20th century, when the communists made good their promise to get rid of entire social classes. And I mean, get rid of — like, gone forever. With the Bolshevik Revolution and the ensuing Russian Civil War, followed by collectivization, and then the Great Purges that finished the job.

This is the continental world. It is a world without insurance policies.

Okay, now for the maritime world.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

表面上看，Paine 描述的是俄罗斯的"顺序吞并"战略——一次消化一个邻居，制造缓冲区，等待时机。但真正令人震撼的洞察藏在她随口提及的一句话里：**"环顾俄罗斯和中国，它们被地球上一些最功能失调的地方所包围。是运气不好，还是它们本身就是共谋？"**

item.title: [YC Root Access] Building the Agentic AI Platform for Hospitals
item.dc:creator: YC Root Access
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、真正的产品不是 AI，而是"让说'试试看'的成本趋近于零"

大多数人以为 Bunker Hill 卖的是医疗 AI，其实他们卖的是组织决策的边际成本。传统医院里，一次"要不要用某个 AI 工具"的决策，绑定着两年的采购、IT、合规、变革管理——所以每个"是"都必须是慎重的、经过任务小组反复审议的、押上职业信誉的"是"。这种高成本迫使决策者不得不用"重要性排序"来筛选想法，而重要性排序本身就是创新的杀手，因为真正颠覆性的想法在早期看起来总是不够重要。

Bunker Hill 真正做的事情，是把"说 Yes 的成本"从两年降到两周。一旦这个成本足够低，医院的决策模式就会从"审议模式"切换到"实验模式"。这不是一个 AI 故事，而是一个制度经济学故事——就像 AWS 之于软件创业，把"启动一个想法"从需要买服务器、租机房，压缩到刷信用卡的十分钟。医疗 AI 的"AWS 时刻"就是他们真正在下的赌注。

二、意外的观察：LLM 让"不酷的问题"变成了"酷问题"的通行证

这是整个访谈里最反直觉的一段。研究者过去只做"酷"的临床问题——心脏病预测、癌症筛查——因为每个模型都要从零训练，你必须选值得投入几年的问题。结果是：医院金字塔顶端的"启蒙问题"被过度研究，底层的行政、账单、转诊排队等"无聊问题"完全没人碰。

而 Mish 揭示了一个几乎所有 AI 创业者都没看透的悖论：恰恰是解决了那些"不酷"的底层问题（账单、转诊、Medicaid 资格审核），"酷"的问题才有可能被采纳。因为医院的采购、IT、变革管理带宽是共享的稀缺资源。你只有帮医院把底层的水管铺好，他们才有余力去关心顶层的诊断创新。

这里有一个跨领域连接可能会让你意外：这个模式和日本战后制造业的"改善（Kaizen）"哲学几乎完全同构。丰田不是靠一次革命性发明超越美国车企，而是靠把无数微小的、不酷的流程改进堆积起来。硅谷 AI 圈今天迷恋"基础模型的下一次飞跃"，但 Bunker Hill 正在证明：医疗 AI 的胜负手在"复利式的微小自动化"，不在"英雄式的诊断突破"。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：他们的组织结构就是他们的产品哲学的复印件

留意一个几乎被访谈者一带而过的细节——21 个人的公司，工程师只做平台层，前端部署团队是"聪明但非技术的"人。这不是效率选择，这是哲学一致性。

Bunker Hill 相信"知识—推理—行动"三个 primitives 可以吸收整个医疗行业的复杂性。如果他们真的相信这一点，那么他们自己的公司架构就必须证明这一点：如果连他们自己都需要工程师针对每个客户写代码，就等于承认自己的抽象是漏的。

这里藏着一个更深的洞察，跨到语言学里去：Chomsky 的生成语法认为，人类有限的语法规则可以生成无限的句子。Bunker Hill 在做的，本质上是为医疗流程发明一套"生成语法"——21 个人的团队之所以能服务数十家医院、几百个用例，就是因为他们相信自己找到了医疗操作的"深层结构"。工程师维护语法本身，前端部署团队负责用这套语法"造句"。

这也解释了为什么他们敢押注在"团队规模保持极小"这件事上——一个真正找到正确抽象的公司，扩张的应该是 token 消耗，而不是人头。这句话（Ankur 半开玩笑说出来的）其实是本次访谈里被严重低估的一句预言：它可能是下一波 AI 原生公司的组织学新范式——员工数与营收脱钩，token 消耗与营收挂钩。传统 SaaS 的"每美元 ARR 需要多少员工"这个基准，可能在 AI 原生公司里要被"每美元 ARR 需要多少 token"取代。

四、一个额外的隐藏结构：创始人的"火鸡骨"就是他自己的父亲

访谈里 Mish 用"火鸡骨病人"讲了 Bunker Hill 的完整价值链——病人为一个无关的原因来到急诊，CT 扫描顺便发现冠状动脉阻塞，AI 触发一整套后续照护。而故事的另一端，是 Mish 自己的父亲——他花了几年时间试图部署一个能预防心脏病的模型，几乎放弃，然后父亲在 Abu Dhabi 心脏病发作，医生做 CT 时正好发现了他模型能识别的那种斑块。

这不是巧合，这是创始人-市场契合度（founder-market fit）的极端形式：他要解决的问题在他放弃之前，先在他自己家里发作了。硅谷谈论"founder-market fit"通常是软性的经验相关性，但 Mish 的案例接近于存在意义上的绑定——他做这件事的意义结构里，藏着他不做这件事的代价。这种绑定极难在 pitch deck 上看出来，但它往往是长期抗周期的最深护城河。

📝 转录文本

开场与融资公布

[music]

Today we're really excited to have Mish Khandwala from Bunker Hill Health. Thanks so much for joining us. Thanks for having me, Ankur. Mish, you have a exciting announcement to share today. Why don't we kick things off with that? Yes, absolutely. Uh we are very, very excited to announce our Series B today. Uh we have raised $55 million thus far. Uh this round was led by Vinod Khosla at Khosla Ventures. And joining this round is obviously Y Combinator. Uh also Alfred Lin from Sequoia. Uh Octum Ventures, Felicis, and this all will go into building the Agentic AI platform for health systems. Um we think that this is the way how AI gets adopted in the future and I'm excited to share more about what we're up to.

Bunker Hill Health 在做什么：降低医疗行业的"迭代成本"

So, tell us a little bit about Bunker Hill Health. Um so, maybe to begin, what does Bunker Hill Health do today? Like, give us the full span of kind of what the company does.

Absolutely. We are really obsessed about lowering the cost of iteration in healthcare. I think healthcare has this reputation of it's hard to move the needle in or it takes a long time to get something done. We are really obsessed about changing that. How can we make the speed of iteration faster? How can we make the cost of iterations less?

So, what do I mean by that? Um I'll give you two sides. Let's take the side of an executive at a health system, at a hospital, where this executive is responsible for all AI initiatives. It's because the barriers of entry are very high. So again, from that viewpoint of that physician or that executive at the health system responsible for AI, I am like, there is no way I can incorporate all of these tools. So it feels almost like I see the potential, but I do not know of a good way to capture it.

guys have built integrations to all of these things.

Knowledge is how we obtain information in. So that's those are the connectors that are doing the read activity. So common sources would be the EHR, the electronic health record, um imaging archives, EKG archives, ERPs, things like that. And also the internet. You know, there are a lot of clinical guidelines on there. So that's the knowledge work.

That feeds into the reasoning brick, which is where the AI lives. And then AI gets you an answer. Which is great. But if we don't take an automated action on top of that, you just have a dashboard that allows you to admire the problem as opposed to actually [laughter] doing something around it.

And so, the re-output of the reasoning brick gets fitted fed into the action brick where we can notify patients. We have, you know, auto snail-mailed patients. Uh we have auto-texted SMS'd patients. Um we have obviously through the EHR AI voice calling to some more nascent uh level. We can message providers through the EHR through automatic fax.

Got on a CT scan of the chest where the heart was visible, where the arteries around the heart were visible. The patient felt through care gaps, literally needed a heart heart attack before they saw a cardiologist.

And so his proposal to our lab was, could you build an AI model that could comb through every scan that is being done at Stanford? Basically you screen every patient coming to Stanford Hospital and see if they're at high risk of heart disease and if so bring them to the cardiologist. And that problem got super exacerbated by LLMs. Because now many algorithms just became prompts.

Right.

now do with these much easier to program algorithms so they're just prompts, but it also I guess simplifies the deployment to some degree because well, you just need to pick some LLMs and then you can solve a huge number of problems as opposed to before you needed some like, you know, bespoke deep learning pipeline for each different use case and then for each of them figure out how they're going to deploy it through a single infrastructure and they might need very different amounts of compute and whatever. And so, if you look at like the Maslow's hierarchy, there are some problems at the base level that a health system faces at the care delivery system faces. And then there are some like at the very top that are enlightened use cases. And everyone was just going after the enlightened use case because why else where where else would researchers spend time on?

That's right.

But with LLMs, we now actually had a way to offer solutions for every problem, every type of problem within the Maslow's hierarchy, um such that a health system could say, "Hey, we want to invest in this type of platform. offering the very bottom of the pyramid here.
