If you give the kids the money more likely to graduate, more likely to, you know, to buy a home, start a business, et cetera.

So I think that we're, as I said in, in the oval, this is day one and I am fully committed to the next decade as is my partner in crime on this Michael and Susan Dell. I appreciate your guys's comments on on on the legacy of this it has been the most profound work and kind of honor of my life and standing in the oval office with my two sons who who are really my two co-founders on this. We made the sketch of this at our kitchen table in the fall of 2020. That's where the conversation started. Lincoln's been with me in every single meeting with every congressman, senator, president, former president, et cetera. The president shouted him out again when we were there. That journey as a father with my kids has been... like the payback has been really extraordinary

政府造出了好软件——Joe Gebbia 的功劳

Thing that's notable here and again like you got to call balls and strikes and give credit where credit is due joe jebbia joined this administration a lot of people in the tech industry are like oh you know oh you joined the trump administration whatever yes okay there's some criticism he's an incredible world-class designer I was talking to producer Nick, our producer here, also happens to show the same last name as me. He signed up for this, right? He's doing well, but his wife signed up for it. The software is fantastic.

Let's pause for a second. The American government has made exceptional software? and this all got done in trump's first 18 months immense credit for this this is like of all the you know challenges this presidency has had and ran war and other issues we made great software the american government because of joe jebbia makes kick-ass software like Like just also major shout out to him. And you know, he can be doing whatever he wants. He's, you know, he's done incredibly well as the co founder of Airbnb. And he's doing this, like that's a real patriotic thing to do. I'm just over the moon with this. I think it's fantastic.

The dream team. You had like Michael Dell, myself, Vlad Tenev, Joe Gabbia, the Treasury Secretary, Luke Pettit at the Treasury talking basically every day for the last year. And our objective was not we want to build the best thing that the government's ever launched. We wanted to build one of the best consumer products, period, that's ever been launched. And there's any Silicon Valley consumer company would be thrilled with the numbers that we're seeing, the ratings that we're seeing, the engagement that we're seeing.

So you know it's been fun doing it with that incredible team and you know everybody's in this for the right mission as well and so I agree with you jason it's rare that government recruits or embraces uh you know that that that mission and I think the tent's getting a heck of a lot bigger it is bipartisan it's bipartisan in support you know this is important what governor uh uh moore from maryland said yesterday he said republicans and democrats have been trying to do something that looks like this feels like this for 40 years This guy got it done. Give him credit. This is great for America, great for our kids.

And especially on the 250th anniversary, you know, you're standing in the Oval Office looking at the original Declaration of Independence and you realize what people laid down for this experiment. And then I read this chatter in my feeds about Mondami and others literally wanting to set this great experiment on fire. Yeah. Yeah. Right. Wanting to burn the place down because they they think they have a better formula. No. The answer is evolving and doubling down on the formula that has worked for 250 years. And rather than making all these kids socialists, we get them all into the game of capitalism. They become owners, owners and shareholders in America. So that's what we got accomplished. And I appreciate the chance to talk about it.

Listen, incredible, Brad. And, you know, just amazing to watch you do it. And if you if you if you really think about it, the tech industry, it needs to win and capitalist and creators and the makers, the people who build stuff, this is our chance to say, here's an example of something that helps the people at the bottom, right? And I talked a bunch about the minimum wage here, we got $7 minimum wage, like, we need to address that as well. These are the things that if we address what people are scared about what people who are who don't have what we all have here, if we have empathy for those people, and we actually care about them, when we give them a path to believe in the American dream, they will take that path. We have to give them a better path than the socialist lunatics. And this is so much of a better path. Let's do it again. Let's do another one of these things. Let's keep growing this space. Spirit of getting everybody in the country to have equity in these great companies. That's why the entire world is trying to replicate what we do here. Every single country I go to wants to recreate Silicon Valley. We have it. We're not stopping here.

And David, to your point, whether it's the AI companies or frankly, whether it's the Intel shares or whether it's the TikTok fee, I now have a place where all of those wins are achieved by this administration can go they ought to go directly to all the citizens you know the country into their accounts and compound for a lifetime and then as far as people over the age of 18 there's certainly a lot of talk about that as well um not again as social security is a sacred promise by both parties nobody's going to change that but there's a huge opportunity to have a supplement here why shouldn't people you know between 20 and 30 or 20 and 40 also have a trump account that they can begin on a supplemental basis adding dollars that they own and control

Remember the big difference between this and social security Social Security takes 12.4% of my W-2 income and puts it into something akin to the black hole of government. I don't own it. I don't control it. If I ask a room of 3,000 people how much they've contributed, they have no idea. If I die, I don't have title. It doesn't pass to my heirs, et cetera. So it's really not mine. But if we created a supplemental IRA, it doesn't even require new legislation, I don't think. It's just expanding the age. These are IRAs after the age... of 18, right, then people could start building supplemental wealth and participate in these gains. And so there's a lot of conversations going on about that as well. And so we're not done. But it was a hell of a milestone on the 250th birthday of America to ring the bell in the oval and to watch all these kids get their accounts lit up. It was pretty special.

尾声：为什么这是"中产家庭规划"

What I think is really cool about the Trump accounts, I think it's an amazing philanthropic platform. But in addition to that, it's an amazing platform for middle-class family planning. That's the plan I'm trying to make is all the CPAs that I'm seeing talking about this or saying, this is like one of the greatest things ever. And if I could only tell my clients to do one thing, this would be the one thing. Finally, something for the middle class, right? This is what people have been asking for. Hey, let's get something for the middle class. Let's get something for the, you know, for poor people. Yeah.

And I think the market gap, correct me if I'm wrong, but basically the market gap that was created here is that you can't get an IRA, which is basically a tax advantage savings account until you have your first job, right? And get earnings, which would be for most people at age 22 plus. So for that first 22 years, your kids can't have an IRA, right? And you're effectively giving every child at birth IRA. Correct. You know, it's a Trump account that has certain rules. It's actually better than an IRA.

Better than an IRA. It's better than an IRA because with an IRA, your employer can't contribute $2,500 tax-free, can they? I mean... I don't think so. And philanthropists can't contribute to it and moms and dads.

But the most important thing, you know, like I say this, you know, Buffett's like the secret is to find a really small snowball on a really long hill. Okay. But the size of the hill in this country, we've cut off the first third of the hill forever. Nobody saves anything until they're 25. okay the easiest compounding in the world the easiest compounding in the world is between zero and 25.

All right listen this has been amazing yeah because they're dependents frankly they're still on mom and dad so you you pick up the first third of life incredible right in in compounding and so it's a it's really remarkable let's go uh I mean amazing job all right we'll see you all next time bye-bye

花絮

And it said, "We open source it to the fans and they've just gone crazy with it." Love you, West. I'm the queen of kids. Besties are gone. We should all just get a room and just have one big huge orgy because they're all just like this like sexual tension, but they just need to release somehow. You're the beat. What? You're the beat. We need to get merch. I'm going all in.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、隐藏的系统性洞察：这是一场"托管战争"，不是"模型战争"
大多数人把 AI 竞赛看作"谁的模型最聪明"，但这期节目里藏着一个更深的信号——真正的战场已经悄悄从 **model** 转移到 **harness（宿主/骨架）**。Databricks 的 Ali 说同一个模型换个 harness，成本能砍一半；Uber 用 "agentic pods" 把工程师嵌入业务部门；DoorDash 在做智能路由。这意味着什么？
**模型正在被"电力化"**——它会像电一样被交易、被路由、被套利，而**真正的价值层将是"AI 的电网调度系统"**。Chamath 一句话被大家忽略了：他把 token 成本降了 95% 后，agent 从"每天跑一次"变成"每小时跑一次"，然后又拆成三个 agent。这不是效率提升，**这是一次相变（phase transition）**——当边际成本趋近于零，人类会自动重构工作流的时间颗粒度。你以为你在省钱，实际上你在改变时间本身的定义。
## 二、跨领域连接：Trump Accounts 和大语言模型是同一个"复利战争"
看似完全不相关的两个话

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Why Land Powers Lose Millions and Sea Powers Don't – Sarah Paine
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的"成本函数"：地缘不是宿命，而是一个残酷的会计方程

Sarah Paine 表面在讲海权与陆权，但她真正揭示的是一个更深的东西——不同的地理位置，决定了一个国家使用暴力时的"单位成本"完全不同。

陆权国家每一次冲突，都是在自己客厅里打架：家具会碎、孩子会死、房子会烧。海权国家的战争则像"点外卖"——可以挑时间、挑地点、挑是否参与。这不是道德差异，而是成本结构差异。

一个惊人的观察：英国和美国二战阵亡人数居然可能低于意大利和法国。这意味着"胜利者"和"失败者"的死亡代价，比"陆权"和"海权"的死亡代价差得还要小。换句话说，在 20 世纪，选择站在哪块大陆上出生，比选择站在哪一方阵营，对你活下来的概率影响更大。

二、跨领域连接：这是"技术债"的地缘政治版本

我要提一个可能让你意外的类比——陆权国家的处境，本质上和背负巨额技术债的软件公司一模一样。

  • 技术债公司：每一次功能更新都要付出巨大代价，因为改一处会牵动十处；
  • 陆权国家：每一次外部冲击都会引发内部动荡，因为边境的一次震动就会传导到腹地。

而海权国家（英美）像是微服务架构的公司：模块解耦，故障隔离，可以选择性介入、选择性撤退。这解释了为什么斯大林式的极权治理会在陆权国家反复出现——当你的系统耦合度极高、故障成本极大，你会本能地追求"强控制"。军队的第一使命是"保政权"而非"打敌人"，正是这种系统结构的必然产物，而不是某个独裁者的个人癖好。

真正反直觉的推论：中国近年来大力发展海军，不只是为了投射力量，而是在尝试做一次地缘政治层面的"架构重构"——从耦合度极高的陆权模式，向解耦的海权模式迁移。但架构迁移永远比新建困难，这是华为、苹果、还有任何一个陆权大国都要面对的同一道题。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：海权的"选择权"才是真正的战略资产

大多数人以为海权的核心是"海军强"，但 Paine 悄悄透露了另一个更深的东西：海权国家真正拥有的，不是舰队，而是"是否参战"这个选项本身。

这在金融学里有个精确的名字——期权（Optionality）。

  • 陆权国家持有的是"被迫参战的义务"，本质是卖出期权（short option）——收益有限（守住领土），风险无限（可能灭国）；
  • 海权国家持有的是"可选择参战的权利"，本质是买入期权（long option）——损失有限（不介入就零成本），收益无限（介入胜利可重塑秩序）。

这才是最反常识的洞见：一个国家的地缘位置，本质上决定了它在国际博弈中天然持有的是"看涨期权"还是"看跌期权"。乌克兰的悲剧，不是它选错了盟友，而是它出生就被强行分配了一个"卖出期权"的头寸，还没得选择行权时间。

这也解释了美国当下最深的战略焦虑：过度介入意味着它正在把自己天然持有的看涨期权，一次次贱卖出去。Paine 那句"美国需要非常仔细地思考在到处介入之前"背后藏着的，其实是一个期权定价问题——不是"打不打"，而是"这个期权值不值得现在行权"。

一个更狠的推论：任何组织——公司、家庭、个人职业选择——都可以用"陆权/海权"来重新审视。你是否给自己保留了"不参战"的选项？还是每一次冲突你都必须应战？这决定了你的人生是"陆权人生"还是"海权人生"。

📝 转录文本

一、陆权世界：邻居即威胁

In this world, power is a function of land. Neighbors are dangerous, even the itty-bitty ones. If they're unstable, that instability can ooze over the border. Worse yet, if they're strong, stable neighbors and they have an idea that you're going to become a menu item for them, that's even worse.

二、两种军队，两种使命

So, in a maritime power, the army is for expeditionary warfare. What's the expedition? You cross the ocean and go somewhere else. That's the expedition.

In this world, purpose number one of an army in this world is you protect the ruling regime. So, if you think about the People's Liberation Army, it's goal number one, mission number one is to keep the Communist Party in power. That's not what the armies are in the maritime world. It's also secondarily is garrison the empire, prevent defections. And then thirdly, it's for border defense, and as you're fighting at the border, whatever you win, you keep, right? It's — you're expanding outwards, you hope.

In this world, away games are a rare event. Most of the fighting's occurring right on the border.

三、二战死亡数字：轴心国的沉没成本

So, these are statistics about deaths, not just casualties, wounded people. This is dead people. Uh, these are the sunk costs. All right. No one's breathing life into these people. They're sunk casualties.

So, if you're looking at, on axis, military deaths, you got:

  • 3.2 million dead German soldiers
  • 1.5 million Japanese
  • 330,000 Italians

四、盟军陆权 vs 盟军海权

Now, let's look at the Allied land powers:

  • 8.5 million dead Russian soldiers
  • Nearly a million dead Poles
  • 1.3 million dead Chinese
  • 340,000 dead Frenchmen

Okay, look at the maritime powers. By some statistics, Britain and the United States — Britain with 326,000 deaths, the United States 295,000. By some statistics, they suffered fewer deaths than Italy or France, and France wasn't in the war that long.

五、把平民算进去，差距变成天堑

And then when you add in all the civilian deaths, the numbers for the continental powers go catastrophic, whereas they hardly budge for the maritime powers. So, you've got:

  • Over 7 million dead Germans
  • Over 25 and 1/2 million dead Russians
  • Almost 7 million dead Poles
  • 11 million dead Chinese
  • Nearly a million dead Frenchmen

So, the deaths for the land powers are measured in millions, whereas the maritime powers, it's hundreds of thousands — still not a great number.

六、为什么会这样？没有海洋做缓冲

And what's going on here? It's because you're not insulated by the oceans, so the fighting tends to be on home territory. If that's the case, you're ruining civilians at a really rapid clip.

And no country in its right mind wants to fight on the main front if there are alternatives. But this is the continental powers' unavoidable agony: that when the neighbor invades, you've got a choice on that day — either you're going to capitulate or you're going to fight — and you're doing it on the neighbor's timing. That's Ukraine's problem.

七、海权的特权：选择是否介入

Whereas if you're a maritime power observing someone else being invaded — because in theory it's pretty tough to invade you by sea — it's a major point of strategy as to whether you're going to intervene at all, and if you are, what is the timing, what instruments of national power are you going to use?

And these are things that the United States needs to think about very carefully before it intervenes all over the place. And overextends.

八、代价与回报：胜利也要标价

But their advantage is: if you win this kind of warfare — if you think about Russians, enormous sacrifices. I've just listed that — for Stalin, it means he dominates Eastern Europe, or Russia does, for the duration of the Cold War.

But understand that that kind of territorial domination comes at a huge price. The value of the object had better be worth it before you try it.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here, and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

Sarah Paine 表面在讲海权与陆权，但她真正揭示的是一个更深的东西——**不同的地理位置，决定了一个国家使用暴力时的"单位成本"完全不同**。
陆权国家每一次冲突，都是在自己客厅里打架：家具会碎、孩子会死、房子会烧。海权国家的战争则像"点外卖"——可以挑时间、挑地点、挑是否参与。这不是道德差异，而是**成本结构差异**。

item.title: [Latent Space] Podcast Crossover: AIE, AGI, frontier lab strategy with ​ ⁨@matthew_berman⁩ and @swyxtv
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、隐藏的"中立性经济学"：为什么实验室愿意在敌人的主场同台竞技

Swyx 说的最有趣的一句话被大多数人忽略了："the labs like winning on an even playing field"。表面上这是关于会议的商业逻辑，但底层揭示了前沿实验室之间一种囚徒困境的反转——他们在自己的 Dev Day 上赢得毫无意义（那是主场作战），只有在中立的擂台上击败对手，才能真正兑现为工程师心智份额。

这与冷战时期的奥运会有惊人的同构性：苏联和美国都拒绝在对方的邀请赛上认真参赛，但都全力投入奥运会。中立性本身成为了稀缺资源。 换句话说，AIE 之所以能存在，不是因为 Swyx 有资源，而是因为他成功地把"中立"变成了一种可以定价的经济商品。这是社区型创业者最容易忽略的杠杆——你不需要拥有任何资源，你只需要拥有一个所有巨头都无法自己创造的坐标系。

二、"AI Engineer"的空间生态位：Swyx 无意间描述了一个物理学模型

他说："AI engineer exists in the white surface area between the peak capability and deploying it everywhere else"——这一句话可以直接映射到热力学中的相界面（phase interface）。

在物理学里，最激烈的化学反应从不发生在均匀的液体或气体里，而是发生在两相接触的边界——催化剂之所以有效，就是因为它人为地增加了这个界面积。Swyx 的洞察在于：AI 工程师不是在能力峰值那里工作，也不是在成熟部署那里工作，而是在两者之间不断扩张的、参差不齐的表面上工作。

由此衍生一个反直觉推论：模型能力提升越快，AI 工程师越忙，而不是越失业。因为峰值上升了，但部署面永远滞后，界面反而更厚。人们担心 AGI 抢走工作，其实忽略了"能力-部署"落差的几何学：曲面越陡峭，界面越大。这也解释了他为什么坚定看好 Agent Lab 模式——那正是相界面催化剂的商业化。

三、跨领域连接：50,000 年时间尺度、新加坡淡马锡、和 Foundation 里的心理史学

Swyx 谈 pdoom 时突然把时间尺度拉到 50,000 年，然后又提到新加坡的 Temasek/GIC 主权基金模式，最后引用了阿西莫夫的《基地》——这三点看似跳跃，实际暗藏一个统一的思维模式：他习惯用超长时间尺度去解构短期政策问题。

这里有个真正令人意外的观察：Swyx 的政治直觉是新加坡式的，而不是硅谷式的。 硅谷主流对 OpenAI-政府 5% 股权的反应通常是"这就是社会主义/贝多社会主义"，但 Swyx 立刻从淡马锡模型的角度理解为"这是养老金社会契约的一部分"。这不是意识形态偏好，而是他成长在一个政府就是最大 LP 的经济体里，所以他看到 5% 股权时想到的不是"国家控制"，而是"分红机制"。

跨领域的深层连接是：新加坡模式其实和阿西莫夫的心理史学是同一种思维——都是用超长时间尺度换取短期政策的正当性。淡马锡可以持有一只股票 40 年，因为它的持有者（国家）时间跨度是无限的。Foundation 里的心理史学能预测千年，因为它假设人类作为群体的行为在长时间尺度上是可预测的。当 Swyx 说他的 pdoom 是"50,000 年为分母"时，他其实是在用主权基金持有者的时间视角看 AI 安全问题——这是硅谷 EAC 和 doomer 双方都想不到的第三条路。

附赠一个反直觉预测

他谈到 Etched、Cerebras、Groq 时说了一句"cerebras of the world didn't optimize for it because it didn't exist back then"。反过来推：下一代 ASIC 公司的最大风险不是模型架构变化，而是它们的芯片会训练出人们对 transformer 架构的路径依赖。Etched 的成功可能会推迟下一代非 transformer 架构的出现 5-10 年，因为整个推理经济的沉没成本被锁死了。这正是自动驾驶里雷达 vs 纯视觉之争的翻版——赢家不是技术更好者，而是先把生态锁死者。

📝 转录文本

Podcast Crossover: AIE, AGI, Frontier Lab Strategy

with Matthew Berman & Swyx (Latent Space)

1. The Origin of the AI Engineer Conference

Matthew: I don't even know how to start this. You have 30 minutes, right?

Swyx: Yeah. Yeah.

Matthew: This conference is incredible. There are so many people here. I want to start with the origin. How did you think to start an AI engineering conference? How did it come about?

Swyx: Yeah. I've been a speaker for many years before that and I also had seen people seize a moment where you can see an industry shift. And I thought it was pretty clear. I had seen basically front-end engineering become its own professionalized field with dedicated conferences, dedicated influencers and tech stacks and all those things, and I'd seen the same thing for cloud engineering and data engineering and all that. And I was like, it's very obviously going to happen to AI.

And so I bought the domain AI.engineer and planned this conference and I wrote the blog post, and it really kicked off when Andrej Karpathy also endorsed it. And I think it's gotten more true over time — which is the kind of way I like to see it. Gartner put it at the peak of its hype cycle two years ago. It's still hypy, still going.

Matthew: Had you started a conference before? Had you ever done a conference before? You were just like, okay, I'm just going to do it?

Swyx: Well, I didn't do it alone. I partnered with a friend who had done previous conferences. So basically from my conference speaking career, I just took the best of every conference I had been to prior. And Ben was running React-a-thon, which is the best React conference on the west coast. So I just had him start AIE, and I would do the content, he would do the logistics, and that's how it goes.

Matthew: What was the most difficult part first, during your first AIE, and then this one?

Swyx: Yeah, I think the first one was just convincing people that this is even worth showing up to. Because like a first of anything, you don't have any history. Now people have word of mouth, they have the YouTube channel, it's all good. I think the very first one, you got nothing, and people just have to take a leap of faith of like, well, my reputation, Ben's reputation come together — do let's do something interesting, I don't know, right?

And it was also down not that much in terms of the tech, right? Mostly we were still talking about prompt frameworks and maybe a bit of RAG, but there just weren't any agents. It was very, very primitive at the time.

I think also the last thing was getting a big lab to come. For us it was Logan at OpenAI at the time. And I think a big part of our value proposition now is that we can get all these labs to show up at a neutral place where they all compete on the same grounds, which is maximally beneficial for engineers and most competitive for the labs. And it turns out the labs like it. They like winning on an even playing field. But you're just not going to get that in like a Dev Day or Code with Claude.

Matthew: You know what I really appreciate? There seems to be the entire stack of complexity here. Everything from really practical everyday user, kind of standard user, and then all the way down to highly technical, deep in the weeds.

2. Custom Silicon: Etched, Cerebras, Groq

Swyx: I just did a one-hour chip session with Etched, which just came out of stealth this week.

Matthew: So good. Yeah. Yeah. I just saw that. What do you think about Etched, and are they going to disrupt Nvidia?

Swyx: I don't think they want to disrupt Nvidia. I think the better framing is that all of inference is just so goddamn big that of course you're going to have ASICs for inference. And Nvidia is going to continue to build GPUs.

Matthew: Is Etched more akin to a Groq or a Cerebras?

Swyx: Yeah, I have been pitching them — or not pitching, but framing them to friends as next-generation Cerebras, next-generation Groq. Cerebras started 10-plus years ago. Now they're IPO'd. That process and form factor is relatively well established. What Etched and MatX and the other new-gen chips are doing are much more dedicated to basically everything that we know post-transformers, post-ChatGPT, and optimizing for that workload. So it completely makes sense that the Cerebras of the world didn't optimize for it, because it didn't exist back then.

Matthew: Yeah. So when you make custom chips, you run the risk that the model architecture changes drastically due to some kind of innovation, right?

Swyx: Yeah. They don't care.

Matthew: They don't care.

Swyx: The ChatGPT-like GPT-3.5-ish architecture has mostly stayed the same this entire time. It's a pretty good bet, man. Even if there is a new model sometime in the future, the current workloads on existing models — like 4o is still being used by some folks, because they just don't move. Once the thing works, it works. Don't touch it.

3. Fable 5, Refusals, and the "Nerfed" Narrative

Matthew: All right. I want to talk about something new, and you've been obviously quite busy this week. Fable 5 is now back. Have you had a chance to —

Swyx: They released it for us.

Matthew: They did. They released it just for you. I don't think — maybe you tell me — have you even had a chance to —

Swyx: Yeah. Yeah. So there's an Easter egg on the website that is built by Fable. It's a game that lets you —

Matthew: AI Engineer.

Swyx: Yeah.

Matthew: Okay. You want to give any hints as to what to look for for the Easter egg?

Swyx: Look for a globe icon that bounces when you hover over it.

Matthew: All right. So you've played around with it. Does it feel the same as those couple days when it was first released? Because a lot of people are talking about it being nerfed and there's a lot floating around on X right now.

Swyx: I see. Yeah, people are very quick to complain about Anthropic. I don't know if it's nerfed since the first Fable launch. I haven't — I don't have hard numbers on that. I know that there are false refusals — they happen and still happen a lot, and those are really annoying when they happen, right? The downgrades, the Opus.

Matthew: I haven't had that happen to me once. I haven't had a reroute happen to me once yet, which is —

Swyx: Maybe you're not spicy enough.

Matthew: Maybe. I'm definitely ripping tokens, though. So if it were going to happen, it would be there.

Swyx: But maybe you need to talk about chemical weapons or something and then it'll go.

Matthew: Not a lot of my projects need that.

Swyx: Exactly. So no, look — when it works, it is extremely smart. But it's also very slow. And definitely I don't see myself using it for everything because of that. You only use it for the smart problems, and that's probably the way they want it anyway.

I think the other part about — you know, there was a narrative like a month ago where people were like, "Oh, Anthropic's limited on chips, limited on compute capacity, that's why they're not rolling out Mythos and Fable." That's clearly not true.

Matthew: Well, now it's not true, right? I mean —

Swyx: It probably wasn't true a month ago. It didn't change that much. Like, they know their roadmap. They just partnered with — I think it was AWS — and they landed a bunch of chip deals, a bunch of compute bandwidth deals.

Matthew: But you think they weren't planning these for months before?

Swyx: They knew this. XAI, that was the big one, right? Anyway, I'm just saying, these things don't happen just overnight. The Mythos rollout was genuinely safety related — that's which why it was limited. It wasn't because there's some secret conspiracy to limit or ration compute.

Matthew: I still do think they were bandwidth limited or they were compute limited, because if you look at their quota and how aggressive in reducing it and using it — it's gotten much better, but especially two months ago, I mean, you would burn through your quota in a matter of minutes.

Swyx: It's happening again to be fair with Fable, because it just uses so many more tokens.

Matthew: Two months ago, it just seems like they could not get enough compute to serve the models that they have.

Swyx: Yeah. Well, only they know.

4. OpenAI, the 5% Equity Stake, and Government

Matthew: All right, let's talk about the other frontier lab. One of the others. Let's talk about OpenAI. I woke up — I think it was this morning — to the news, I guess rumored, I haven't actually read more deeply into it, but OpenAI's offering 5% equity stake to the US government. What do you think is going on there?

Swyx: I saw that headline. I don't know anything about the sources. I think it kind of tracks. It's not outside the range of possible things to do. OpenAI has been relatively more friendly with the White House than with other frontier labs. That's factual to say.

I also just generally do think the people of the United States — or the people of any country where all such frontier intelligence is created — they do need to have a share or a say in some upside. Otherwise, you have a permanent underclass in significant social distress.

I've played around with the idea of universal basic AI — like everyone gets a ChatGPT Pro subscription or something. But giving the government 5% to have the government have a stake in the success of OpenAI as a leading lab in the US does tend to make sense.

I come from a country where this is normal. I'm from Singapore. Temasek and GIC own significant portions of the Singapore economy, and it works just fine. And actually a lot of our pensions and our savings and our insurance and whatever is all invested in nationally critical, important companies where the government owns the stake. What's so different?

Matthew: Do you think they're going to treat it more like a utility? There's a few different flavors of how this plays out. The government owns 5%, they give out distributions to US citizens — or maybe they treat it much more like a utility and they start heavily regulating and can step in at any time. Obviously we're speculating now — what do you think is most likely to happen?

Swyx: I think this thing is too volatile to treat it as a utility, right? Imagine if Edison was working on his electrical stuff and lighting and all that, and he immediately tried to regulate it. Like, no, you'd probably wait 50 years first. So it's a little unfair to, in 2025, four years after ChatGPT, be like, "No, we should regulate it now like a utility because there's no more innovation to be had and you should just leave it to natural monopolies and charge cost plus." I don't think we're there yet. I think we need to wait another few decades.

Matthew: So what is it? Is it pay to play? Is it bend the knee? Is it, hey, we're going to give you 5% and you're going to allow us to release our models more quickly? You're going to step back on regulation? Speculate with me, Swyx.

Swyx: So, I'm not a citizen, so I do tend to be careful about what I say politically. I do think that it is interesting to think through people optimizing for this administration — versus, well, there's an election every four years and actually this is a multi-turn game, not a single-turn game.

Matthew: So, maybe don't only think in one turn.

Swyx: Yeah.

Matthew: Okay, so then let's continue down this track a little bit. The government, especially in the last few weeks, has been very hands-on deciding what models are publicly available, the timing of models, right? GPT-5.6 was just announced, but they have to wait a few weeks to actually release it. Do you think this is the new standard? Do you think they're kind of scrambling to come up with a framework that's consistent across all of the AI labs? What do you think's going on?

Swyx: I think the White House thinks it is consistent, and it is probably better than what the states tried to do themselves a year plus ago with SB47 — all those things. So, yeah, I mean, yes, on the national level, do it.

And beyond that, I think the best I can say about this is: this isn't new in terms of tactics in politics. And this is the Art of the Deal.

Matthew: Yeah. Honestly, I want to read the book. It's an initial gambit that is quite extreme, then you roll it back, but you —

Swyx: Yeah. I mean, it's literally — he wrote it about 40 years ago, however long it was, 30 years.

Matthew: It works.

Swyx: It works.

Matthew: And would it be funny if this is actually how humanity is saved from Skynet — is the Art of the Deal? Because there's no other mechanism under capitalism to control companies.

5. p(doom), 50,000-Year Timelines, and Alignment

Matthew: Do you think the government is more worried about Skynet, or do you think they're more worried about other countries doing distillation hacking, or using the model for cyber warfare themselves? What do you think they're most afraid of?

Swyx: I think there's multiple reasons that all align. So when they all align, it's hard to tell motivations because they all point to the same answer. So there's no point ranking them because they're all aligning.

I am a doomer in terms of people talking about p(doom). I've met people with p(doom) of zero, p(doom) of less than 1%. Mine is closer to 90 — on a scale of 50,000 years. I think people's p(doom) should be attached to a timeline.

Matthew: 50,000 years?

Swyx: Which is the rough approximate time of homo sapiens, right? 50,000 to 400,000 is our span. And we don't have a right to exist any more than anything else that went existing before us. And if we're birthing a new life form, it is reasonable to expect that, by accident, we may not do so well compared to it.

I mean, how are trees doing versus human civilization? And when we move much faster than trees, these things move much, much faster than us. I think it absolutely bears some thought.

Matthew: Yeah, what you're saying is basically the chance of actually aligning these models in the long run is very low.

Swyx: I think our default assumption should be that it's low, and if we miss in the right direction, we will have missed in the safe direction, anyway.

Matthew: Yeah, that's fair.

Swyx: So one thing that I often don't comment about is where AIE is in relation with E/Acc and the Deel movement — and it's right in the middle. I talked about this at the last WS Fair keynote: politically, we want to be pragmatic. You want to be optimistic, but you also don't want to be unconstrained optimistic, which is what E/Acc is. And that's why putting guardrails and systems and fine-tuning and doing evals are important. At AIE, versus E/Acc — you just talk about AGI timelines all day long, right? Because you don't care.

I do think that some measure of having the engineers in control of things, watching things, monitoring chain of thought, probably is the right path for humanity. So like the line of, you know, as far as we know we're the only life form in the observable universe — life is very fragile, and we shouldn't assume that by default we get the right to continue existing unless you engineer it.

Matthew: If you had to shrink the timeline from 50,000 to — let's get your p(doom) on 10 years and 50 years?

Swyx: Oh yeah. I mean p(doom) in 10 years is near zero.

Matthew: Well, that's good. That's good.

Swyx: 50 is like the end of our lifetimes. I don't know. I'm just throwing out 10. No, 10's too high. 5%. And I think on the timelines, more of what you see in Foundation, you know — the TV show.

Matthew: Yeah.

Swyx: Or Dune.

Matthew: Great show. Great show.

Swyx: When you think about the long evolution of life forms and history, you shouldn't be so near-term focused. Because probably LLMs are going to run out at some point and they're not AGI, and okay, we have maybe another 30 years of AI winter or something, and then the next paradigm really is actually the thing — or it takes another 3,000 generations. Who are we to say that we happen to live in the exact moment that everything ends? That's very egotistical — like you're the main character, really. Who's to say you're not in prehistory right now, as far as the future people are concerned?

6. LLMs, Recursive Self-Improvement, and Data Efficiency

Matthew: Do you think large language models are enough to lead to recursive self-improvement, thus leading to some next architecture that might be AGI?

Swyx: Oh, I see. That is a very nuanced question. I think actually my answer is no on that one. Yes, LLMs enable RSI. RSI is really just — is it recursing? And yes, it is recursing. We have a whole auto-research track yesterday covering that stuff. But it is limited in its recursion because it probably just explores things that have been explored before. So it's not that far off from the distribution of stuff we already know.

So discovering true unknown unknowns, having real innovation, having a real sense of world models — that is still the domain of research, and yeah, we probably need something else. We did have a world models track here. It is not very well developed. Everyone knows it, but clearly this needs to be fleshed out more, because the stuff that Dwarkesh is saying is absolutely right.

Current learning paradigms — data efficiency is the next problem. We had a data track on the same thing. Learning over trillions of tokens in order to get to some form of human-equivalent labor is very, very inefficient versus what humans do. Humans learn on the order of millions, and they can already do very useful things. Billions, and you're a full working adult.

Matthew: So do we have to try to fit large language models — the compute that it requires — into kind of the human box? Why does it have to be, well, if it's not exactly how humans learn, then it's not the right way?

Swyx: No, I absolutely agree with what you're saying there. I call this a sour lesson, right? Every time you try to make a human analogy to machines, you probably fail, because machines develop very differently from humans.

So no, I agree. And I also think that it can still be an alien form of more efficient learning. Doesn't matter. We just know that it's super inefficient. That is something that we know is an unmitigated negative. So let's make it more efficient and better. That means, you know, instead of 2,000 examples to learn one thing, what about 20 examples? What about two examples? That scales a lot more.

And that means we can get to a point with continual learning that we can actually have agents that adapt and build up a real world model. Otherwise, we're always stuck to the pre-train / post-train paradigm that is probably hitting some kind of limit right now.

I genuinely do expect Fable to be the end of this era of LLMs, because — I already told you about the slowness. Do you agree with the slowness, by the way, that Fable's slow?

Matthew: I've actually been using it in Cursor and it feels a little bit faster than when it first came out a couple weeks ago. But it is slow. It is slow.

Swyx: I mean, Anthropic is working on better inference. But it is slow, and therefore you just won't use it for some things that don't require Fable. You also don't want to pay Fable prices for everything.

I'm trying to live in an infinite budget world, but the budget I don't have is time, right? This is the limiting budget that everyone has. I don't care if you're working at a frontier lab, you still have time as a limiter. So if a 20-trillion model is it — there's no 200-trillion, there's no quadrillion-scale model — then that's kind of it as far as usability is concerned, living in an infinite budget environment. So therefore, you just need something different. Whatever it is — maybe it's the Thinking Machines stuff, maybe it's Together AI's SSM stuff — whatever it is, we don't have it yet.

7. Capability Overhang and Advice for Founders

Matthew: I've thought a lot about this. The model capability overhang still seems very real — even from the previous generation Opus, GPT-5.5, the amount of value that can be extracted from those models still seems, at least to me, to be critical. And now we have a whole new generation of models that we're even going to get more model overhang from. Do you agree with that? Do you think we need to keep building the tools around —

Swyx: AI Engineer exists in the white surface area between the peak capability and deploying it everywhere else, right? So the more model research peaks and spikes capabilities in one domain, but it's not evenly distributed in all products yet — that's where engineers have a job forever, basically. So I'm very pro that.

I think capability overhang will exist for a long time. I think it does keep having waves of consolidation where you're like, actually, all this stuff I built out, I don't need it anymore because the next model has got it from just a single prompt. So I'm going to throw it out. But we do spring cleaning every now and then. That's normal. And we build it up on previous-gen model assumptions that then go away. And I don't think we should feel any attachment to the code. At the end of the day, we're all just trying to serve customers better, do work cheaper, faster, easier.

Matthew: Let's talk about that a little bit, because I've built a bunch of cool one-off projects that one model generation later I was like, "Oh, well, that's kind of useless now." And then you have Anthropic moving in different directions. They're moving up the stack to the application layer. If you're a founder, what advice would you give to founders who are trying to build at the application layer? Where should they be spending their time? How should they think about the expanding model capabilities in each subsequent generation of model?

Swyx: Yeah, my answer to this is two words: agent lab. I like the two-word solutions. I like AI Engineer. I like agent lab. I have a piece on this if people want to see. It's on — they can find it at latent.space/agentlabs.

Matthew: Okay.

Swyx: The idea is that you always want to be the AI guys for your customers. Actually don't pick the solution — pick the problem. And if you're like, okay, whatever it is in AI, whatever the hot thing is, whatever the new trend is, whatever the new model is, I will be the AI guy for dentists, or for lawyers, or for finance people, or for coders, whatever — then you will just build that lasting brand of, we will build the products that do the last mile for you and fold in all the new functionalities and features that people discover into that. That is the sustainable thing.

Matthew: Isn't that betting against the generalization of the models and the capability of the labs, frankly?

Swyx: No. Yeah. So let me think about this. No, but maybe yes. I don't know where I stand with regards to your question, because the models do generalize and sometimes wipe out entire product categories. But I do think that if you are smart enough — like, I literally am the AI layer for lawyers, I'm your outsource tech team for lawyers. Okay, that, my old business model is gone. Fine. I'll make a new one with whatever new capability overhang is created.

What you're betting against is capability overhangs ever existing in the future. And I think that's a pretty safe bet to make. There will always be capability overhangs. They may not stay still. So you got to be nimble. But the Sierras of the world, the Cognitions of the world, the Cursors of the world, the Decagons and Harveys — these are all agent labs for their field. They can be trusted brand names to always apply the latest AI to their thing.

And maybe they're a little bit behind the frontier labs on the latest models, but in terms of solving user/customer feedback — I've been inside of Cognition for the last six months. There's no one else. The labs do not have 200 people dedicated to being on call with Goldman Sachs going, "Okay guys, what do you need? We got it. You need the Microsoft Teams Zero integration — got it. You don't use GitHub, you use this weird org thing that is like a fork of Atlassian's Bitbucket when it was open source — got it." Claude's not going to do that.

8. Token Budgets, Model Routing, and the Model-Agnostic Era

Matthew: I mean, let's continue on that. So you've been at Cognition for six months, and you had the food — it scores well on Food Bench.

Swyx: It is a very good food-bench-high on food-bench benchmark. We need to score better on other benchmarks, but food is a good start.

Matthew: You just have to invite me back. That's how you got to run the benchmark again.

A lot of discussion lately has been around token budget and really token-maxing being unattainable to most people. Do you think that's actually a huge value to the non-frontier lab, agent-lab companies? So the Cursors, the Cognitions, the Factories of the world — isn't this kind of like prime time for them? Because first of all, they're model agnostic. They actually have incentive to do model routing really well, whereas the frontier labs don't. Do you think this is the era of the model-agnostic companies?

Swyx: Yeah, I think that is a reasonable conclusion to make — which doesn't mean that I think it. I think that is currently what everyone is saying because of this current debate.

I do observe that in general, the big, big wins have just been going all in on one thing. And I'm old enough in tech to have seen this before with the cloud wave, where there have been companies that were all in on AWS, or all in on GCP. And there have been other companies that were multi-cloud there always — you know, like, I'll use the Terraform, the whatever.

And the simple argument is that if you route, if you pride yourself on routing, you will never exploit the full capabilities of one, because you're not all in on one. So it's a really good way to be lowest common denominator of every model out there and completely miss all capability. You also don't have the economies of scale that an AWS has going all in owning the entire stack.

Yeah. So agent labs get discounts from every model provider. And that's also very interesting when people compare public pricing of a discounted Claude Code from Anthropic versus what Anthropic does with agent labs. That's also an interesting discussion on that front too.

Anyway, all of which to say — I do think that this routing thing is a marketing line. I wouldn't necessarily believe it so much as when I talked to the top-tier agent builders, because they're all about maximizing and fully exploring the complete prompt surface area and optimization area and tool use and caching and God knows what else in there, right? Exploit everything they give you. Or do you want to just stay superficial and only do chat completions across 100 different models? Which is more likely to win as an agent lab?

9. Closing

Matthew: All right. Well, Swyx, I want to say thank you very much. I appreciate the conversation.

Swyx: It's good to chat, man. I'm glad you could see me on my biggest day, on my biggest show. I think you've seen me in the podcast, the office and all those things. This is — I'm an in-person real community guy.

Matthew: I am genuinely impressed with what you built here. You have a bunch of great, excited, enthusiastic people, not only giving talks, but also learning a ton, making connections, getting co-founders.

Swyx: I need your help on our YouTube stuff.

Matthew: Let's do it.

Swyx: Yeah. Lots of learning to be had.

Matthew: Thanks, Swyx.

Swyx: Thanks. Very nice.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、隐藏的"中立性经济学"：为什么实验室愿意在敌人的主场同台竞技
Swyx 说的最有趣的一句话被大多数人忽略了："the labs like winning on an even playing field"。表面上这是关于会议的商业逻辑，但底层揭示了前沿实验室之间一种**囚徒困境的反转**——他们在自己的 Dev Day 上赢得毫无意义（那是主场作战），只有在中立的擂台上击败对手，才能真正兑现为工程师心智份额。
这与冷战时期的奥运会有惊人的同构性：苏联和美国都拒绝在对方的邀请赛上认真参赛，但都全力投入奥运会。**中立性本身成为了稀缺资源。** 换句话说，AIE 之所以能存在，不是因为 Swyx 有资源，而是因为他成功地把"中立"变成了一种可以定价的经济商品。这是社区型创业者最容易忽略的杠杆——**你不需要拥有任何资源，你只需要拥有一个所有巨头都无法自己创造的坐标系**。
## 二、"AI Engineer"的空间生态位：Swyx 无意间描述了一个物理学模型

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Why Oracles Are DeFi's Achilles Heel and How to Ensure They're Not Manipulated
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、Oracle 的"沉默恐惧"：不是报错，而是失语

Nicholas 顺口提到的一个观察值得反复咀嚼——"Oracle 最糟的不是报错价，而是彻底停止报价"。这颠覆了大多数人对系统风险的直觉。我们习惯把"错误"当成最大威胁，但在 DeFi 里，"沉默"比"谎言"更致命：谎言至少给了协议一个可以反应的信号（哪怕是错的），而沉默让整个偿付能力判断陷入形而上学的悬置——你不知道自己是不是破产了。

这其实和临床医学里的一个悖论惊人相似：ICU 中真正致命的往往不是"仪器报警"，而是"仪器脱线"。心电监护仪响了，护士会冲过来；监护仪没信号，可能只是"设备故障"，被暂时忽略。在高风险系统中，"无信号"是最昂贵的信号，因为它伪装成常态。

二、跨领域连接：Oracle 其实是一种"分权制衡"，而非"数据管道"

Nicholas 用了一个非常关键但容易被忽略的比喻——Oracle 应该是 "neutral reconciliation agent"（中立对账代理）。他把它类比为传统金融里 fund admin / custodian / transfer agent 的角色分离。

这里藏着一个跨领域洞见：Oracle 的本质根本不是"技术基础设施"，而是"宪政意义上的分权"。它更像美国联邦制里的"独立审计署"，或古罗马的"保民官"——一个存在的意义就是打断利益共谋的第三方。

这个视角一旦建立，你会突然意识到： - LayerZero、CCIP 这些跨链桥，本质上也是 Oracle（Nicholas 亲口证实了这一点，这个观察被大多数人忽略） - KelpDAO 事件的教训不是"桥不安全"，而是"当发行者、桥、Oracle 出现角色重叠时，制衡失效" - 而当前 DeFi 生态里最危险的趋势，正是"一体化协议"试图把发行、定价、清算、风控揉在一起——这在宪政意义上是"三权归一"，是走向暴政的第一步

三、令人意外的观察：DeFi 的"进入容易退出难"，暴露了一个金融物理学的不对称性

最出人意料的洞见藏在 Nicholas 那段关于 leverage loop 的描述里。他指出：在代币化资产的 leverage loop 里，"建仓"可以用 flash loan 完成，但"平仓"却卡在赎回窗口上。

这不只是 DeFi 的技术问题，而是一个深层的金融物理学不对称：

杠杆的"熵"是单向的——建立需要 O(1) 的时间，拆解需要 O(n) 的时间。

这个不对称在传统金融里被"做市商 + 央行最后贷款人"这个双层缓冲吸收了。但 DeFi 想跳过这层缓冲，直接把不可即时清算的资产（CLO、私募信贷、REIT）塞进即时清算的引擎里——这相当于给一辆 F1 赛车装上了拖拉机的刹车。

Grove Basin 那种"redemption facility"其实是在用 DeFi 原生方式重新发明"做市商"。这暗示了一个反直觉的预测：

代币化 RWA 的下一个大爆点，不会是"更多资产上链"，而是"上链后的退出流动性即服务"（Exit Liquidity as a Service）。

谁能解决"下车问题"，谁就控制了下一个百亿美元的赛道。这个位置目前几乎是空的——而这，可能才是 Chronicle 这类基础设施提供者真正的护城河：他们不只是知道资产"是什么"，他们还是唯一有能力实时判断资产"能不能被卖掉"的人。

一个额外的系统性直觉：Nicholas 说他们"10 年零 Oracle 被黑"，这不是运气，而是"delusional, psychotic focus on security"。这句话隐藏了一个 Lindy 效应的深层机制——在加密世界里，"没发生过的事"本身就是资产，而且这种资产无法通过营销制造，只能通过时间沉淀。这也是为什么 DeFi 未来会出现一种奇特的分化：不是按 TVL 排序，而是按"未爆炸秒数"排序。

嘉宾介绍：Chronicle Labs 与 Nicholas Kunkel

Alright, welcome back. So I'm here today with Nicholas Kunkel. He is the founder of Chronicle Labs, an Oracle provider supporting the entire DeFi space with a heavy focus on tokenized assets. And we're going to talk about the landscape of DeFi, the growth of tokenized assets, and how they're being integrated into the plumbing of the entire financial ecosystem.

Really, one thing I really want to get into is how we can understand the differences between various Oracle providers, given how critical they are to this space, and explore how the industry has dealt with certain challenges over the years as well. So a lot to talk about, Nicholas. Welcome. Thanks for joining.

Nicholas: Thank you for having me. Yeah, I'm a huge fan of the show, and I watch almost every episode. So great to finally be on here.

Steve: Okay, that's nice to hear, nice to say. I keep trying to get my kids to watch, and they are not interested at all.

MakerDAO 起源：DeFi 从零开始的那些日子

So let's really kind of get started, because Chronicle and yourself has a pretty interesting background. I mean, you've been in crypto for at least a decade at this point. And it kind of started with MakerDAO, the DAI stablecoin, SAI, I think now it's called USDS, etc.

It's a really fascinating project because in many ways it created the entire DeFi space. And what I find most impressive is that it hasn't gone down. I mean, just like Ethereum itself, it's operated through bear and bull markets alike. It's one of the real success stories. And that's the origin story for yourself and what you're building at Chronicle Labs.

So maybe just for anyone in my audience who's not familiar with your background, could you please walk us through what those early days were like and how it spawned the idea for Chronicle?

Nicholas: Absolutely. Yep, so my background — I was part of the founding team of MakerDAO. And I think looking back, it seems kind of like manifest destiny. But at the time, there wasn't really any DeFi. The only stablecoin was Tether. And there wasn't an ecosystem of, say, DEXes and lending protocols.

I think it was quite forward-looking at the time to say, "Oh, there's going to be this DeFi, and at the center of DeFi there will be these stablecoins, and we don't want centralized stablecoins — we want a decentralized stablecoin." In hindsight, it all looks very obvious, but at the time it was very out there and experimental.

Even Ethereum as a chain had only been live for maybe less than a year. There weren't really any apps on it — at least nothing serious. So we really had to build everything from scratch.

Oracle 的诞生：从没有 Solidity 调试器的时代

And this is really kind of where my journey started with respect to Oracles. One day we were building a debugger because there was no Solidity debugger. One day we're building a DEX because it was actually the first DEX on Ethereum. And it was because Maker didn't have any funding, and we're like, "How do we get funding? Well, if we build this thing called a DEX, we can sell some tokens and we can get some funding and maybe we can pay salaries this month."

It was very much DIY, build everything on your own. And that included Oracles. This notion of an Oracle didn't really exist, but we knew we needed a way to externally price something in a kind of trustless manner. And that really kind of set everything off for me.

Steve: Did you call it an Oracle then, or what did you actually call it? And what you needed, correct me if I'm wrong, is you needed to be able to understand the price of ETH because that affects the LTV for the stablecoins that were borrowed against that collateral.

Nicholas: Exactly. The way Maker works in a simple way is, you could lock up your ETH as collateral and you would borrow DAI stablecoins against that. And so the protocol needed to know how much is an ETH worth — both for determining the initial amount that you could borrow, but also just maintaining the collateralization of the system over time. If the price of ETH dropped, it would know which loans it would need to liquidate.

早期价格提供者的匿名传统

Steve: Just going back through a little of the history of this myself as I was preparing for the show, I didn't even realize this, but it seems like a lot of the individuals that were those early-day price providers were actually pseudonymous, because there were concerns that if they were identifiable, there could be pressure for them to manipulate the price and thus manipulate the value of DAI.

It seems like the antithesis of where Oracles are today, where it's named participants who provide that information — I'm generalizing across the whole span of Oracles — but in exchange for compensation. So it's really fascinating that you saw a security threat with that. Perhaps appropriately cautious to maintain the fidelity of the price feeds.

Nicholas: Sure. I mean, when you look at a DeFi protocol, the Achilles heel of any DeFi protocol is its Oracle. It lives and dies by its Oracle.

If an Oracle prints that ETH is a trillion dollars, anyone can just go and drain all of the liquidity out of any lending market, out of any credit protocol. Similarly, if the Oracle reports ETH is zero, you can liquidate all of the ETH positions in a lending protocol. You could potentially trigger — if you're looking at the likes of Aave and Sky — you could potentially trigger a liquidation cascade on ETH that has far more second-order effects than just the on-chain liquidations.

Oracle 的本质：数据完整性与可用性

Steve: Yeah, absolutely. And I guess just a level set for my audience. Most people probably know what an Oracle is, at least at a high level. But could you just briefly talk about how deeply Oracles have penetrated crypto? Across different types of either assets or different DeFi protocols. Maybe put a few dollar amounts behind it in terms of either TVL or the amount of daily DeFi activity.

Nicholas: I think it would be easier to say what are Oracles not touching in crypto than to go the other way around.

In simple terms, an Oracle is like a data reference. So you have some data somewhere and that somewhere can be off-chain, it could be another blockchain, and it needs to be transported to wherever that data is needed.

Let's use ETH as an example. You're fetching prices from all kinds of centralized exchanges — from Coinbase, Binance, OKX, Bybit. You're combining that with on-chain sources like Uniswap. You may even be combining that with the ETH futures on CME. You are aggregating all of this information, and then you're shipping it off to the blockchain where it's needed, so an application can consume it.

The primary two things here that an Oracle needs to concern itself with are:

  1. What are the data integrity guarantees? How do you ensure that [the data is accurate]?
  2. What are the data availability guarantees? How do you ensure that this flow of data continues and doesn't just stop?

Because the second worst thing to happen to an Oracle is not that it prints a wrong price, but that it just stops printing any updated price. And now a DeFi protocol has no idea whether it is solvent or not, or whether it is issuing safe credit safely, or whether it is issuing credit at a loss.

历史事件：Mango、USDe 与价格协调

Steve: Yeah, I mean, all those points make a lot of sense. And I think anyone that's followed the DeFi space over time has seen some of those concerns or risks really come to reality. We can talk about a few of the various issues with price — from like the Mango Markets exploit, which was a "quote-unquote very probable trading strategy" to exploit — even what happened on 10/10 when I think it was, what was it? Like USDe price was different on Binance compared to other exchanges. I think those two events are very different though.

Funny ways to have accurate pricing, also harmonize it across various platforms and stuff, so there's not those sort of hiccups that can lead to liquidations, etc. It just underscores the importance of the work that you guys do.

Chronicle Labs 与 RWA 焦点

So let's talk a little more about Chronicle Labs in particular, because one of the things I found really interesting about your company is how you really focus on RWAs now, and in particular, making sure that when someone buys a tokenized asset, they can be sure that the underlying collateral is actually there — maybe at the DTCC — or just it's not a token that's minted out of thin air. So can you talk a little bit about what you guys do and the problem that you're trying to solve?

Nicholas: Sure. So I don't think it's actually — iterating and expanding into tokenized assets is really all that surprising or different from what we previously did. The role of an Oracle is to preserve the integrity of DeFi.

And when DeFi was just using kind of crypto-native assets — like a Bitcoin or an ETH — that was actually relatively simple, because the protocol was holding on to the actual asset. And so it just needed to know how to price that asset.

代币化资产带来的机会与风险

With the expansion of DeFi into tokenized assets, there is an incredible opportunity here to: 1. Not just expand DeFi out of its very self-contained scope, but 2. Open it up to the entire ecosystem — a catalog of financial assets that are out there.

This is incredibly attractive for everyone involved. From an asset manager's perspective: "I want to give my clients more utility with their assets. So if my client can lend this asset to earn a yield, now I can provide more utility to my clients." I think if you're looking at a BlackRock or Fidelity, that's very, very attractive to them.

And from a DeFi perspective, we're about to take this from a $200 billion market to potentially a $100 trillion market.

However, there is a risk. And it's that tokenized assets are not crypto-native. Regardless of whether they are what you would call a wrapper, or whether they are issued actually on-chain but the underlying assets are sitting somewhere off-chain — that presents a whole new problem for DeFi to deal with.

And so it's actually imperative that Oracles are keeping up, and are ensuring that DeFi, through its integration of these tokenized assets — which I absolutely believe is necessary and critical — that DeFi protocols can maintain their integrity and maintain this anti-fragile property.

Like you mentioned earlier, it's amazing that Maker has survived — not just survived, but thrived — for 10 years. And that doesn't just happen through serendipity. That comes through very rigorous analysis of every part of the system and every edge case that you can find yourself with, and really mapping out how the protocol should respond in every single scenario.

As soon as you open Pandora's box with tokenized assets, that problem becomes a lot more complex. And so as an Oracle, we're here to simplify that. We're here to say, "Okay, here is this tokenized asset, and we can provide all of the metadata related to all of the context that comes with that tokenized asset," so a lending protocol — like a Morpho, like an Aave, like a Sky — can handle it in a responsible, risk-managed manner.

案例：BlackRock 与 BUIDL

Steve: So let's ground this in just one or two tangible examples. One of your clients is BlackRock, and I believe it's supporting their tokenized treasury fund BUIDL, which is the largest in the world. Talk to me about specifically what you are doing for them. What are the services? Just kind of please walk us through that relationship.

Nicholas: Absolutely. Yeah, so very important relationship for us. I mean, BlackRock is the largest asset manager in the world. And they recognize the need for Oracle infrastructure when it comes to tokenized assets.

So when you have something like BUIDL, from a DeFi protocol's perspective, it doesn't know what a BUIDL token is. Even if you feed it a price, it can't account for all of the other independent variables.

从"第一性原理"重建基金

And so what we're essentially doing is saying, "Okay, we can connect to the back end where all of the assets are actually being held." And we can say, "Okay, we know there is this treasury bill that was bought for this amount, it's going to mature on this date, and it's going to be worth $100 at maturity. And there is this overnight repo to JP Morgan, and there's this other overnight repo to Deutsche Bank or to Royal Bank of Canada."

So we can really rebuild the fund from first principles, from the ground up. Saying, "Okay, here are the actual assets underlying the fund," and now we can recompile in a way the entire fund.

This way we can independently come up with our own NAV and share price, identify any kind of maturity blips. Like — you want to have relatively smooth maturations of a portfolio of bonds and a portfolio of treasury bills. So you don't want to see that 30% of the portfolio is going to mature in June or next August, because it means if the fund needs to do a bunch of redemptions, they could potentially need to sell some of those at a loss.

CLO：更受关注但流动性更差

I think that problem is less pronounced with money market funds like BUIDL, like other money market funds that we support — like Centrifuge's Janus Henderson JTRSY, like Superstate's USTB.

I think it's more pronounced in products that are really popular right now: CLOs. Because as monetary policy rates are dropping, CLOs have higher yield. So those are a little bit more interesting, but they're also a little bit less liquid. So that's kind of where the risk part of the equation comes in.

Chronicle is really able to give you that magnifying glass — full view into the guts of the fund. So a DeFi protocol can basically plug that into their risk management system to handle these assets appropriately.

Oracle 作为"中立对账代理"

Steve: Okay. One more quick question and then we have to take another break. But I just want to drill down a little bit more specifically on what you're providing to BlackRock, because I just want to make sure my listeners understand.

Are you actually pricing the token, or pricing the treasuries that are in the other elements of the money market fund that underpin the tokens? Or is your job really to just verify that what CUSIPs are in there, what the maturity dates are, if there's any liens against them — that kind of stuff? What precisely is your role, just using that one engagement as an example?

And then — sorry, not to make the question more complicated — but it is interesting because things like BUIDL, or I guess maybe Benji as well from Franklin Templeton, etc., they're starting to be used as collateral. And I would imagine that you probably get some questions from primes and other people that are accepting them to make sure that those assets are fully backed. So I'd really just love to get a sense of that.

Nicholas: Yeah, absolutely. So as I kind of alluded to previously, we are starting from first principles. So we don't take anything at face value. We know which assets are in custody and we rebuild the fund from there.

What I mean by rebuilding the fund is, we say: "Okay, we are pricing these T-bills using this methodology. And given all of the treasury bills in the fund, given all of the overnight repos and their expected yield, we are coming up with this NAV. How does that compare to what the fund admin is reporting? How does the yield that's being reported compare and contrast to the yield that we are calculating?"

TradFi 分权制衡的类比

I think the way to think of an Oracle's role in tokenized assets is more of a neutral reconciliation agent. Because in traditional finance, you have all kinds of conflicts of interest. And so there is this separation of roles.

When you have a fund, certain parties — someone will be a fund admin, someone will be a custodian, someone will be a transfer agent — but you usually don't see a lot of overlap in these roles.

Take BNY as an example. BNY is one of the largest custodian banks in the world, is the custodian for BUIDL. But then on a different product that we support — for Securitize, a Securitize stack — BNY is the sub-advisor on that. So they're acting in this fund admin capacity.

DeFi 中的角色分离

And so it's kind of the same in DeFi. If you have a lending protocol and there's a risk curator and there's an Oracle — you don't want the issuer of a tokenized asset to also be the one that is reporting the pricing data into a market. The same way you don't want a risk curator of the market to also act as the Oracle. There needs to be this separation of duties.

The Oracle really nestles in nicely into being able to be this neutral arbiter of: "These are the facts relative to this tokenized asset."

I think it's important that you're not just doing "Simon Says" — like, not all Oracles go about this the right way. It's important that you're not just doing "Simon Says," like the issuer told me this or the asset manager told me this. That you're really going as far back into the chain, down this let's-call-it "factory line" that comprises the tokenized asset, and are really just checking every step in the way. It's okay to report what a fund admin reports, as long as you have reconciled and determined that those numbers are accurate.

类比：GPU 计算指数的利益冲突

Steve: Yeah, it's funny. This reminds me of my interview last week with someone who was a provider of basically an index for GPU computing. I think the first one was really kind of out there. And she told me that she's been getting flooded with inquiries from perp DEX providers that want to make markets. And they asked her if she would be willing to make a market on her index. And she flat out said no, because of the perceived conflict of interest in that.

I mean, not that she would, I guess, try to manipulate the price for the index to go up, but certainly the ability could be there for someone who was perhaps a little less honest. So I understand the importance of what you're saying there.

I've been in crypto for almost 13 years at this point — probably as long as you have, if not maybe a little shorter — but I've been in since 2013. And it seems like two big categories are: money market funds/treasuries that we spoke about, but then also alternative assets — the ones that are not quite as liquid, private credit, etc. — that tokenization could offer a way to reduce some of these illiquidity discounts that they trade with.

I'd love to get your thoughts on that part of the market in particular. Are you doing anything to support them right now, and where do you think that could grow to?

Nicholas: Absolutely. I think we kind of get a frontline seat to seeing where the market is heading, because we see what clients are working on and how we need to adapt our technology to be able to support those use cases.

As you pointed out, I think the shift over the last 12 months as monetary policy rates have kind of steadied is: How can we increase yield? It's no surprise that in DeFi, people love their yield. They love their kind of yield-looping strategies.

CLO 与 Galaxy 的入场

So the race has been to create products that are attractive to that part of the market. And that's really where we've seen CLOs take off.

We saw Centrifuge with Janus Henderson launch JAAA. We saw — what else did we see? — I mean, Hamilton Lane and Acred.

Steve: Exactly.

Nicholas: There was the Apollo Accredix — all of these, which we actually support at Chronicle. And I think even just yesterday, or I think it was like two days ago now, we announced that we were supporting Galaxy's first tokenized CLO.

Which I think is quite interesting because — Galaxy, if they're getting into tokenization, they're going to be a really huge, huge player. They have the network and they sit kind of perfectly nestled in between DeFi and on the TradFi side. So I think they're going to do really well.

The product that we support there — Galaxy is either directly or indirectly letting people borrow against their crypto: against Bitcoin, against ETH, SOL, XRP, whatever it is. And so all that we are doing is: we are getting the loan book from Galaxy. And then we're going to their custodian, we're going to Anchorage, and we're checking: "Okay, is there actually all this BTC, all this SOL, all this XRP, all this ETH in custody? Can we trace every satoshi that is claimed to be as collateral on these books?" We identify the liabilities.

And now we can say: "Okay, this CLO is, I don't know, 200% collateralized. And it's yielding 7%." So it's giving pretty good yield.

Chronicle 的双重价值：透明度 + DeFi 集成

Now, one — anyone who wants to potentially buy this Galaxy CLO can look into the guts and know that someone independent like Chronicle has validated all of this. But two, now someone like Morpho — you can have a vault and you can create a looping strategy on this. So you can juice your 7% yield into 14% or 21%, what have you.

That's really what Chronicle enables: 1. Transparency that gives the confidence in these assets 2. The ability for DeFi to integrate them

In terms of what's coming up: we're seeing, for example, REITs — real estate investment trusts. There's a couple of those coming out, so we're excited about those. I think you may have seen Centrifuge just announced with New York Life that they're going to be releasing a new fund as well.

代币化股票的机会

I think equities right now are really hot. And I think it'll take a while to get there. Like you were alluding to before, there's like all these promises and some of them have panned out and some of them haven't. I don't think it's that it doesn't pan out — I think it's more just it needs more time than anyone thinks it does. But the promise is huge.

If you have some stock, if you have Tesla stock and you want to go borrow against your Tesla stock, your broker is going to charge you very high single digits, low double digits. If you're going to lend these things with an LTV of like 30% or something, DeFi will offer you mid to high single digits on this stuff — like all day.

If you look at, what do you get for lending USDC? You're getting 2.7%. You're literally getting less than T-bills — 2.7%.

So there's really an opportunity for DeFi to be the mechanism. One of the flywheels for tokenized equities taking off is just going to be this ability to get leverage against your equities at much cheaper rates than you can in TradFi with your broker.

SpaceX IPO 争议与代币化透明度

Steve: Yeah, one thing I was actually kind of really dying to ask you: I want to get your thoughts on some of the, I guess, controversy that surrounded, for instance, the SpaceX IPO and some of the markets that were spun up where people were able to kind of bet on the price of some of these pre-IPO shares or private market shares.
