I actually wrote a story when I was at Unchained on Acred in particular. I noticed I think a mistake in some of the marketing material where it's an interval fund — which means you're not supposed to be able to get out your money. You can only get out your money on a quarterly basis, and they can cap the redemptions. And I think there were some discrepancies inadvertently on the marketing surrounding that.

But it just shows the danger of these types of products when people are just thinking about dollar signs. And I'd love to just get your thoughts on: what are some of the lessons learned for your business from that one exploit in particular?

桥就是 Oracle

Nicholas: I mean, I think it's very simple. I think what people fail to recognize is that all of these interop protocols — like a LayerZero, like a Chainlink CCIP — that's an Oracle.

Anytime you use cross-chain communication, anytime you're doing bridging — bridging a token from one chain to another chain — these are all Oracles, just white-labeled with a different name like "cross-chain" or "bridge."

So it really comes down to: the LayerZero Oracle was compromised, and the DeFi protocol was insufficiently risk-managed to deal with that.

It doesn't really call into question, "Oh, Oracles are bad." No, you need Oracles. But you need to be very careful about which Oracle you choose. And when you're deciding, "Oh, well, this one costs a little bit more, I'm going to go with the cheaper one" — you need to understand the choice that you're making, that you are directly trading security. Security of your users, of their funds, of the existential risk of your protocol or your company — to save a few bucks.

Lindy 效应与十年零事故

I think the bad players get weeded out over time. And you see players like Chainlink, like Chronicle, that have the Lindy. We've gone — I think not quite 10 years. I think we launched the first Oracles on Ethereum in May of 2017. So come May next year, we'll be celebrating 10 years on-chain without a single Oracle hack.

And that's what you don't see. That's in the marketing material. It's what you don't see. Because you don't luck your way into 10 years without an incident. That comes from a culture of just extreme, delusional, psychotic focus on security and durability.

跨链风险管理与断路器

Steve: How do you — I'm curious, and forgive me, I may not just be familiar enough with your business model — but how do you account for something like that? Like a token that, for instance, a BUIDL token — I know I think it's based on Ethereum, but I don't know if it's available on other chains or not. But how do you make sure that if certain tokens need to be able to move cross-chain, or even across L2s, for instance — what would you do if somehow somebody was able to trick LayerZero or one of those connectors to mint unbacked tokens? How would you handle something like that for one of your clients?

Nicholas: So on the lending protocol side — I'll just use Aave as an example here, because we're talking about the KelpDAO incident, but this goes for any lending protocol — they need to have circuit breaker safeguards in place. And whether they are getting that data on their own or whether they are getting it through an Oracle, they need to have those risk systems in place.

KelpDAO 事件的技术复盘

So in the KelpDAO incident, an attacker was able to convince the bridge — with this false message — to unlock the locked KelpDAO on Ethereum, that shouldn't have been unlocked. Because the corresponding Kelp on the source chain was not actually burned to do this.

From a risk management perspective, you should be checking the supply on every single chain that a token can be on, and summating those and ensuring that there is no mismatch between the amount that is floating around versus what the token — even just asking the token contract, "How many RSEs exist?" That's not enough.

And if you notice any wonkiness, what do you do? You immediately freeze the market. You do not allow anyone to have the protocol take on any more risk by taking on more debt against these assets. Nor do you allow anyone to repay and take stuff out until this whole mess is sorted. You just pause.

代币化资产将放大复杂度

And this has nothing to do with tokenized assets already. This is just a slightly more complex, let's call it, DeFi asset. And we're already seeing lenders struggle to keep up with the complexity of that, when the risk management solutions are quite simple.

So when it comes to tokenized assets, the complexity of this just becomes exponential. So yes, it's on the Oracles to develop the ability to get all of the required data, all of this metadata, all of this context, compile it so it can plug into the risk management systems of these lending protocols.

But the onus is on the lending protocols to take that risk management seriously, and to create — not like a phone call, or there's an alert that goes off or something. That's already too late. It needs to be real-time, automated, and triggered by data that is provably correct.

未来展望：代币化资产将成为主战场

Steve: What do you see as the biggest areas of growth for your business in the next, say, five to ten years?

Nicholas: I mean, it's hands down tokenized assets. Like — let me paint you a picture.

So today, Chronicle secures just under $15 billion across DeFi and tokenized assets. So we're not quite at Chainlink level, but we're five times larger than say a Pyth or a Redstone. It's not even close.

And within that business, about $10 billion of it is pure DeFi and $5 billion of that is tokenized assets. Those $5 billion in tokenized assets, we have done that over the last 18 months. Like, we didn't release our tokenized asset Oracle till January of '25.

So that is easily, by far, the fastest growing part of our business. And I expect that by the end of the year, frankly, the tokenized asset business will be bigger than our pure DeFi business.

代币化股票的 S 曲线

On that front, to me, it has to be equities. There are problems that you have with funds that you don't have with equities, and vice versa. And I think there's like a kind of step function here. You're not going to see very gradual, incremental growth. I think you're just going to see huge S-curves, localized S-curves, play out.

赎回难题：进得容易出得难

Let me give you an example. On the money market fund side, these have existed for a while now. People — we know people in DeFi love to do their little leverage loops and stuff.

One of the problems, not just with money market funds but with these CLOs and other types of funds, is the redemption ability. You touched on this earlier with the Acred product — like, okay, you can only redeem on a quarterly basis.

So there's kind of a problem here where you can get into the position: if you want to build a leveraged position, you can enter it very easily. Lock up your Acred, lock up your JAAA, lock up your BUIDL, your USTBs. You borrow against them. Great. And then you just go and you buy more of that initial asset. So you can enter a position extremely efficiently.

The problem is when you want to unwind this whole thing. In DeFi, we have a flash loan. You take out a flash loan, you repay your whole debt, you can now withdraw your entire collateral, and now you can sell your collateral on a DEX, and you use the proceeds from selling your collateral to repay the flash loan. That's an amazing superpower that DeFi has.

However, now when it comes to — if the underlying asset is not something that you can just sell on a DEX — there's not millions of dollars of liquidity for, I don't know, some private credit product or for some CLO on a DEX. Well, what do you do?

Someone needs to essentially offer a redemption facility, for a fee, and essentially take on directionally that underwriting risk, or find ways to hedge that on the other side. That's still been like a really big piece that's missing.

Grove Basin：赎回原语的解锁

But just recently, for example, we saw Grove — shout out, a great group of guys. They just did their TGE a couple of days ago. So cool. They launched a new product called Grove Basin.

And essentially, for right now, they're just supporting money market funds like BUIDL, like Centrifuge Janus Henderson JTRSY. But they'll now basically — they have a billion-dollar credit line from Sky to just, in-kind, allow you to redeem your JTRSY or your BUIDL for USDC.

So now you can exit these positions extremely efficiently, and not having to go to the issuer and do stuff, you know, like paperwork stuff or do stuff off-chain. It's all just in a transaction, right? Completely composable, one transaction. DeFi at work.

So that's — it takes new utilities like that that just unlock the ecosystem. It's like a bathtub filling with water. There's only a certain — if the bathtub is this size, there's only a certain amount of water that can fit into it. As soon as we open up these new primitives, now all of a sudden the bathtub is much bigger, and it'll fill up with water like immediately. I mean, greed is ever-present. If there's money to be made, money will find a way.

原生链上发行 vs. 代币化封装

Steve: Okay, we're just about at time, but I have one more quick question I want to ask you. Tokenized assets are clearly a growth area, but we are starting to see some issuances directly on-chain without an underlying, like, more traditional asset backing it. I'm just curious your thoughts on that, and how you might account for it moving forward.

Nicholas: So — actually, it's kind of funny because I get this question a lot. Like, it's supposed to be some kind of gotcha.

Steve: I mean, it's not meant to be a gotcha. It's just it's more just sometimes, like, for instance —

Nicholas: Yeah, no, no, I understand.

Steve: Yeah, so why have something that's backed by something where something could just be issued directly?

链上发行不等于底层上链

Nicholas: So one thing I want to say, for example, is: you can have on-chain issuance where the assets are still custodied off-chain. An example of this is the new BNY / Baillie Gifford product that was just announced recently, where it's issued natively on-chain. But the underlying loans in the fund are still off-chain. And so those will still require what I kind of consider the very standardized Chronicle Proof of Asset Oracle.

Now, you mentioned specifically funds that are issued on-chain where the components of the fund are also on-chain. For instance, JP Morgan just announced they're going to issue a money market fund on-chain. Yeah, but where are the underlying T-bills for that money market fund? Are they off-chain or are they on-chain? I would tell you they're going to be off-chain.

Can't wait for the day when Treasury starts issuing bonds on-chain. That would be awesome.

递归问题：即使底层上链，仍需 Proof of Asset

But let's take this example further. Let's assume that we start tokenizing individual T-bills. That's really cool. Now we can actually create a natively issued, on-chain money market fund where even the underlying T-bills are on-chain.

But you still now have the problem of: "Okay, well, I know that the money market fund is composed of these on-chain T-bills. But how do I know that these on-chain T-bills are actually legit?"

And so, again, it just becomes the recursive problem of: I need to go back and now I need to have Proof of Asset for the underlying T-bills all over again.

Steve: Let me find that source of truth, I guess, one way or another.

Nicholas: Exactly.

Steve: Okay, alright, well we need to end it there. So yeah, Nicholas, thanks so much for joining. We'll have to have you back again.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、Oracle 的"沉默恐惧"：不是报错，而是失语
Nicholas 顺口提到的一个观察值得反复咀嚼——**"Oracle 最糟的不是报错价，而是彻底停止报价"**。这颠覆了大多数人对系统风险的直觉。我们习惯把"错误"当成最大威胁，但在 DeFi 里，**"沉默"比"谎言"更致命**：谎言至少给了协议一个可以反应的信号（哪怕是错的），而沉默让整个偿付能力判断陷入形而上学的悬置——你不知道自己是不是破产了。
这其实和临床医学里的一个悖论惊人相似：ICU 中真正致命的往往不是"仪器报警"，而是"仪器脱线"。心电监护仪响了，护士会冲过来；监护仪没信号，可能只是"设备故障"，被暂时忽略。**在高风险系统中，"无信号"是最昂贵的信号，因为它伪装成常态。**
## 二、跨领域连接：Oracle 其实是一种"分权制衡"，而非"数据管道"
Nicholas 用了一个非常关键但容易被忽略的比喻——Oracle 应该是 **"neutral reconciliation agent"（中立对账代理）**。他把它类比为传统金融里 fund admin / custodian / transf

item.title: [Business of Sport] Norway’s Talent Machine Unpacked, Silverstone’s Record Weekend, & NBA Europe’s Billion Dollar Bids
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

三个隐藏的系统性真相

1. 挪威模式的反直觉："延迟专业化"是一种高级形式的风险管理

表面上看，挪威把足球职业化推迟到 15-16 岁是"人文关怀"，但这其实是一种期权定价思维在体育人才培养中的应用。

传统精英俱乐部体系（英格兰、苏格兰、南美）本质上是在 9 岁做投资决策——他们必须在信息极度不完整时"押注"哪个孩子会成才。这就像风险投资在 Pre-Seed 阶段就要 All-in，注定错误率极高。而挪威系统实际上是把决策点推迟到"信号-噪声比"最高的时刻（青春期发育之后），这时候每一份投资的边际回报率呈指数级上升。

哈兰德是最好的证据——他早期身材瘦小，在任何 9 岁筛选制的国家都会被淘汰。挪威系统"保留了他的期权"，直到他自己行使了它。这跟金融里的"美式期权 vs 欧式期权"是同一逻辑：保留决策权到最后一刻，永远比提前锁定更有价值。

2. 一个跨领域的意外连接：特朗普事件与 Silverstone 的 BRDC 揭示了同一个"治理悖论"

这两个故事看似毫不相关，但在系统层面上讲的是同一件事——"程序正当性"作为一种稀缺资产的定价。

  • FIFA 允许特朗普通话后启动"独立小组"程序，即便小组做出的是"正确"决定，程序正当性也已经归零。因为感知即现实（perception is reality），这是体育治理最脆弱的资产。
  • 反过来看 BRDC——一个由 850 位"血统纯正"的赛车贵族组成的、看似封建的私人俱乐部，2015 年濒临破产。它没有 DEI，没有政府背书，但它有跨越百年的程序正当性储备。正是这份"我们不是为了赚钱而办赛"的可信度，让它在 Liberty Media 接手后能够重新议价，并从 5 万英镑的破产边缘走到 500 亿英镑估值。

洞见：现代体育治理中，"程序信誉"是一种可以被瞬间摧毁却需要几十年重建的资产类别。Infantino 一通电话烧掉的信誉，可能比整届世界杯的商业收入还要贵。

3. 最令人意外的观察：NBA 欧洲和挪威人才工厂在做完全相反的赌注——而两者可能同时错

NBA 欧洲的核心逻辑是：IP 空投——用 10 亿美元的特许经营费在伦敦、巴黎凭空造出一个篮球品牌，假设"美国式的球迷经济学"会跟着 IP 一起漂移过来。

挪威模式的核心逻辑恰好相反：IP 培育——从 5 岁开始用 20 年时间把整个国家的"运动身体"变成人才的原生土壤。

有意思的是，这两个模型都在挑战同一个残酷现实：在体育产业里，"人才"和"关注度"其实是同一种商品的两个面。你不能只买其中一面。

  • NBA 想只买"关注度"（用特许权费吸引资本、用美式娱乐化包装），却发现欧洲的顶级球员和俱乐部（Barcelona、Real Madrid、Fenerbahçe）根本不搭理。
  • 反过来，Euro League 只有"人才"（这些俱乐部有百年历史），却做不出商业化包装，永远无法从纯赛事升级为文化产品。

深层洞察：这与网飞早期做原创内容 vs 迪士尼买 IP 的对抗是同构的。真正赢家往往是既有原生人才又有跨界娱乐化能力的混合模型——这也许解释了为什么足球（既有本地俱乐部的百年根系，又有 FIFA 的全球化叙事）能吃下体育产业的最大份额，而其他体育联盟无论怎么砸钱都只能做局部市场之王。

啊哈时刻：网球其实也困在同一个陷阱里——它有全球顶级人才（Sinner、Alcaraz），但没有本地俱乐部根系（大满贯是候鸟型 IP，不是常驻文化）。所以你会看到本期播客里出现的"看似矛盾"的现象：网球是全球观看量前三的运动，却挤不进商业价值前十。根系决定天花板。

📝 转录文本

开场：热点抢先看

He started off by going, you know, "I know sports." About a minute and a half later said, "I didn't know what a red card is." Whatever happens after that, independent panels or not, whether this is the right call or not, it doesn't really matter. When Donald Trump admits he's made that call, the integrity of any decision made disappears.

This is the first time that one of the major American sports has made a serious effort to establish a European league. And it makes sense, right? Because basketball's got much stronger roots in Europe than American football and baseball have.

Do you know how many people turned up at Silverstone over the weekend? 564,000 people. By 2015, they're on the verge of bankruptcy. I mean, it's the most astonishing sports business turnaround.

At 95% rate of Norwegians playing a team sport by the time they're 25. That gives Norway a really broad pool of athletes. There's a very high chance that in a different ecosystem, Erling Haaland would have fallen through the cracks where physically a coach will assess them and say, "You're not quite up to it."

Substack 上线预告

But first, big announcement alert. We are launching on Substack. The first few months have been awesome. Now, we want to do more with you, get to know you better, and bring you another layer of content and conversations. We're talking bonus segments, Q&As, written pieces, events, votes, all launching officially next Friday. We'll be posting exclusives from tomorrow to give you a sneak peek. So, click on the link in the description and come join us for real.

温布尔登近况与英式着装闲聊

Good morning, Charlie. Very early morning, Charlie, this morning. So, I'm sorry to drag you out of bed into the London studio.

I wouldn't say very early morning, Charlie. Both of us have young daughters. Um, 9:00 is absolutely absolutely fine. And also on a day which is going to be as hot as today means that we can get in here before the tube becomes too sweaty. You had a few jibes the other day about me not wearing linen, which I didn't really sort of get, but today I'm wearing more linen than a Brideshead Revisited rehash.

I am muk linen today. So I am — off. I'm very lucky to be off to Wimbledon after this.

Yeah.

Breakdown morning linen for Wimbledon at lunch. However, I obviously have to be wearing a shirt, right, for the lovely part of Wimbledon. I'm going to have literally that shirt. So we could have been sitting here identical matchy matchy.

Yeah, I've been invited by a business of sport alumni and an absolute legend who I used to watch at Stamford Bridge when I was about six or seven years old. It's not John Terry. It's not John Terry.

It's Mr. Graeme Le Saux.

Graeme Le Saux. He's a great guy.

He's a lovely guy. The sophisticated footballer. Huge tennis fan. Famously reads the Guardian, which we won't hold against him. Well, look, he reads. He reads.

Anyhow, off to Wimbledon. What have you made of Wimbledon so far?

女子网球缺的是"人格魅力"，不是天赋

So, we said last week that we thought that it needed a bit of a spicing up, a bit of a kick. People came back going, "Oh, but no, look at the women's game. It's been stocked with talent." I think maybe we were misunderstood. I don't think it was really a question of talent. I think some of the games and matches and players are exceptional. What we were really talking about is the feeling and the atmosphere that comes with the biggest profile players and the personalities that we've had in the past that add to that excitement.

And if you look at those top women's players, Sabalenka, I think has done a very good job on building a kind of brand and a personality beyond her game. But without being too harsh, your number two seed was Elena Rybakina. Don't know a huge amount about — very very quiet. Iga Świątek, exactly the same. Jessica Pegula, who's the four seed, again quite similar. So your top players, while being excellent, exceptional tennis players, don't have that kind of personality factor.

And we've already discussed the Jannik Sinner challenge, which is the most incredible player, but also suffers from a little bit of the —

Yeah, he wouldn't have charisma as his top trump. Yes. Um, you know, sort of 10 rating, but is nonetheless an outstanding tennis player.

英国男单：Arthur Fery 的意外崛起

So, long and short of it, we've — I think firstly from Britain, England — we said that we were short a player or two to follow in this tournament. And Arthur Fery seems to have picked up.

I don't think that anyone — I mean, I'm not a tennis cognoscente. I just like watching it. So, perhaps there is somebody somewhere out there in the ether who was going, "I tell you what, you need to keep an eye on this Arthur Fery bloke." But I certainly didn't hear it. He was a long long way down. Even the British pecking order — and the British tennis pecking order is a long long way down the international pecking order. So Arthur Fery, who I assume was given a wild card — he was given a wild card — and he's now into the quarterfinals.

Into the quarterfinals, which is an astonishing achievement. We do have to enter a little asterisk, Charlie, which is: he's French. Arthur Fery — it's a wonderful name. He sounds like the kind of bloke who would have been playing cricket for Yorkshire in the 1930s. "Arthur Fery came out the mine and bowl quick. He bowled quick, I tell you." But actually, as it turns out, he's not only French, but he's the son of a hedge fund boss, which is brilliant.

I'm grateful to have him here.

Yeah, we're grateful to have him here.

Well, I am. Yes, absolutely. Go Arthur. Absolutely. Brilliant.

Go Arthur. Let's be quite clear. To be fair, he's grown up in England, so he is English, naturalized English, but he is basically French.

Wimbledon 的钱到底流向哪里？

But yes, this goes back to the conversations we've been having about Wimbledon and prize money etc. Because of course whatever the top tennis players want in terms of how much money they want Wimbledon to give them — at the moment that money, the vast majority of it, does go to enabling people throughout Britain to play tennis. That's where that 40–50 million quid a year goes — to the LTA, who then spreads it throughout the country.

And leaving aside young Arthur, most of the other British players actually come from reasonably humble backgrounds and have been enabled through LTA schemes. So, if we give all that money to the top players in prize money as they would all like, then there's not as much money to open up access to everybody else.

Now the criticism that would legitimately be made is: okay, well that's really nice for Britain because you happen to have a Grand Slam in your country, but is that really opening up access to the rest of the world, and why should I, a Belarusian tennis player, care about access in Britain? So there are arguments to be had around it.

Incidentally, I did check in — I sent you a couple of notes over the weekend over this tennis player strike, i.e. "we're only going to spend 15 minutes of time with the media. That'll show you." But even that is not being broadly observed. I mean, it's about 50% of the players who have agreed to only spend 15 minutes of their time with the media. And I wouldn't particularly say that —

I mean, do we want more than 15 minutes?

Oh, really?

15 minutes is fine. In fact, I would almost pay a bit more money. I'd pay a bit more money to get it cut back to 10. Most of these interviews I do with the media are so astonishingly banal. You only see one-minute clips on the BBC really anyway. So, I mean, that's what it's all boiled down to. Yeah, again, if you're going to strike, strike. If you've got the points to make, strike on that.

That final piece you just made there was really interesting. The show that we did with Craig Tiley, which will come out today — that was a key point we were talking about for the broadening of participation. And it was a key point you made of tennis being a top three viewership sport in the world, and not in the top 10 commercial sports in the world. So, they are not making enough money to be able to facilitate the broader participation.

Ecosystem very focused on Grand Slam places, very focused on the areas that already have established tennis backgrounds, but they need to spread far more money — and that just comes from the sport really not managing itself well enough and therefore not making enough money.

That was a point actually that Sampras made a comment on the weekend. He said this whole thing over squeezing the Grand Slams for more money is absolute rubbish. He said what the focus has to be on is the ATP Tour and the WTA Tour. That's where the problem is, not the Grand Slams. The Grand Slams are paying vast amounts of prize money. The issue is in all the other events. That's where the real challenge comes.

Yeah. So, it's good. But yeah, I always love Wimbledon regardless. Even if it seems flat at the beginning, everyone, you know, if you enjoy your tennis, everyone generally gets into it.

世界杯：特朗普致电 FIFA 事件

There's been some big news in the World Cup, Charlie. Not on the football pitch —

Well, there has been big news on the football pitch, but we may come to that a little bit subsequently. But I think what you're going to refer to, Charlie, is big news off the pitch.

Big news off the pitch. I don't want us to go all talk sporty as we occasionally get accused of if we show our real views on England. What a game though. Wow.

Okay. So, yes, we'll get that out. That was fantastic to watch. I'm very excited because obviously, Charlie, this is my derby. This is the Stebbings derby. English England versus the ski jumpers. Sorry, not even the ski jumpers — the people who were not deemed suitable for ski jumping probably for physiological reasons and who therefore converted into being footballers. Exactly.

So, the Stebbings derby. Any conflicted loyalties?

No. You're England all in.

I'm England all in.

I said, as we'll cover shortly, you know, I'm Norway when it suits me, and increasingly so in sport, it suits me to be Norwegian quite a lot because they're increasingly good across various elements of the sporting world. But they were exceptional against Brazil. Brazil have been pretty average throughout the whole tournament. Again, I don't think that takes a genius to figure that out, but the way they closed out that last quarter — and it is interesting, isn't it? Because I do feel strategically with these hydration breaks it does feel like quarters. That last quarter they were awesome. So it'll be an interesting one to see how England approach it.

But it's funny when these types of sort of massive amounts of hoopla surround a particular sporting team or an individual player, it's quite rare you get a very high level of performance out the back end of that. So, moving on to another thing that we need to catch up on, which is NBA Europe, Charlie. This causing a few ructions with the pre-existing and quite significant Euroleague Basketball, which is where professional European basketball has been at for forever and a day, including with some really big franchises.

So, what's the latest?

招标进展：伦敦、巴黎估值破 10 亿美元

The latest is that the NBA has been running a bidding process for the franchises. There are going to be 12 franchises to kick off the league. And that bidding process closed on June the 30th. And they fielded, I think they said, over 20 serious bids, some of which topped a billion dollars for the biggest cities. And those were assumed to be Paris and London, the most valuable. Some others would go down to about 500 million. There was suggestions from those trying to spin negatively that a few of the smaller ones hadn't met the minimum 500 that they were hoping for. But either way, I think you could safely say that the NBA is pretty delighted with the numbers that have been put on the table for the franchises so far.

NBA 与 EuroLeague 的博弈

The interesting story of course with this has always been the relationship of the NBA and the Euroleague, as you suggested, and how this would actually ever come into fruition. Because you've got the Euroleague on one side basically threatening all kinds of challenges to this. You've got the NBA basically going, "We don't care and we'll find our way through this regardless."

The NBA have been suggesting that for teams to get out of the Euroleague and into the NBA system, there's break clauses in contracts of about 10 million euros. I think reportedly the Euroleague have been saying it's more like a hundred million euros. You've got football clubs in Euroleague who have been a huge part of this. So for example, Barcelona, Real Madrid, you got Turkish clubs — Fenerbahçe I think — and Beşiktaş, Olympiacos in Greece — huge. There's a real kind of thread between —

So just to make that absolutely clear, there are some major European football clubs who also have basketball teams, who have been playing in Euroleague, who are very significant size. And effectively the NBA has been trying to tempt across various of those clubs — not all, but various of those clubs — into NBA Europe, so they sort of have some pre-existing fan bases effectively coming across to start the whole league.

But of course if you've got a billion valuation for, let's just say, a Madrid franchise, and you're Real Madrid, how are you running the economics of that as the NBA? Because Real Madrid are not in a position, nor are its socios, of sanctioning a 1 billion entry into a league. And Real Madrid, Barcelona, a few of the others — there's been a pretty strong suggestion that they believe that the NBA needs them far more than they need it.

NBA 的让步：3 年盈亏平衡承诺

And by the way, Charlie, in the interim period between when we first discussed this back in January and now, the NBA and Adam Silver, the commissioner of the NBA, have had to make some significant concessions to make these franchises attractive. Chief of which has been the amount of money which the NBA subsidizes NBA Europe with.

The original concept was — if they could have pulled it off, this would have been great — was first of all, we're going to charge entities huge amounts of money to have a franchise, and then effectively they are then going to have to also bear the cost of the league as a whole. And it became clear that wasn't really going to fly. So what happened was, the NBA said, "Right, of the franchise fees that get paid, we are going to reinvest a significant chunk of that in effectively frontloading the costs of the league for a substantial period of time until it gets itself fully up and running."

Which part of this pitch was that the franchises will break even in three years.

Yeah. So coming back to why people always want to invest in sport, the importance of that investable environment is: you're not going to lose bucket loads of money if you invest in it. Now, if you're already spending a billion and you're having to pump in a billion over the next 3 years — let's say, or 5 years — you're very happy to hear that there's a period in the near future that won't require you to do that.

Euroleague 的反击与结构性挑战

However, the Euroleague is kicking hard. It's fighting hard. It's trying to sign its current teams to 10-year deals. Real Madrid actually committed the day before the close of the bids to the Euroleague. So, they've actually gone and put their colors on the mast of the Euroleague. You're back in the situation of very high franchise fees being paid for assets that don't exist yet, right off the back of the success of something that works incredibly well in — as we know — a very difficult environment to replicate in Europe, i.e. the American sports ecosystem.

Now, are we saying that it wouldn't work? Absolutely not. Basketball is a huge sport in Europe. But what we have discussed previously at length is the difference between establishing brand new IP and assuming that audience and fandom and passion that is associated with European sport follows, versus could we look at the Euroleague and build something out of that — in a similar way that we suggested LIV Golf and the PIF should have done with the European Tour. Complexities in that. And by the state of the Euroleague's comms, I'm sure they would have told the NBA exactly where to stick it if that had been the communications anyway.

But this may be great. There's a long way to go, I think, in making it a reality in the ways the NBA would perfectly like. There'll probably have to be a few more movements and concessions over the next few months because it's meant to start next year.

Yeah. I mean, at the moment, it looks like there's going to end up being two leagues.

Yeah. And that's really not beneficial because in terms of narrative that then becomes quite a difficult thing to explain to the broader sporting public, which is really the point of this. Okay.

So if you think about basketball in Europe — professional basketball in Europe — the Euroleague is already having an established audience in certain places that has been built up over decades and decades and decades. And that's great. These are what might be called the basketball fanatics. These are the people who really really care about basketball.

Now the question is, for the new franchises to work, it's not going to be enough simply to transfer across some of the professional basketball fanatics. They're going to need to penetrate the broader audience. So, if you think about that London franchise or the Paris franchise, whatever it might be, they're going to need to fill a big arena. And they're going to need to be able to draw in a much broader cross-section of the sports watching community than those people who are already established, passionate basketball watchers — otherwise why bother, frankly, overall.

So I think it's going to be really interesting to watch. This is the first time that one of the major American sports has made a serious effort to establish a European league. A serious one. A really serious effort. Massively heavily backed, very ambitious, right? "We're going to really, really go for it." And it makes sense, right? Because basketball's got much stronger roots in Europe than American football and baseball have. So, of the three, this is the right one to be giving it a real go.

And I don't want this to be us being critical. I think this is having a look at it and trying to pull out where it is right now and where some of those challenges will be. But as you say, at the heart of it, basketball is a big sport in Europe.

It is a big sport in Europe. Some of the biggest attendances in indoor arenas, great viewership as we — I mean, it's like 500 million, wasn't it, across Europe on broadcast. So that is absolutely there.

But just as a lot of other audiences have been there for sports that have also spun off new leagues — again, it just comes back to the point of a break even in 3 years will also have assumptions of X amount of attendances and participations from a fan base that may not have actually been converted to a London franchise or a Paris franchise. So, if you don't break even in 3 years, the NBA is going to have to subsidize some of that to ensure that those franchise owners don't come back and go, "Well, that was a key part of our investment thesis."

What are we going to move on to now, Charlie?

Charlie, we are going to go back to my second homeland. Because while Norway have been tearing it up, should we say, at the World Cup, I think there's a more interesting story to really pull out that we've actually touched on a tiny bit in the past when we were going through Winter Olympic sports — but we didn't really develop. And now you've seen the development of Norway in a global sport. There's a real story here around how Norway are managing to produce talent that is competing on a global stage. And we've seen it across various sports over the last 10 to 15 years. And now we're starting to really see it in football.

对比设定：苏格兰 vs 挪威

So, what we're seeing here — I want to take this story and use it as a comparison point against Scotland, right? Scotland is, you know, the land across the water from Vikingland. And there have been all sorts of treaties in the past to do with the Shetlands and all this type of stuff. So you've got two countries which are both geographically quite contiguous and also they're about the same size of country as well in terms of population, in terms of category of country. So we're not talking about one country that's 50 million and another one that's 5 million. We're talking about — what's the population, in a way, Charlie?

5.5 both, pretty much 5.5.

Same size of country, similar kind of climate for the most part. Probably you'd say Scotland has got the advantage in some ways of having some very large established football clubs who therefore would have the finances to finance proper youth development systems and all that kind of stuff. And they've got more footballing history as well. And they've also got extreme proximity to England, which means that their players growing up in the Scottish system can translate into the English system very very easily — both literally physically but also from a visa/passport point of view. So there's a whole lot of reasons why Scotland should be advantaged compared to Norway. And yet they're not.

So what have Norway been doing that Scotland have not?

挪威哲学：让运动"好玩"到 12 岁

So let me lay out the Norway case and give you the explanation for how they've actually done this, and you can then tie that into how we could look at Scotland. So I just want to read this out at the beginning because this is going to build the fundamental structure of really how Norway have done it.

By the age of 25, 93% of Norway's population has been involved in some kind of organized sports. Conversely, the American Academy of Pediatrics reported in 2024 that 70% of youth athletes drop out of organized sports by the age of 13. Injury and burnout are the principal reasons. Discontinuation of sports during childhood plays a role in more than 75% of adolescents in the US who fail to meet physical activity recommendations.

So the reason I read that out is because it's at the heart of Norway's thesis on sport, which is: sport should be fun and it should be built around enjoyment. So Norway basically have this philosophy — up to 12 years old, you will only play in a kind of local club environment. You will try lots of different sports. It will not be boring in its tactical sessions if you're playing football. It will be far more about how you enjoy and engage in the sport. So by the time you get to 12, when you're ready and starting to physically develop, you will hopefully be coming to that age going, "I love this."

"I love it. I haven't been overexposed. I've got the exposure that I needed, but I haven't been overexposed. By the time I'm 12, I'm ready to push on." Not "I'm just feeling forced into doing this and I want to push back."

基础设施与"精英学院" Landslaget

That system has basically allowed them to go: "Right, you enjoy yourself, have a great time, then at the right time we'll start looking at how to develop you."

They've done that in a number of ways. Firstly, they provided facilities. So, tie this into Scotland as well — this will be a key point. They've got a real challenge with the weather, of course. Norway is incredibly cold and all the pitches freeze over for a huge part of the year. So, what did they do? They invested heavily in the infrastructure. Over 60% of pitches were artificial by 2015. Most of them are free to play on. So, there is no barrier.

They basically said, sport — and it's a very egalitarian country, Norway. There's very much this equal belief of access and rights. So, why would we change that with our sporting philosophy? So, we're not going to keep sports access for those that can afford it. It's going to be for everyone.

What they then have done on top of that is created something called the "Landslaget" — the elite academy post-12 that players will then get picked out of their football clubs to then go and play. So the top 10% will be nominated and they will then go and start playing in training camps together, which is centralized to develop the future Norwegian football team. But it comes out of the clubs that have been rearing these players in a very specific way from whatever young age they've started.

This is the track that Erling Haaland has taken. This is the track that Martin Ødegaard has —

Something quite important to ask here. So up until the age of 12 they play in local clubs, not in professional clubs.

They play in local clubs and then at 12 the national system picks them up. Okay. So at this point they're still not in professional football clubs.

No.

Right. So they are not entering a professional football club environment until they're much closer to the point of their professional career.

案例：Haaland 差点被淘汰

So if you look at again — if we go back to that statement at the beginning about age — you know, Erling Haaland of course is a great example, right. Erling Haaland physically is an absolute specimen and that is a huge part of his game. He was actually quite small when he was younger, until he —

That's how he escaped being a ski jumper.

I think it could be.

And I'm the late developer.

I'm delighted that he — he developed. Because he's found — yeah, he was. Or maybe not. I don't —

I was wondering that. Because I was looking at him the other day going, "Surely a ski jump —"

Surely a ski jump.

You can't make any assumptions.

You can't make any assumptions. But you know, there's a very high chance that in a different ecosystem, Erling Haaland would have fallen through the cracks. He would have suffered from the fate that a lot of footballers do, particularly at a young age, where physically a coach will assess them and say, "You're not quite up to it. You're not a striker, you don't have the physique, you know, whatever."

However, in Norway's system, it's perfect because by the time he actually started to show his promise, he had had the physical chance to start developing into the player that he's now become.

16 岁进入职业系统：可视化的路径

At 16, he then goes to Molde. See, now this is the revolutionary bit, right? This is the bit where they are different from everywhere else in Europe and indeed in Latin America, which is that the elite clubs are not being entrusted with developing these players. Okay?

The very local system, and then the national system, is doing it up until the age of 15, at which point the boy is starting to become properly physically developed and more mentally developed and has decided, "I really enjoy this. I love this. This is something I want to do." So effectively they are then put into professional clubs at the age at which a professional career is right in their eyeline. Right? "I'm two years away from making my first team debut. So I'm going to probably play for the under 18 team" — this is probably what they have in the Norwegian professional clubs — "and then I can see the pathway to the first team straight away."

与英格兰模式的对比

Now if you contrast that to the system that we have — before we even go — Scotland has the same system as the rest of Europe effectively, where youth development is entrusted to the professional elite clubs.

So in other words, what happens is: kid starts playing at 5, 6, 7, shows some promise, is the best kid in the local schoolyard, somebody somewhere says to a scout who works on behalf of a local professional football club, "This kid's got some ability." At 9 years old, they're brought into that professional football club and the professional football club will have a look at 80 of them at the same time.

So — sorry, just on there — that a key point of difference there is you've already identified the sport that they're going to be playing.

Absolutely. Now, this is a key weakness of this system, right? Which is effectively then from the age of 9, they then only play football.

Yeah.

Pretty much. Now, there's a lot of research that's been done that suggests that that is a leading contributor towards injuries in the medium to long term. Because effectively you are repeating the same movement patterns as your body is developing. And there's quite a lot of interest now at the better academies in: right, we actually need to get our kids in the academies doing other types of sport and moving in other types of ways. First of all to keep them mentally fresh so that they are not just football, football, but also frankly so their bodies are moving in different ways — otherwise we could end up with a lot of injury problems down the track.

小国的战略优势：广度是资产

What gives you a better chance of success? You — if you're a small country, you're 5.5 million people, again versus let's just say the US because it's 350 million — you want to keep as many of those players in the system for as long as possible. You've got a much higher likelihood of doing that.

That's true. If they enjoy it.

So again, like a 95% rate of Norwegians playing a team sport by the time they're 25 — that gives Norway a really broad pool of athletes to then be able to go and build elite professionals across various different areas.

挪威跨项目冠军名录

Johannes Klæbo, the record winter Olympian, who won 11 gold medals — smashing the record of 8 previously in this Winter Olympics. Let's just be clear, he won 6 this Winter Olympics, but 11 in total. He was always going to be a footballer. That was always what he thought. But then as he develops and gets to the certain age where, because he hasn't necessarily specialized or been pressured into one or another — hey, cross country skiing is actually not too bad. And he's bloody good at it. And so there was then a choice that was made purely off his own free will.

Karsten Warholm — the 400 meter hurdles gold medal in Tokyo. Jakob Ingebrigtsen — we were talking about Josh Kerr the other week, a great rival of Josh Kerr in the 1500, and he's 5,000 meter champion as well. Markus Rooth — decathlon. They won the decathlon gold in Paris. So you're getting Olympic medal winners.

You're getting Casper Ruud, the tennis player, been top of tennis for a very long time. Viktor Hovland — won 8 PGA titles, really should be winning a major, Ryder Cups. Ada Hegerberg — won the Ballon d'Or Féminin just a few years ago. They've had players at the top of that game for a long time.

We're just now starting to see this be the fruits of the work that they've been putting in place behind the scenes really start to show the value in the biggest sport of all. And whether it's Haaland, whether it's Ødegaard, whether it's the team — I mean Berg in midfield, again without getting too tactical, but he plays for Bodø/Glimt.

Yeah. I thought — I was just watching the game and he just stood out a mile in the middle of the pitch. He was unbelievable. And you suddenly look — oh, part of the Bodø group. They've got this culture around the team which also underpins it.

I don't want to get too washy but it's a really important point because it comes back to: if you succeed in encouraging that enjoyment, you'll build better people, you'll build better team environments. And a huge part of what they have been able to achieve as a team clearly comes from that collective approach and mentality.

两条可复制的原则

There are just a few things I want to pick up here on the football side, right. Let's just summarize it thus.

First of all: keep as many promising footballers playing football and enjoying playing football for as long as possible. Because you don't quite know at what stage a lot of them are going to develop. And if you leave it to the elite clubs, the elite clubs will start eliminating them at a much too early stage because basically it's costing them money and they have to start making choices. They have to focus on certain players. So that's the first observation.

The second observation I'd make is cross training — which is, they're getting them to do other sports as well at a crucial physical developmental stage.

Now those two things are what I think is underpinning this. And they are two things that other small nations can copy. I think it's harder in a very big nation because that national piece — when you bring them all in at whatever it is, 15 (is it 12 you said? actually between —)

It's 12, but Haaland for example signed for Molde when he was 16.

  1. So he didn't make it into the top-end professional environment until he was at the stage where Wayne Rooney was making his debut.

Exactly. Right. Well, Max Dowman's at 15 running around in Arsenal.

Right. So my point here, tying it back to Scotland. And the reason we're discussing Scotland and Norway is because Scotland had a terrible performance at the World Cup and Norway have had an outstanding performance at the World Cup.

Both hadn't participated for 28 years. Literally had exactly the same — same populations, all that type of stuff.

So if you look first of all on the club side: you've got Celtic and Rangers massively underperforming in Europe, and you've got relatively modest Norwegian clubs like Bodø/Glimt massively outperforming in Europe. And then you go on the international stage and you've got the same thing. Right? So this is something which is quite consistent. And you can't just write it off saying, "They've got Haaland." Well, Bodø/Glimt don't have Haaland. They don't have Ødegaard. They've got Norwegian —

Five games in a row against Atletico, Man City and Inter Milan home and away. I mean —

Yeah.

为什么苏格兰做不到？

So, I think it's just something which might be interesting for Scotland to have a look at.

Now, why won't that happen, Charlie? The reason that won't happen is because — if we think that the FA in this country are stuffy blazers who aren't particularly progressive, which is an unfair characterization actually — I think the Scottish FA and Scottish football as a whole is the most hidebound conservative backwards-looking sport in terms of it being the national sport, which it is. It's quite extraordinary.

When I speak to people in Scottish football, the amount of time that they reference the 1960s, the 1970s as being "that's what we need to get back to," as opposed to looking forward — they always look backwards. And then you've got the politics that surrounds Celtic and Rangers being the dominant forces within Scottish football, and then basically being at loggerheads with each other, and therefore the Scottish FA not being in a strong position when you've got these two mega clubs who basically are easily the wealthiest parts of the whole ecosystem.

So this would need to be something that was being decided, as happened in Norway, at a political level. This would have to be being decided up here. And you look at the Scottish government and bless them, you can't see them having this kind of strategic overview or interest in excellence in any way.

反驳："这不是钱的问题，是社会哲学的问题"

If they were listening to this, I could — I know the response that would be given. "Oh, but Norway are one of the richest countries in the world."

Yeah.

Well, yes, but actually really what we're talking about here — a lot of this is societal and it's an approach. It's not the spend. If we're looking at spend, the spend here for Norway was creating a system, well, an infrastructure system that facilitated all-year-round football if they could on pitches that didn't freeze over. That's an expensive product.

The other side of it is coaching. So they have 17,000 coaches that come through a grassroots system. It's basically a coach every 320 people in the population. That's totally astonishing. Coaching is a national sport.

They also had 2,000 people that completed their UEFA B licenses in the last decade. It's a really incredible model. It's absolutely — it is completely possible in the smaller countries that have much more control around this type of thing. And it would be very hard to do this in England.

Yeah. Even at 60 million, let alone — as I said, it's a bit unfair. But the US, the reason I brought the US into it is because a lot of the US sporting analysts have been looking at Norway. And they did during the Winter Olympics — that was their moment to just try and understand how Norway would top the medals table for a third year in a row.

For the third Olympics in a row.

But anyway, I think, look, to conclude, it's a really interesting sporting success story. As you say, it stretches across many different sports. It's being brought to the fore particularly in football, which is the world's global game. And therefore — having advantages such as loads of snow helping being good at ski jumping is not so relevant when it comes to football.

And then just casting a light on how countries can think differently to be able to enable themselves to compete in what otherwise simply becomes a numbers game. So in other words, if Scotland do the same as England, then England has 10 times as many people and therefore will do 10 times as well. Right? So therefore, if Scotland really wants to compete against England, it has to do it differently. And this is what Norway have done.

Completely.

Absolutely. So, well, look, I hope you get an absolute pasting. By "you" I mean Norway.

I hope Norway gets an absolute pasting. But if we were to have to lose, then we couldn't lose to better Vikings.

Exactly. And just to be clear, I am firmly England shirt on the weekend. But it's fun to give them the credit they deserve as and when.

Indeed.

Silverstone 与英国大奖赛：从破产到 5 亿英镑估值

So, moving on. So another massive sporting event that happened last weekend, Charlie. And almost, just for once, outside of Petrolheadland, a little bit under the radar. Because you had the World Cup going at full pelt right here, including the buildup to the big England game, you had Wimbledon building towards its finale here — and then the little matter of the British Grand Prix comes along.

Now, the race itself — did you watch it?

No.

Okay.

I was at a one-year-old's birthday party, which is the social event now that really keeps you going.

Indeed. I did watch it, at least I watched parts of it. And it ended very unsatisfactorily. And that would be a whole conversation for a different day and probably not on this podcast about why a major sporting event like that can finish behind a safety car, meaning that you don't get any excitement or any sort of fulfillment even towards the end of the race, right? So basically it was a —

Are you calling for a rerun of the Lewis Hamilton Max Verstappen disaster?

No, I'm — as I said, it's a very complex topic. I don't want to go into it right now. It was an unsatisfactory race. Let's just put it that way.

Charles Leclerc won, didn't he?

Charles Leclerc won. And —

But then it was just a run of British drivers.

Loads of them. Loads of them. Anyway, we got a lot to talk about here, so let's not talk about the race, right? The race was the race and it was unsatisfactory. And it was what it was.

564,000 人：史上最大 F1 事件

But the event — the event was absolutely massive. Do you know how many people turned up at Silverstone over the weekend?

564,000 people.

564,000 people over 3 days.

The biggest F1 event in history.

So you're talking about almost 200,000 a day, Charles.

Four days.

It was four days.

Four days. Yeah, cuz they include the Thursday as well.

So it's 150,000 on average a day, right? 150,000 people on average a day over a 4 day period. It was an absolute beast of an event. And I thought it'd be quite a useful moment to examine both the British Grand Prix itself, but also to have a think about this area of the Formula 1 industry, which is those countries and tracks that host events and the economics of that.

We've been very focused since Liberty Media took over Formula 1. We've been very focused on the top end — i.e. what Liberty are doing. And increasingly focused on the teams of course. And we've discussed usefully over the last few months how the economics and the valuations of the teams have been moving dramatically. And meanwhile the economics of the hosting side have dropped down the agenda where 20 years ago this was at the top of the Formula 1 sporting agenda.

BRDC：850 位成员的私人俱乐部

So, where to start? I think perhaps —

Do you want to really start? I think the first thing, just based on how we're going to develop this, is to really look at who actually is responsible for putting on Silverstone itself.

Indeed. That's right. So Silverstone is owned and operated by something called the British Racing Drivers Club, the BRDC. Were you aware of this body until I brought it up with you?

I was.

You were? Yes. Okay. But only — I was only relatively recently — because I've been spammed by Jake Humphrey's social media accounts as he has just recently been made a member of the BRDC, which he was absolutely ecstatic about because it's very meaningful within petrolhead world.

There are 850 members. And to become a member you have to have achieved something of excellence within motorsport. This isn't something which is: "Okay, well, I'm rich, I can just pay to get in." This is an invitation thing. You're proposed and seconded and accepted. But your proposal and second have to explain: "This is why this person has achieved something significant in motorsport." And you have to be either British or from the Commonwealth. Right, now they made a little exception to let Eddie Jordan in — the late Eddie Jordan — where they said, "Okay, well, if you were born in Ireland before it officially became fully separated, if you're in the Irish Free State or something like that." Basically it was, "We want Eddie in and we'll create a sort of exemption there for him because we like him."

从 1928 到 Silverstone

But it's 850 members. So, it was founded in 1928. So, we're coming up to the centenary of the BRDC. And it was founded by a man called Dr. Dudley Benjafield. And he was one of what was called the Bentley Boys, and these were the early motor racing drivers. Back when just cars themselves were a relatively recent innovation, they were racing some of the early faster types of cars, including obviously Bentleys, hence why they're known as the Bentley Boys.

And the BRDC was based at Brooklands, which was a track in Surrey. And it was an up-and-coming sport. And during the Second World War, actually a lot of BRDC members died — a lot of them involved in flying. So you have Battle of Britain people who died etc. So it was still a relatively young organization as we come out the back of the Second World War, and this is when we start the move towards Silverstone.

So they took over the lease of Silverstone in 1952, the BRDC did, as a motor racing track. And then in 1966 — right, so this is within some people's, not mine before you mention it, you cheeky sod. Do you know my age? — this is within living memory for some people who might be listening to this show. So the BRDC opened a subsidiary which is called Silverstone Circuit Limited, which was specifically for the development of the British Grand Prix.

Bernie Ecclestone 时代：Hosting Fee 的诞生

Right. So that's where we're going back to. So over the following 20 years the British Grand Prix develops and grows as do other Grands Prix around the world, but in slightly chaotic fashion. The whole thing wasn't sort of properly put together until of course famously Bernie Ecclestone, who had been both a driver himself and then a team boss, came along and said to the other team owners, "We need to organize this properly. We need to sell the rights properly. We need to do this whole thing in a much more structured way."

And of course by this point the British Grand Prix is one of the established Grands Prix — one of the legacy, historic Grands Prix which Bernie's new F1 circuit — effectively circus — inherits. Silverstone as already being a major event. This is the early — late 1970s, early 1980s. So it's already a major sporting event.

And Bernie's business model then starts to develop during the 1980s. And one of the ways in which Bernie starts to make money — where previously they would say, "Oh, well, the British Racing Drivers Club is having a Grand Prix and that's great. Let's all go and race there and happy days" — Bernie then starts saying to circuits, "If you want your Grand Prix to be in my circus — if I'm going to bring all of these teams to race at your circuit — you need to pay me money. You need to pay Formula 1 management money." It's what's called a hosting fee, right?

So that starts off quite small, but as Bernie then starts to access Grands Prix in different parts of the world where they're very happy to pay very large sums of money — particularly the push into the Middle East that started to happen 20, 25 years ago — some of the historic circuits start to drop out because they can't afford to pay these hosting fees. Okay?

2015：Silverstone 濒临破产

And eventually the hosting fees started to get so big that Silverstone started to struggle to pay them as well. So this is around about 10 to 15 years ago — they've gone from 5 million to 10 million to 15 million a year. Right? And by this point, what Bernie's done within Formula 1 is he's basically said, "Right, I keep pretty much all the commercial revenues for Formula 1 management. Obviously we've taken the TV rights revenues." So as a host of the race, you don't get any TV rights revenue. You don't get any commercial revenue. The one thing you do get is ticketing. Congratulations.

Right. So, you're starting to be confronted with quite a stark thing, which is: "We pay Formula 1 management £15 million a year. We then need to get £15 million worth of ticketing just to cover our costs."

So, the chairman John Grant — I was just reading this. He very effortlessly put it: "The costs are more than the revenues."

Yes.

Great. I mean, that's the kind of —

So what happened was by 2015 they're on the verge of bankruptcy. They were being bankrupted by Bernie Ecclestone. And Bernie Ecclestone's view was, "Well, guys, if you don't want to have a Grand Prix, don't have a Grand Prix." Right?

Really interesting.

Yeah. He used to — terrible circuit anyway. Terrible facilities. He was always slagging it off. He was always slagging off the BRDC, right? I think was there an element of sort of chip on shoulder about the whole thing? That there was this rather sort of grand club which included lots of dukes and sirs, and there was Bernie the Southeast London car salesman who wasn't really part of that whole set. Was there a bit of that going on? Paying off old scores from what he saw as — maybe he felt slighted in the 1970s by not being invited to be a member back then?

All of maybe there was a bit of all that going on. But the bottom line is is that by 2015, which is just before Liberty took over, they're tied into this contract with Bernie by which basically the amount they have to pay to host the race increases like the equivalent triple lock — ahead of inflation every single year.

Well, it was on 5% escalators, weren't they?

Exactly. So each year it went up 5%.

Yeah. And this is during a period when inflation was very very low. But it's also interesting here because it's during a period where Formula 1 itself is struggling more than it has done in a very long time.

And that was important on both sides. Right? So on Bernie's side, the fact that Formula 1 was struggling in general meant that the hosting fees had become an absolutely critical part of the entire financial ecosystem. Extracting money from tracks was becoming being the main revenue line, right? Because they were going to tracks in the Middle East. Bernie was going, "Right, do you want to have a Grand Prix?" "Well, it's 40 million, right? Do you want to have a Grand Prix? It's 45 million." And then to Silverstone saying, "Well, look, if you're not even willing to pay me 15, 20 million, then I'll just go and take another one somewhere else. I'm not particularly interested in you."

Which of course was disastrous for the sport — and you lost some historic Grands Prix like the one in South Africa and the one in Argentina during this 20, 25 year period — because Bernie knew that they wouldn't pay the hosting fees and he'd rather take it somewhere else. But of course those were the places where you get massive crowds in big countries like South Africa and Argentina. Whereas some of the places where Bernie then took Formula 1 to was a little bit more marginal.

Break Clause 与 Liberty Media 的接手

So John Grant, in effect, he activated the break clause, didn't he, for the BRDC.

Correct. So he basically said —

Exactly right. "We're going to — fine, we're out."

We're out. So just before Bernie sold — or CVC and Bernie sold — to Liberty Media, which is 2017, 2018 — just before then, Silverstone, the British Racing Drivers Club, had activated a break clause in the contract and they basically said, "We can't afford this." Right? So we were looking at the end of the British Grand Prix at that point. And that was much discussed in the media at the time.

And then Liberty come along and Liberty take a totally different view of the industry and the sport and say, "No, the British Grand Prix is a crucial bit of IP which lends into the value of the entire thing. Having a race where there are 200,000 people there and everyone's going absolutely mad — this is like the core of fandom. And we're trying to build fandom." So they then went and cut a better deal with the BRDC, which meant the BRDC could then see, "Right, we are now going to carry on hosting this Grand Prix for a significant period of time, but we have much greater certainty over it — potentially affordable."

Stuart Pringle 与 20 年稳定治理

But at the same time, the BRDC just said, "Okay, well, we also do need to get our own house in order. We need to become a more diversified business so that we can be a proper business rather than a club that holds a Grand Prix. We actually need to be a motor racing business as a whole."

And there's been a guy there, sort of unsung hero of sports business, called Stuart Pringle, who's been the CEO — or I think originally was the secretary of the British Racing Drivers Club, but since then the title's been changed to CEO — since 2006. So we're talking about a 20-year regime stability there. The first 10 of which was extremely difficult when they were having to spend money on repairing facilities and when the hosting fees were going up and up and when Formula 1 as a whole was going down and down, leading to the verge of bankruptcy in 2015.

10 年后：1.1 亿英镑营收，5 亿英镑估值

Lo and behold, 10 years later, through both the efforts of Silverstone themselves but also of course the efforts of Liberty Media in terms of the way in which they run Formula 1, Silverstone now has an annual turnover of 110 million pounds — 50% of which is from the British Grand Prix. So you can work it out. Okay, it's about 50 to 60 million they get from the British Grand Prix and 50% from the rest of the year from the stuff that they do during the rest of the year.

And during that period they have invested heavily in a heritage center, conference center, hotel, increased capacity, hospitality — all this type of stuff. And Liberty have got a much more progressive model in terms of working with tracks, where these days the tracks do get to keep past the hospitality, right? So it's not the old days of the paddock club where that was all kept in-house. These days tracks are incentivized to drive hospitality and to actually sort of become part of the whole mix.

The bottom line is they're now making pre-tax profit of 14 million quid and they've got cash reserves of almost 50 million quid. I mean, it's the most astonishing sports business turnaround.

10 years. In less than 10 years you've gone from basically going, "We can't afford this," to now — just this week gone by, hence why we're talking about it — the biggest event in Formula 1 history.

Yes. That is actually most importantly financially valuable, profitable and massively valuable. So if you think — let's just have a think about this. Right? Just as a business, as a sports industry business, annual turnover of 110 million quid with a profit margin of getting on for 10% — no, more than 10%, getting on for 15%. Right? So you're looking at probably a business which the BRDC, if it were a private company and was now to sell itself — Christ, I don't know, what would it be worth? You're probably talking about certainly the thick end of 500 million.

Right? So you've gone from something on the verge of bankruptcy to something that's worth 500-odd million in the space of 10 years. So it's been a great —

BRDC 的会员构成：一个反 DEI 的组织

Just to give people quickly a bit of a flavor of how idiosyncratic this organization is: so the president is David Coulthard, the former Formula 1 driver. And the vice presidents are Martin Brundle, former Formula 1 driver, media pundit; Ron Dennis, the former McLaren boss; Damon Hill, the former Formula 1 world champion; Nigel Mansell, the former Formula 1 world champion; Jackie Stewart; Derek Warwick, the former Formula 1... So basically —

Their diversity is very strong.

So basically —

The Lancaster cricket board test — they're not passing that.

They're not. But they're actually doing a great job.

They're actually doing a great job. They might not be ticking too many DEI boxes here, but the BRDC are doing a great job.

And for all that, sometimes on this show we do pick apart things that aren't working. I think sometimes we have to take a step back and say that it wasn't Silverstone's fault that this race was a mess. That was Formula 1's problem. In terms of the event as a whole, obviously stonking weather as well which helped, this was an absolutely epic sports business event.

与其他 GP 主办模式的对比

Yeah. And most interestingly as I was going through it as well, it was looking at this compared to the other hosting organizations around. And this is — so this is the only private members club hosting. I think actually I'm going to say now because I think the Dutch Grand Prix was also similar, but they're losing their race. So it will now be the only independent. The rest of the models generally are funded by state. So then thereabouts, Abu Dhabi, Bahrain — actually Australia part as well for the Melbourne Grand Prix. And really for those fees — I'm not quite sure how it works in Miami.

So I do. Yeah.

So but the fees attached on the state naturally — Azerbaijan reportedly are paying $57 million a year. Qatar and Saudi $55 million — 10-year agreements locked in with Qatar. So that's 550 million. Abu Dhabi 42, Australia, Singapore 35–40. You then have kind of a mixture of promoter and state, which is the Monaco and Monza type models, and they pay about 15 to 20 million.

And interestingly they both recently doubled in the new extensions. So prices going up.

Then you have Silverstone, a private members club. Then you have Miami and Vegas — F1 itself pays the hosting fee. And it's not hard to understand why the value for Formula 1 —

Breaking the US market.

Breaking the US market and having Vegas. So those are generally the different ways in which Formula 1 makes its hosting fees. And it totals — you mentioned it was a big part. I mean, it's $700 million a year in hosting fees.

In hosting fees for everyone. That's the amount of money that the holding company of Formula 1 rakes in from places paying them for the privilege of having a Grand Prix.

It's a huge business. It's about 20 to 25% of Formula 1's revenue.

Which is extraordinarily high.

It is. Yeah. It's a key part of the pie.

巴林大奖赛的战略意义

And you can sort of understand it. Like — I advised the Bahrain Grand Prix for many many years. Christ, 8, 9 years. And I would often ask, "Is this worth it?" Right? Absolutely it's worth it. Right? This is one week of the year when the attention of a good chunk of the world is on our little island kingdom. We struggle for attention. We struggle to drive a tourism ministry even though we've got beautiful places for people to come to. How do we drive attention on that? Other countries might spend vast amounts of money on marketing tourism, but we find that by hosting a Grand Prix — with all of the imagery that goes around that and the eyeballs that go onto that imagery — is actually what helps drive our entire economy.

It also means that they can then have a motorsports economy in Bahrain because they've got a Formula 1 track there. So you can test cars, test products. It enables them to be a center for motorsport in the Middle East. So as an investment over a 20-year period, it's been extremely successful. So, this has actually been for most of these deals, it's been a happy thing for the BRDC.

It was a little bit different — they were there first and they were a club. Not — they're not trying to pay dividends and make big profits and all this type of stuff. But they've effectively been forced into the modern age, into the 21st century, by the economics of hosting a Grand Prix. And they've done it.

The F1 also have the flexibility to make the calls as and where they want to on which countries and which host cities will pay more for. So that's a balance of heritage versus — under Liberty they've been much more cognizant of that, where under Bernie it was getting very mercantile. It was whoever pays the most.

It's the luxury of success. When you're successful you can work out how to make those numbers work for you. When you're struggling for cash, making decisions — Liberty did this right from the start, didn't they? So they did it before they made a big success out of F1. They made decisions like, "We need to keep the British Grand Prix." So it's a bit of chicken and egg involved here. But I think you have to give Liberty a lot of credit for saying, "We're not losing the British Grand Prix."

Brilliant. Right. Brilliant. Fantastic.

结尾预告：Elliot Anderson 转会分析

Good stuff, Charlie. Now, if you want to stay with us, we're going to go and do a very nice segment on the absolutely crazy transfer of Elliot Anderson to Manchester City from Nottingham Forest. Because there's a little bit more than meets the eye to some of this, as there is with the broader footballing ecosystem when you start getting into the details of some of these transfers. Come and join us on Substack if you want to keep going.

But otherwise, thank you. I really enjoyed that this week. It was good fun.

Yeah, thanks Chaz.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 三个隐藏的系统性真相
### 1. 挪威模式的反直觉：**"延迟专业化"是一种高级形式的风险管理**
表面上看，挪威把足球职业化推迟到 15-16 岁是"人文关怀"，但这其实是一种**期权定价思维**在体育人才培养中的应用。
传统精英俱乐部体系（英格兰、苏格兰、南美）本质上是**在 9 岁做投资决策**——他们必须在信息极度不完整时"押注"哪个孩子会成才。这就像风险投资在 Pre-Seed 阶段就要 All-in，注定错误率极高。而挪威系统实际上是把决策点推迟到"信号-噪声比"最高的时刻（青春期发育之后），这时候**每一份投资的边际回报率呈指数级上升**。
哈兰德是最好的证据——他早期身材瘦小，在任何 9 岁筛选制的国家都会被淘汰。挪威系统"保留了他的期权"，直到他自己行使了它。这跟金融里的"美式期权 vs 欧式期权"是同一逻辑：**保留决策权到最后一刻，永远比提前锁定更有价值**。
### 2. 一个跨领域的意外连接：**特朗普事件与 Silverstone 的 BRDC 揭示了同一个"治理悖论"**
这两个故事看似毫不相关，但在系统层面上讲的是同一件事——**"程

item.title: [All-In Podcast] Open Source Wins, AGI Is Here, and Scorsese’s AI Toolkit with CEOs of Cerebras & Black Forest Labs
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反常识的观察：这场"AI 大建设"不是在追未来，而是在追"昨天的欠账"

Andrew Feldman 说了一句极具颠覆性的话："They're trying to capture yesterday's demand."

我们习惯把基础设施投资理解为"预测未来 → 提前布局"，但 AI 数据中心的现状恰好相反——订单在芯片出厂之前就已经排队，250 亿美元的 backlog 不是赌明天，而是在偿还昨天已经发生但没被满足的需求。这与历史上所有"泡沫"的心理结构截然不同：泡沫是"如果我建，他们会来"；而现在是"他们已经在门口敲门，我建不出来"。

这里藏着一个更深的系统性真相：人类第一次遇到了一种"消费速度快于生产速度"的通用生产力商品。电力、钢铁、石油都曾经历过短缺，但从未有一种商品的边际效用像 token 这样——用得越多，用户变得越聪明，从而产生更多需要 token 的问题。这是一个自我加速的需求飞轮，不是需求预测问题，而是需求本身在递归自我制造。

二、跨领域连接：Costco 悖论 × 精子鞭毛 × 石匠世家——同一条学习曲线

Feldman 用 Costco 类比 token 消费的成熟过程（从"每条通道都走一遍"到"直奔烤鸡"），这看似只是消费心理学。但把它和后半段 Robin Rombach 谈的"石匠家族传承"、以及 Andrew 提到的"果蝇代际学习"放在一起看，会发现一个惊人的同构：

  • Costco 用户：初期无差别遍历 → 后期策略性收敛
  • 石匠世家：三代人在同一座建筑上叠加认知
  • 果蝇遗传学：一天两代，让千代压缩成人类可观察的时间
  • AI 递归推理：在 24 小时内完成人类几周的思考

这四者的共同底层结构是："代际压缩"作为智能进化的通用引擎。人类文明过去两千年最大的瓶颈从来不是缺乏聪明人，而是范式随人死亡——Jason 引用心理学教授那句"paradigms don't die, people do"是本次对话最锋利的一刀。Freud、Skinner、Jung 的理论都要等他们的学生老去才能被替换。

而现在，AI 把"代际"压缩到了小时级。这意味着范式更迭的速度不再受制于人类寿命。这才是"超级智能"最不起眼但最颠覆性的定义——不是它更聪明，而是它可以在一天内经历十代认知革命。

三、隐藏系统性真相："意图理解"的出现，正在悄悄摧毁一个百年产业——中层管理

对话中一个几乎被忽略的细节：Jason 说 AI 现在会反过来问他"你有需求文档吗？你的目标是什么？"

如果你把这句话放到组织行为学里看，会发现这正是中层管理者百年来存在的唯一理由——把高层的模糊意图翻译成下层可执行的规格。彼得·德鲁克整个管理学体系的基石就是"目标管理 (MBO)"，本质是人肉的意图澄清器。

Reasoning 模型的出现意味着：意图澄清这个功能，第一次可以由非人来完成，且比人更耐心、更结构化。这不是"AI 取代白领"这种粗糙叙事，而是更精准的：AI 正在从组织架构中抽出"意图翻译层"。

跨领域连接：这和 Robin 讲的"图像是比语言更高带宽的沟通媒介"完美咬合。Scorsese 用 AI 把脑中画面"外化"，本质上和 CEO 用 AI 把战略"外化"是同一件事——都是在绕过语言这个 lossy compression 层，让意图直接落地为可执行的产物（图像/代码/决策）。

四、一个让你意外的推论：开源不是"民主化 AI"，而是"AI 主权化"

大多数人把开源解读为"让更多人用得起 AI"，但这次对话透露了完全不同的信号：Feldman 提到 UAE 的 G42、GSK 定制模型、欧洲的主权担忧——开源真正在做的事情，不是普及，而是让国家/大企业能够拒绝依附。

这有个非常反直觉的意涵：开源的最大受益者不是散户开发者，而是"不想依赖美国 AI 巨头"的主权实体。中国的 GLM、Kimi、Qwen 之所以能快速崛起，本质上是地缘政治为开源模型提供了刚需。

再往深挖：如果 AGI 已经到来（Feldman 和 Jason 都同意这一点），那么"谁掌握模型 = 谁掌握认知基础设施"。这就把开源从软件哲学问题，升级成了国防问题。这也是为什么 Anthropic 部署前政府要"红队测试"——它已经不是软件发布，而是认知级武器的军控。

AHA 时刻的浓缩：这场对话表面在谈芯片、GPU、生成模型，深层却在讲同一件事——人类文明第一次遇到"代际压缩速度超过生物代际的智能载体"。所有的建设狂热、开源博弈、主权焦虑、意图理解，都是这个底层现实的不同投影。
