item.description:
## 一个反直觉的观察：Fogel 的"护城河否定论"其实是护城河本身
大多数CEO会向投资人反复强调"我们的护城河很深"，但Fogel 反复说 "There is no such thing as a moat"。这句话表面像是承认脆弱，实则是一种**认知层面的护城河**——它内嵌了一种组织级的"永久恐慌"。
有意思的是，这与 Andy Grove 的 "Only the paranoid survive"、贝索斯的 "Day 1" 心态、以及生物学上的"红皇后假说"（Red Queen Hypothesis：为了留在原地必须不断奔跑）形成了一条隐秘的思想谱系。**Booking 真正的护城河不是 860 万套房源，而是 25,000 名员工共同持有"没有护城河"这个信念**——这才是最难被模仿的资产。竞争对手可以复制库存，可以复制 UI，甚至可以复制 AI 模型，但**很难复制一种集体性的、持续 27 年的不安全感**。
## 跨领域连接：旅行搜索的困境，其实是"婚姻决策学"的翻版
Fogel 讲了一个很坦诚的细节——他和妻子会为"谁来做旅行规划"而争吵。这个私人瞬间意外地

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1450期】绝对反直觉，“真实利率”看清一切✨20260708#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被误读的"沉默"：美联储什么都没做，却什么都做了

小陶在视频末尾抛出的那个"美联储什么都没做"的悖论，其实是这一期真正的洞穴入口。今年上半年美联储的政策利率没有任何变动，但真实利率却几乎独立完成了整段名义利率的上行——这意味着市场自己在替美联储加息。凯文·沃什一句"要把通胀压回2%"就让两年期收益率飙升，市场从"年初预期降息两次"逆转为"预期加息1.5次"。

这里有一个反直觉的观察：美联储越沉默，市场的自我紧缩就越剧烈。因为沉默让"forward guidance"的解释权外包给了市场，而市场会用最鹰派的可能性去自我保护。你会发现这几乎就是量子物理里的"测量问题"——观察者（市场）在等待被观察对象（美联储）表态的过程中，反而通过预期本身塌缩出了一个"实际发生的紧缩"。真正的货币政策，不在利率决议里，而在市场对沉默的解码里。

二、真正需要盯的不是"负相关"，而是"负相关的拐点"

绝大多数评论员会告诉你：真实利率上升→美股下跌，这是过去6年的铁律。但小陶提到了一个几乎被埋没的细节——相关性从-0.9开始向0收敛。这才是真正值得盯住的信号。

这里有一个跨领域的类比：这非常像地震学里的"前震静默"。当一个体系长期处于极端相关状态（-0.9几乎是数学上的极值），任何回归中值的运动都在暗示"主导逻辑正在切换"。从-0.9到0的路径本身，就是一场市场叙事的政权更迭——它不是简单的"关系变弱"，而是决定股价的第一性驱动力正在换手：从"美联储货币政策"切回到"经济基本面"。

这就是为什么小陶那个箭头如此重要：他标出的不是价格拐点，而是"故事线的拐点"。当拐点确立，即便真实利率继续上冲，美股也可能同步上涨——因为市场在讲的已经不再是同一个故事。

三、被忽略的第三重维度：真实利率其实是一个"叙事测谎仪"

大多数投资者把真实利率当成"折现率"来用，但这期视频真正的隐藏结构，是把真实利率当成一个"市场情绪的X光片"。

考虑这个奇妙的三角： - 真实利率↑ + 通胀预期↓ = 市场其实在偷偷讲"通缩故事" - 真实利率↑ + 美股↑ = 市场在讲"经济繁荣故事"
- 真实利率↑ + 美股↓ = 市场在讲"央行紧缩故事"

同样一个"真实利率上升"，可以对应三个完全不同的宏观叙事。这就像中医的"同病异治"——症状一样，但底层病机完全不同，用药方向甚至相反。

而今年最大的一个"啊哈时刻"是：即使经历了3月的战争事件，2年期与10年期的breakeven通胀预期反而下跌了。这几乎颠覆了所有主流媒体每天的头版叙事——所谓"关税推高通胀"、"地缘引爆油价"这些故事线，被债券市场用真金白银否决了。华尔街每天忙着给股价编故事，但真正在交易的那批人，早在breakeven里给出了完全相反的答案。

跨领域的一个奇妙连接：这几乎就是信息论里的"熵最小估计"——债券市场是所有资产类别中信息熵最低的地方，因为它每天被上万亿美元的真金白银定价，任何噪音都会被套利掉。所以当你看到debate上的两个财经评论员在吵通胀会不会回来时，闭嘴，去看TIPS——那才是市场真正在下的注。

📝 转录文本

开场：改变讲话方式的尝试

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单治疗新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场分析员小陶。

在昨天的视频下面有一条留言，让我在讲视频的时候稍微有些停顿，然后稍微语气上有一些重和轻，这样的话大家在听视频的时候可以听到一些重点。那所以接下去我试图去改变我讲话的这个习惯，会比较的难，但是我试图去尝试一下。

因为我的讲话一般都是比较的快，然后语调是比较的平，所以确实，听久了之后，听了20分钟、30分钟之后，很容易就会丢掉一些重点。我自己意识到有这样的一个问题，所以今天我试图要做出一些改变。

改变的方向有两点： - 第一点，我把视频的这个时长稍微压缩一些，这样的话大家在听视频的时候，可能不会有那么大的一些压力。因为20分钟30分钟连续二三十分钟这种非常高频率的、算是知识上的输出也好，或者观点上的输出也好，给大家冲击可能太大了。 - 所以我尽量把这个时间稍微压缩一些，然后内容的话也会压缩一些，就会挑比较的精一些。

所以我今天就挑了一个话题出来和大家讲，但是也正是因为这个话题，可能对于我们投资股市的这些观众来说非常的陌生，且有一定的一个理解难度在背后。所以我今天试图转变一下我讲话的这种方式，试图有一些抑扬顿挫，这样的话大家容易找到这个重点，且把整个的内容稍微压缩一下。看一下我今天能不能成功，大家在留言区里面告诉我。

因为我相信，今天这期话题的一个核心或者话题的一个主题，对于大多数人来说可能是比较陌生的，因为我讲的是债券市场这边。

今日主题：真实收益率（真实利率）

好吧，那我就废话不多说了，我就马上开始今天的内容。今天我就不多做市场的这个回顾了，因为我觉得今天市场比较的无聊，所以我们今天直接就切入主题。当然在切入主题之前，大家记得留言点赞加转发，让更多的人可以看到我们这期内容。

今天我要讲什么？今天我要讲的一个概念叫做真实收益率，或者我们可以奔他把它称之为是真实利率。

我今天在看一家机构研报的时候，看到了这家机构对于股市对于债市这边的一些看法，他这边讲到了一个真实收益率，所以我想了一下，正好我们可以把这个概念摊开来去讲。当然因为我们这个频道大多数的观众都是股市的投资者，对于债市这一块可能没有非常多的一些了解，也没有非常多的一些投资，所以我最后会把真实收益率和股市这边去做一个联系——怎么通过真实收益率的走势，去和股市未来的走势预期联系在一起。

机构观点：真实收益率的故事

好吧，首先我们先来看一下这家机构的一些观点。

第一个观察：今年的利率走势，它是一个真实收益率的故事，而不是通胀预期的故事。目前真实收益率为2.25%，他们总结的时间点是第二季度的季度尾。

那我先解释一下真实收益率，稍后我会讲一下什么东西能够影响到真实收益率。

平时我们在讲十年期美债收益率，比如说在4.5%、在4.6%，这4.5%和4.6%，我们主要讲的叫做名义利率。而：

名义利率 = 真实收益率 + 通胀预期

所以他这边讲的真实收益率的故事和通胀预期的故事，你把这两个故事或者把这两个利率加在一起，就等于名义利率，就等于我们平时所讲的10年期美债收益率4.5%、4.6%。

而通胀预期，平时我也和大家分享过英文的话，它叫做breakeven inflation，理解为是市场对于通胀预期的定价。但实际上市场是没有办法去交易通胀预期的，通胀预期它只是一个结果。

市场交易的是什么？市场交易的是十年期美债收益率，十年期美国国债的收益率，或者我们称它为名义利率——名义利率也是交易出来的结果。另外一个市场交易的就是真实收益率，而真实收益率的这个市场我们叫做TIPS，翻成中文的话应该是抗通胀债券、抗通胀美国国债。

所以市场通过交易真实收益率——交易TIPS这个市场，然后交易美国国债这个市场——交易出来的这个结果差，就是通胀预期。

TIPS 市场的运作逻辑

另外一点，市场为什么要去交易TIPS？

简单来说，如果市场的交易者对于未来的通胀有上升的预期，市场就会去买入TIPS，买入这一个抗通胀美国国债。而市场买入抗通胀美国国债之后，会出现TIPS的价格往上升，TIPS的收益率往下跌。

反过来，当真实收益率在上升的时候，它可以是因为市场对于通胀的担心在下降。所以市场在抛出TIPS，再抛出抗通胀美国国债，使得价格下跌，使得收益率上升。

那我们顺着这样的一个逻辑往下推：观察点是YTD（年初至今），到现在为止，美债收益率——名义美国国债的收益率——几乎全部是来自于真实收益率的上升。如果真实收益率的上升是因为市场对于通胀的担心风险往下降的话，也就是代表YTD到现在为止，其实市场根本就没有担心通胀，反而对于通胀的担心是在下降的。

真实收益率的危险区间

然后我们继续看下去，他说：从讨论中指出，真实收益率在3%和4%之间是历史上的一个危险区间，对于美股这边就会产生影响： - 在3%开始失利，开始对美股开始进行失利； - 而4%的话则代表更为严重的压力点。

所以现在，真实收益率在2.25%，离3%还有一定的距离，所以还没有到需要非常担心的一个地步。

关于 TLT 与久期风险

然后我们继续看下去，倒数第二段。这边美国97就是duration。

我一直劝大家，如果你们不是做债券商的话，不要去交易TLT这个标的。TLT这个标的是留给专业的机构投资者去做的，或者这些机构的一些专业的一些小的机构去做的，不是散户去做。因为散户对于久期、对于美国国债没有理解，对于美国国债的久期风险就更加没有理解了，所以根本就没有必要去分TLT这个标的。聊这句话的时候，起码已经是一两年之前了。那么和大家分享我自己的看法：不要去碰TLT这个标的。

那这家机构说到——你看连机构多少——他说美国久期仍然是最难做出判断的一个标的。为什么？因为当下美联储正面临着异常复杂的一个环境：

  • 资本需求的强劲：就是对于债券这一块的需求。对于债券有需求的话，会造成久期或者说美国国债、美国长期国债这个利率往上涨还是往下跌？利率会往下跌。因为有足够的购买力，有足够的需求在背后，有足够的buying power在背后，价格上涨导致收益率下跌。所以资本需求的强劲，代表它是对利率的下行有一个助推作用——所以我这边有一个向下的箭头。
  • 经济增长具有韧性：代表经济增速还是OK的。经济增速如果OK的话，对于利率是一个上行的压力，或者对于久期风险是一个增加的压力，会使得长期的美国国债利率往上升——所以我这边向上的箭头。
  • 消费者实力参差不齐：那这一点的话我们只能画一个横平的箭头。因为你并不知道整体的消费到底是往上还是往下。如果整体的消费非常的强劲，那应该是往上的箭头；消费非常的弱，有衰退的风险，那应该是往下的箭头。
  • 人工智能AI所驱动的投资：网上还是网下？短期来说绝对是网上的。为什么？因为它会增加通胀的风险，它会增加利率。
  • 以及可能尚未完全消退的通胀：那也是一个向上的风险。

那么所以他这边所列举的一个非常复杂的环境，可能大多数的这些环境，对于长债这一块的利率都是向上的风险。

欧洲久期机会

反过来，相比之下，欧洲那边则提供了更加明朗的久期机会。什么叫久期机会？就是你可以buy duration，你可以去购入久期，购入久期就是做多长期美国国债——增长放缓。

为什么？增长放缓就是经济增长具有韧性的反面，那增长放缓就是向下的压力。央行的职责更为单一，以及越来越多的证据证明，欧洲央行ECB可能只需要加息一次，就足以结束这一次的紧缩周期。也就是他认为欧洲那边加息的周期已经在身后了，接下去的货币政策可能继续往相对更加鸽派的方向去走。更加鸽派的方向去走，有利于久期，会使得利率往下跌，做多久期。

所以这些因素都支撑了对发达市场非美债务，特别是欧洲固定收益产品——就是欧洲这边的债券——超配的策略。所以他们对于欧洲非美市场这一块是超配overweight，对于美国这边可能就是平的，因为他们并没有办法非常准确的去判断，美国国债这边的久期到底是网上还是网下——因为网上和网下背后的环境都有。

影响真实利率的三大原因

我前面讲到，因为YTD我们到现在为止的观察，整个名义利率的上涨，几乎全部是来自于真实利率的上涨。那什么能够使得真实利率出现上涨？主要有三个原因，其实这三个原因分别是来自于三个不同的方向：

  1. 货币政策：如果我们设在一个紧缩的货币政策之下，那它会使得真实利率往上升。
  2. 强劲的经济增长。
  3. 国债供应量的增加：那第三点也很好理解，美国政府如果财政赤字持续扩大，那他们需要持续去举更多的美债。举更多的美债，自然在供给端这一块，你要举更多的债务，那借钱给美国政府的这些人，或者我们说美国政府的债权人，自然就会向美国政府去要求更高的回报。那市场要求更高的回报，那自然它的融资成本就更高。融资成本更高，利率就会上升，那就会代表更高的利率或者更高的真实利率。

关键区分：紧缩货币政策 vs. 强劲经济增长

啊，然后这边我想稍微再提一下第一点和第二点。因为你会发现第一点和第二点对于美股市场这边影响是不一样的。什么叫不一样？

一个紧缩的货币政策，在大多数的情况下，对于美股是一个利空。也就是说，当出现一个紧缩的货币——这边非常重要，我会强调一遍——当出现一个紧缩的货币政策。因为我们都聊到了，紧缩的货币政策和强劲的经济增长都会使得真实利率往上升，但是对于美股的结果是不一样的。

为什么不一样？紧缩的货币政策不难理解，紧缩的货币政策使得真实利率往上升，使得美股会出现下跌，在不同的区间我们都可以做出这样的观察。

但是强劲的经济增长，我们思考一下，强劲的经济增长代表什么？起码代表标普500里边、美国的公司，他们背后营收非常的好、盈利非常的好，或者消费非常的好。那消费非常的好，导致这些消费公司的财报也非常的好。所以强劲的经济增长应该是利好美股，并不是利空美股。

这其实也反映了为什么养老基金会有60/40的策略：60的股市，40的债券。因为股市和债市之间有一个对冲的作用。什么叫有对冲的作用？就是当美国经济非常好的时候，我们会发现真实利率上升，那整个债券利率上升，债券的价格往下走。那所以你40%这一部分的债券，如果我们去看短时间之内的回报的话，它是往下的。但是如果经济非常的好，美股往哪儿走？美股是往上走呀。所以在经济非常好的时候，债券的收益率往上走，债券的价格往下走；同时，怎么去对冲债券的价格往下走呢？美股价格的往上走。

这也是为什么养老基金会配置更多的美股，然后相对会更少比例的债券——60的美股40的债券。因为长期来说，美国的经济是向上的，所以长期来说，你一定是布局一个回报相对更高、然后长期的时间里面会出现上涨的标的，那这个标的就是美股。

正相关 vs. 负相关的判断法则

好吧，所以这边我再重复一遍，我再强调一遍，因为这背后这个概念非常的重要：

紧缩的货币政策和强劲的经济增长都会使得真实利率往上升，但是这两个真实利率往上升对于美股的影响是不一样的：

  • 当我们看到真实利率往上升，美股也往上升，真实利率和美股之间出现一个正相关的时候，说明市场在走一个经济增长为主导的真实利率故事线。
  • 当我们看到真实利率往上升，美股在往下跌，真实利率和美股之间出现一个负相关的时候，就代表那一个时间段里面，市场交易的故事线或者逻辑线是以紧缩的货币政策为主的逻辑线。

在不同的时间区间里边，美债对于美股的影响是不一样的。

Breakeven 数据：市场其实不担心通胀

前面我和大家说到，今年到现在为止，美债名义利率的上涨几乎全部是来自于真实利率的上涨。也就是真实利率的价格在出现下跌，收益率在出现上涨。也就代表市场对于通胀其实没有担心在那边，甚至于市场对于通胀的担心是在下行的，并不是上行。

那我们来看两组数据：

  • 十年期breakeven：前面他们做的观察是在6月底。如果看6月底的话，其实6月底的价格比年初的价格利率是稍微低了一些。也就是市场对于通胀的预期和年初做对比的话，十年期通胀的预期是出现了下跌，并没有上涨。
  • 两年期breakeven：和前面完全是一样的结果。年初的时候在2.3，然后在6月底的时候是在2%。即使经历了3月份的战争事件，你会发现回过头来，市场对于通胀的担心和预期反而是下跌了。

这就是为什么，只要我们能够看懂债券市场交易出来的结果，我们就会发现，整个华尔街每天试图找原因，每天都在找为什么股市出现下跌、为什么股市出现上涨，给我们灌输各种各样的一些思考、一些思路，包括最重要的一些故事线——这些故事情往往都是错的。

真实利率与美股的历史相关性

那最后我们来把真实利率和美股放在一起。图里两条线：白色这条线是真实利率，蓝色这条线是美股。我看的是从2000年一直到2026年，因为我并没有80年代当时的数据。其实我挺想去看80年代的数据，我只能用AI去做80年代的验证。因为我知道80年代，那时候利率出现上涨、真实利率上涨、然后美股上涨，就是非常简单——我前面举的能够导致真实利率上升的第二个例子：经济增速在向好的时候、经济增速在非常快速在增长的时候，我们就会看到真实利率上涨、美债价格下跌，同时美股会出现上涨。所以利率本身是可以和美股一起上涨的，并不是所有的时候利率上涨美股都应该要下跌。

那么我们回到这张图，这张图里边下面是两者之间的相关性——就是我前面所讲到的相关性。什么时候两者之间是正相关？当经济成为影响到真实收益率主要因素的时候，我们就会看到一个正相关。就是我前面举的例子：经济好，真实利率涨，美股涨——所以两者之间就是一个正相关。

你会看到，从2000年一直到2020年之前，当下面这条线在绿色的时候就代表正相关，在红色的时候代表是负相关。所以你会看到大多数的时候，其实过去2000年到2020年之前那20年，大多数这两者之间其实是正相关，也就是说利率上涨美股上涨，利率下跌美股下跌。

当然有一点我需要提出来的是，如果你去看一个非常长的趋势的话，你会发现白色这条线利率是下跌的，蓝色这条线美股是上涨——你会得出的一个结论是负相关。这主要这个问题在于，你没办法看那么长的趋势。而我这边主要看的相关性，你得给他一个数据进去，比如说你是看过去60个交易的相关性、40个交易的相关性，所以我这边相关性观察的区间，其实是一个相对比较窄的区间。

而这一个相对比较窄的区间，反而是对于我们平时在做交易、也在做一些trade的时候，其实反而更加有帮助。而这一些相对比较长的趋势——就是20年之间20年的走势——两者之间的相关性，可以用什么去解释？可能你就可以用PE去解释，你可以用risk premium去解释都可以。那么利率下跌，导致你discount rate下跌，所以会使得美股的价格上涨，就有各式各样的理由。你可以解释一个长期来说这两者之间的负相关。

但是，如果我拿去看过去20天或者过去60天的相关性，可以非常的明显看到大多数时候两者之间是正相关的。

2020 年之后的关键转变

然后在2020年之后出现了一个转变。从2020年一直到2026年，过去6年的时间里面，你会看到好像很多个区间里面大多数时候都是负相关。负相关的时候代表什么？代表前面第一个因素成为了主导因素——第一个因素是美联储的货币政策。

也就是说美联储的货币政策，在过去的6年反而成为了一个主导因素。当美联储的货币政策在变鹰的时候，价格下跌，利率上涨，那就是非常标准的负相关。

所以22年是一个比较明确的负相关，那当时真实收益率白色这条线出现非常明显的上涨，然后蓝色这条线美股的股价出现非常明显的下跌。

2026 年 YTD：极端负相关及其含义

那如果我们把时间线放到整个2026年，可以看到，其实在差不多两周之前，整个相关性是非常非常的负相关。其实一般来说，你看负相关的话，差不多负的0.5、负的0.6，但是在两周之前，这个负相关达到了负的0.8甚至于负的0.9。相关性负的0.8到0.9是非常非常的负相关。

说明什么问题？说明美联储的货币政策反而是成为了影响了整个债券市场真实收益率和美股之间的相关性，它成为了一个主导因素。

讲到这边，很多人可能又有非常大的疑惑了：你说美联储的货币政策，那YTD到现在为止，美联储什么都没做呀？对吧，除了两周之前，凯文·沃什新任的凯文·沃什上来之后，他可能表达了一些相对比较鹰派的发言之外，你会发现前6个月美联储是什么都没做，他们也没有做任何的利率决策。

没有错，这就是美联储怎么去影响市场。

Forward Guidance 才是真正的政策

美联储去影响市场，很多时候你会发现，到底是美联储加息本身影响市场，还是美联储的言语本身影响市场，或者我们说的forward guidance？其实最终你会发现是forward guidance，是美联储的言语——包括凯文·沃什的言语其实是已经影响了市场。最终美联储是加还是不加，我不能说没有那么的重要，而是其实在讲出那句话的时候，市场就已经在帮助美联储去做事了。

举个例子：当凯文·沃什讲出了他一定要把通胀带回2%的时候，市场接下去的反应是什么？两年期的美债收益率马上就往上飙。说明什么？说明市场马上就开始定价：9月份美联储要加息了，年底前美联储就要加息了。也就是市场其实已经通过两年期美债收益率的上涨，帮助美联储做了一个紧缩的行为。

所以即使YTD到现在为止，美联储什么都没做，没有任何的货币政策，但是市场交易了什么？市场在交易——年初的时候认为2026年会降息两次，一直到现在认为2026年会加息1.5次。市场其实已经在做这一个紧缩的行为。

当然，我们不能简简单单地把YTD到现在为止的走势理解为"美债收益率上涨然后美股也上涨，所以这两者之间是一个正相关"，你没有办法这么去讲——就像我前面说，你没有办法把20年的走势放在一起去证明他两者之间是一个负相关，不能去这么去做解读。

我这边看的相关性，看的就是20个交易日rolling，就是每20个交易日你去计算出一个相关性。那么所以他看的是一个相对比较短周期的相关性。从相对比较短周期的相关性可以看出来：YTD到现在为止，其实美联储的货币政策——我打引号——虽然说货币政策他们什么都没有做，但是市场在帮美联储干活。所以市场交易出来的美联储的货币政策，其实是在影响着美股，且对于美股的影响是负相关——就是利率往上升、美股往下跌。

关键拐点：从 -0.9 向 0 的收敛

最后这张图这边我要点出来。我这边画了一个箭头，可能现在已经被埋没了，这个箭头是负相关开始出现了一个拐点。

当负相关开始出现拐点的时候，最重要的时间点是拐点的那个时间点。就从非常负的负的0.8开始出现变成负的0.6、负的0.5的时候，这个拐点相当的重要。这个拐点代表接下去可能相关性从负相关变成一个正相关。

如果我把前面那张图放大，我们放到YTD，那么整个今年年初到现在，白色和蓝色这条线其实可以看到比较明显的负相关。但是如果这个负相关开始出现转向的话，也就代表这两者之间接下去会出现一个正相关或者是没有相关性。

如果出现一个正相关或者没有相关——起码现在是在往0这个方向去走、往没有相关性的方向去走——那就代表接下去即使美债的利率开始往上升、继续往上升，不代表对于美股这边就是一个负面的影响。

对于这一个判断和观察，我再说一遍：我的观察是，首先在6月初的时候，我们可以看到这两者之间——美债的真实利率和美股之间的负相关已经达到了-0.9，是一个非常极致的数据。你但凡从负的0.9恢复到0的话，就代表这两者之间零就代表这两者之间是没有任何的相关性。那从-0.9到0这段时间里边，就代表这两者之间的走势会出现一个无规则，甚至于是正相关。

那无论是出现一个无规则，还是两者之间出现一个正相关，都代表即使接下去美债的真实收益率继续往上升，不代表对于美股的影响就是负面的。那么这是我做出的观察。

通过这个观察所做出的推论，当然就像我前面所说的，从2020年之后一直到现在过去的6年的时间里边，大多数的时候这两者之间是一个负相关。代表什么意思？大多数的时候，当我们看到真实利率往上升的时候，在那一天我们会看到的、或者我们应该有的期待是——美股会往下跌（我这边看的是标普500）。

最后总结

最后帮大家做一个总结：

今天帮大家分享的一个概念叫做真实利率。

  • 什么是真实利率？你可以理解为它是来自于TIPS市场。什么是TIPS市场？可以理解为翻译过来的话叫做美国抗通胀国债市场。顾名思义抗通胀美国国债市场：就是你对于通胀有担心的话，你就会去买；你对于通胀没有担心的话，你就卖。如果你对于通胀没有担心，当你卖出的时候，价格下跌，收益率就上涨——这是债券市场的特性。当你抛出这个债券的时候，就会发现现在价格下跌，利率就自然上涨，或者收益率就自然上涨。

  • 所以YTD到现在为止，我们看到真实利率的上涨，可以把它理解为市场对于通胀的担心是在下降的。当然了，我前面告诉大家，有另外一个解读：你也可以理解为市场对于经济的增速的预期往上抬，所以真实利率出现上升。但是你回过头去看，一季度GDP的增速1.6%、1.8%，算不上是非常好的GDP增速。所以很难得出一个结论：今年YTD到现在为止真实收益的上涨是来自于市场对于经济增速非常的乐观所导致它的上涨。这是第一个观察点。

  • 第二个观察点：你从相关性这个角度去看，今年是负相关的对吧，真实利率和美股之间是一个负相关。那我前面聊到当真实利率和美股之间负相关代表什么？代表影响到美股市场里面的一股力量，不是来自于经济增速的预期，而是来自于美联储的货币政策，或者说市场交易出来的美联储的货币政策。

  • 然后第三个可以影响到真实利率的就是美国国债的供给。

三大来源汇总： 1. 第一个来自于货币政策； 2. 第二个来自于经济增速的预期； 3. 第三个来自于美国财政部在债券市场这一块的增发。

结合美股市场的判断

最后我们还得把真实利率和美股市场所结合在一起。那么起码要对于我们美股市场这边的判断有帮助吧。

  • 首先从数据里面我们看到了，从00年到20年这20年的时间里边，其实影响到真实利率的收益率——包括真实利率收益率和美股之间的影响是什么？不是货币政策，而是整个经济增速。

  • 直到2020年之后，市场出现了转变，因为这两者之间真实利率和美股之间出现了一个负相关。当真实利率和美股之间出现负相关，就代表当利率出现上涨的时候美股要下跌，当利率下跌的时候美股要上涨。这就可以证明恰恰可能是因为美联储的货币政策影响到了市场——因为当美联储的货币政策变鹰的时候，美股自然就下跌；美联储变鸽的时候，美股就上涨。

  • 所以通过观察，我们会发现2020年至2026年，在过去6年的时间里边，这两者之间是一个负相关，也就是货币政策所主导的市场。

  • 同时我们再把这个时间点缩小，缩小到今年YTD到现在为止的走势，我们观察到大多数的时候是一个负相关，且今年我们看到在6月份的时候是极致的负相关。那当市场走到一个极致的时候，这个相关性就会出现一个反转。从极致的负相关，它反转的方向必定是不相关或者是正相关。

  • 那无论是不相关也好，还是正相关也好，这都代表了即使接下去的美债真实利率继续往上升，不代表对于美股的影响是负面的。

反问：如果真实利率下跌呢？

那最后可能部分的观众会问：如果接下去真实利率下跌呢？你说你告诉我talking，你告诉我相关性会从负相关变成一个正相关或者不相关，那如果变成一个正相关，那美债真实利率接下去如果出现下跌，是不是代表美股要下跌呢？

这个情景我是没有说过的。当利率下跌、美股下跌，市场在交易什么？其实就是交易第二个逻辑线——不是经济向好，而是经济衰退。

那为什么我没有聊这种可能性？因为从概率这个角度去看，现在我们可能不会去思考接下去经济有可能会出现衰退的这个风险。

结语

好吧，今天我讲的比较多，但今天这个内容会比较的深，但是我认为我已经讲的非常全面了。就针对这个概念然后去展开，最后跳到美股这边，聊了和美股之间的相关性。我觉得那个人还是比较深的，因为对于很多人来说，债券市场是一个非常陌生的市场。但希望今天这期内容对家有帮助。

如果大家有问题的话，我们可以在留言区里面继续交流。今天我也不做广告了，我们就到这边，我们下期见。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被误读的"沉默"：美联储什么都没做，却什么都做了
小陶在视频末尾抛出的那个"美联储什么都没做"的悖论，其实是这一期真正的洞穴入口。今年上半年美联储的政策利率没有任何变动，但真实利率却几乎独立完成了整段名义利率的上行——**这意味着市场自己在替美联储加息**。凯文·沃什一句"要把通胀压回2%"就让两年期收益率飙升，市场从"年初预期降息两次"逆转为"预期加息1.5次"。
这里有一个**反直觉**的观察：**美联储越沉默，市场的自我紧缩就越剧烈**。因为沉默让"forward guidance"的解释权外包给了市场，而市场会用最鹰派的可能性去自我保护。你会发现这几乎就是**量子物理里的"测量问题"**——观察者（市场）在等待被观察对象（美联储）表态的过程中，反而通过预期本身塌缩出了一个"实际发生的紧缩"。真正的货币政策，不在利率决议里，而在市场对沉默的解码里。
## 二、真正需要盯的不是"负相关"，而是"负相关的拐点"
绝大多数评论员会告诉你：真实利率上升→美股下跌，这是过去6年的铁律。但小陶提到了一个几乎被埋没的细节——**相关性从-0.9开始向0收敛**。这才是真正值得

item.title: [Latent Space] The 100,000 Sandbox Problem — Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、把「DX 变 AX」的隐藏含义：这不是改名，是把语言基座换掉

表面上 Modal 只是把 SDK 团队从 Developer Experience 改成 Agent Experience，听起来像营销术语。但仔细看 Akshat 讲的："why would you have an agent read through hundreds of Kubernetes files and like write YAML that's not even typed" —— 他们做的是一件更激进的事：把基础设施折叠成一种"语言语法"，让 agent 不是在"操作"云，而是在"写代码"。

这里有个反直觉的观察：过去十年 SaaS 的护城河都建立在"人机交互摩擦"上（Retool、Notion、Linear，都在优化人的注意力）。可是 agent 对摩擦的感知是完全不同的——它不会因为 UI 好看而更愿意用你，它只关心「函数签名可不可预测」和「错误信息能不能被解析」。AX 一旦成为北极星，过去十年 DevTool 的护城河可能一夜之间被反转——不是被替代，是被"重估"。Modal 早两年下注 decorator + 类型化，无意间站在了这个反转的顺风口上。

二、Modal 已经不是云公司，是「算力对冲基金」——请对比西南航空

Akshat 自己抛出了一个惊人的类比：西南航空的燃油对冲。但更深的类比其实藏在他后半句里："we have to have an opinion on how the supply chain is going to evolve and then you have to take bets."

跨领域连接来了——这个动作在金融里有个名字叫 term structure arbitrage（期限结构套利）：1 年 vs 3 年 reservation 的价差、region A 与 region B 的供需错配、H200 与 B200 的替换弹性……这不是 FP&A 的活，这是大宗商品期权定价。所以他们招人叫 "Compute Strategy"，而不是 SRE 或 Finance——这是全新工种。

再往深走一层：Modal 不建数据中心（capital-light），却掌握了 17 家 NeoCloud 的调度权。这个结构不像 AWS，倒像亚马逊零售早期的负营运资本 + 供应商现金流玩法——用软件层的确定性向硬件层要利润差。真正的护城河根本不是 GPU snapshot，是他们比任何 NeoCloud 都更懂全球算力的隐含波动率。

三、最容易被忽略的"金句"：Agent 幻觉正在变成产品路线图

Akshat 说了一句极其安静但含金量爆炸的话："If they're reaching for this thing it's product feedback. Give it to them."

Agent 因为不知道 Modal 有没有某个 CLI 指令，就"幻想"了一个，然后 Modal 团队真的去把它做出来。这意味着大模型的幻觉，正在成为一种新型的、比用户访谈快 100 倍的"聚合需求信号"。因为 LLM 的先验分布 ≈ 全体开发者心智模型的均值 —— agent 幻觉出来的功能，本质是"全人类开发者认为你理应存在的功能"。

跨领域连接：这在演化生物学里有个精确对应，叫 exaptation（扩展适应）——一个器官原本为 A 而生（比如鸟类羽毛用来保温），却被环境重新利用为 B（飞行）。Modal 讲的 I6PN 私有网络就是典型 exaptation：他们造它是为了 RDMA 的 key exchange，用户却发现 TCP overlay 层本身超好用，"literally not even in our docs page"。

惊人的推论：如果你的 API 表面积足够"生物化"（即：由小的、正交的原语组成，而不是大的封装抽象），那么用户和 agent 会一起替你"演化"出你没预料到的产品。这是产品设计的下一次范式革命——从"我为用户设计功能"到"我培育一个可被涌现使用的原语生态"。Modal 无意间证明了：谁的原语越像"氨基酸"，谁的平台越可能自组织出复杂应用。

四、彩蛋：一个几乎没人注意到的隐喻反转

播客里聊到 GitPod / Ona 转向 OpenAI 时，Akshat 轻描淡写地说："sandbox is sandbox, it's just like buildtime sandboxes versus runtime sandboxes and actually it turned out runtime was better."

这一句话，判了整个 CI/CD 时代的死刑。过去 15 年 DevOps 的核心假设是：先 build，再 run——所有价值都在构建管道里。AI 时代把这个箭头掉了个头：先 run，边 run 边 build（RL rollout、agent 自我迭代、continual learning）。所以曾经无比先进的 GitPod 反而"下错了注"。这不是竞争失败，这是认识论的相变。

📝 转录文本

Cold Open — DX vs AX

We've actually changed our SDK team to think about agent experience instead of developer experience. And we think that the same benefits that apply for DX also actually apply for AX — which is: why would you have an agent read through hundreds of Kubernetes files and write YAML that's not even typed, when it can basically make a couple changes in a decorator and get this sort of self-provisioning runtime of being able to see its changes live in action?

Sponsor Note

Before we get into today's episode, I just have a small message for listeners. Thank you. We would not be able to bring you the AI engineering, science, and entertainment content that you so clearly want if you didn't choose to also click in and tune into our content.

We've been approached by sponsors on an almost daily basis. But fortunately, enough of you actually subscribe to us to keep all this sustainable without ads, and we want to keep it that way. But I just have one favor to ask all of you. The single most powerful, completely free thing you can do is to click that subscribe button. It's the only thing I'll ever ask of you, and it means absolutely everything to me and my team that works so hard to bring the Inspace to you each and every week. If you do it, I promise you, we'll never stop working to make the show even better. Now, let's get into it.

Intro — Akshat, CTO of Modal

We're here with Akshat of Modal, CTO of Modal, together with Vivu. Congrats on your Series C.

Thank you.

Your party yesterday was amazing — all the photos and all the swag.

Yeah, we had a bunch of art installations, which is kind of fun — seeing our products on pedestals next to Rodin.

Very nice, very nice.

The Origin Story

When you started, it was not the GPU inference company. I mean maybe it was in your mind. Take us back to the origin story.

I actually first met Eric — who's the CEO — through an investor. And back then Eric was already thinking about building a new kind of runtime. He got there thinking through: why are workflow orchestration products so hard to use? It's because you have to run them on Kubernetes. Kubernetes is hard to manage. It's not built for burstiness and custom images and has a terrible developer experience.

And I'll inject for listeners who are new — we interviewed Eric two years ago and there's a bit more of the story there from Spotify and all those things. I actually came across Eric through Data Council, because he did that talk on the sort of serverless container stack that you guys did — which was my first "okay, I need to take Modal very seriously" moment. But it was still very unclear: do I actually need all this for just my data pipelines?

Yeah. Initially what we were thinking about was: if we build a better runtime, it's a very useful primitive in itself. There's a lot of things that get solved by serverless functions — you can do ETL stuff, you can do job queues, you can do all this bursty processing, which it turns out every company had needs for.

But then we were also thinking about this as a primitive that we can build a whole collection of products on. So perhaps data engineering would have been the first one, but we were thinking about inference — back then it was more classical inference, like computer vision stuff and running XGBoost and whatnot. But we added GPUs to the product a year before ChatGPT came out. We just didn't think it would be that big of a deal.

Yeah. Just like, add A100. Was there any early key problem that really sparked off why you built it?

Yeah, primarily it's just — none of the tooling that was out there was built for a really great developer experience. And also there's a general trend of a lot of the workloads that we were seeing being very... I wish there's a better word for it, but compute-heavy — like they need a lot more resources, so you need to burst up and down a lot, versus Kubernetes designed for slow scaling and more for web server use cases. And also there's just a lot more specialization in what kinds of environments these workloads run in. Sometimes they need accelerators, sometimes need different kinds of images. And this is just a consistent thing we saw across a lot of companies.

Software-Defined Infrastructure & The Decorator Insight

That would be the next step. Be nice — I don't know how much this factored into the early story, but I wrote a post when I was at Temporal about software-defined infrastructure, or something like that.

The self-provisioning self.

Yeah. I couldn't even remember my own post and then you put me on the landing page.

Yeah, we really like the term, and so we stole it.

Because you had the insight that everything can just be in decorators, co-located with the code, right? Was that a big part of the original story, or was it just like a DX layer?

That was really important, because we really didn't want people to spend so much time writing YAML. And it seemed like you could really condense the surface area of what you're doing, put it in code so you can actually operate on it — just like you can operate in other code, build stuff that's more expressive and dynamic. So yeah, that was always a very important part.

Then the pushback is: this is a DSL, it's closed source, I am locked in to Modal.

Yeah, we never really got pushback for that, because the nice thing about Modal is you can bring whatever code you have. And sure, the DSL is the configuration layer for what hardware you're using, how you're scaling things up, but you still own the code. And that's been an important part of our story even as we do inference now.

From DX to AX

How much of it do you think still stays the same today? Like if you were to build something today, DevEx obviously very important — but I feel like a lot of this has kind of been changed with "just hook it up to an agent, have Claude Code, have Codex implement a tool." There's very agent-native primitives that are kind of different than if I'm doing this myself, right?

We've actually changed our SDK team to think about agent experience instead of developer experience. And we think that the same benefits that apply for DX also actually apply for AX — which is: why would you have an agent read through hundreds of Kubernetes files and write YAML that's not even typed, when it can basically make a couple changes in a decorator and get this sort of self-provisioning runtime of being able to see its changes live in action?

From the customers we talked to, they actually find Modal is way faster used for agents versus operating on a different substrate.

Yeah — because again, you co-locate the infrastructure requirements to the code that runs it.

Well, the negative thesis now is that nobody's looking at their code anymore. So there's no point.

Yeah. I mean people aren't looking at code.

One thing we actually still see is really important is observability. How good is your dashboard? Of course, we push a lot of it to the CLI so the agents can do their own investigation, but you still need humans to go interpret what's going on and make judgment calls. And that's, I feel like, maybe more important now than looking at the code itself.

Yes — because you can try to treat the code as a black box and then see the observable action that comes out of it, and just prompt a change.

What Is Modal For?

Yeah. So I actually think it takes a bit of restraint to not specialize — to say "I want to ship a new primitive" and then just be general purpose. People ask you "what are you for?" — you're like "I don't know, we can do this, we can do that." I'd be curious: if we were to ask you, even at a high level, what is Modal for? There's a lot you guys do — sandboxes, GPUs, everything. How do you answer?

Modal is a cloud platform where we've built the primitives from scratch for AI applications. Right now it basically covers inference, training, batch processing, and sandbox workloads — but we're building a lot more.

I noticed you didn't say "web server" — so there is still a role for the always-on, large-scale Kubernetes-type things.

Yeah, absolutely. We're not trying to compete with the Renders of the world. Because we think the differentiator for us is our other workloads that need specialized compute, need to scale up and down a lot. They're shaped differently.

I think you're building a lot of it alongside the startups, right? They're innovating quite a bit. Even in your latest blog post, even in the Series C — the customers you mentioned here, the Cognitions, Decagons, Ramps and whatnot — they're innovating with you, right? And that's not something AWS is doing directly.

Yeah, absolutely. I mean this is again classic: we're a small team, we can move really fast. Our engineers are working with their customers and figuring things out.

Aside — Cognition, smol developer, and Sandbox Origins

My first week at Cognition I walked in — there was someone wearing a Modal shirt. I was like, "what are you doing here?" They're like, "yeah, I just — I am embedded inside of Cog."

Yeah, I think that was Payton. We sent him over because the latency of communication was too high otherwise.

Um, yeah — distributed node. You have to place one to co-locate.

So actually, I had a direct personal experience, right? I worked on smol developer 3 years ago. It was inspired by Cloud One. I think you onboarded me at some point, just before. And I was like, "oh, I need some bursty compute — I was just going to try using Modal." And it was a pretty pleasant experience. Apparently I showed up in the board meeting like the analytics.

Yeah, you blew up on Hacker News and we got a big traffic spike. I think the way you used smol developer was Modal functions for running stuff — which was a good use case.

Yeah — that was, to me, proto-cognition, right? If only I had stuck to it. If you just draw the tech tree out — like, yeah, probably this will happen.

Yeah, like he was so close.

But the funny story there is: at the same time we were talking to a bunch of customers who needed something like sandboxing. This is like 2023.

So you needed a new API right after that.

Yeah, we built sandboxes in May of 2023, before anyone even knew this was going to be a thing. And the first example we published was: we took smol developer and put it in a loop, so the agent can iterate on itself.

Loops are hard these days.

Loops in — when was this, 2023?

Yeah — smol chick.

Yeah, it's like mid 2023.

I mean, obviously for listeners, the problem was the models are not built for any of this, right? Like — you're trying — they're not post-trained to understand looping and self-correction, and tool calling was there but also not that great.

Yeah, I don't remember if you used tool calling in this one, but yeah, the models would just diverge after like 10 iterations and not produce anything meaningful.

Yeah. But then — okay, now, talking to myself three years ago — the answer is: collect all the failures, build a benchmark, and then collect all the examples, build the RL environment, sell it for like $10 billion to Meta, and then also train a model and then sell that for $60 billion to Elon. And this is a money machine. It's actually not that hard.

I mean, it's hard to have that kind of inherent conviction that this stuff will get that much better.

In retrospect, it's so obvious. Like, fair enough. What else were we doing back then? I don't know. Anyway.

The Quiet Years of Sandboxing

So that was the start of your sandboxing journey, right? I feel like it didn't blow up until last year. So there was a couple years of quietness.

Exactly. Yeah.

Very underrated product value. My experience with Modal — Charles, before he had joined Modal, met this guy at a hackathon, and he really insisted we wanted to run some small model, not hosted anywhere. And he's like, "ah, there's this cool company Modal, they'll spin up a GPU sandbox so we can throw it on there — it'll take a Hugging Face link" — and there's so much value just right there. Instant hosting, spin it up, spin it down, it'll stay cold, but you know, we run the demo a few days later it'll come back up. All this stuff in retrospect is still what we needed today.

Yeah, it's still needed today. Obviously workload shapes have changed a lot — we run stuff for people with really massive production scale. And there, it's not about scaling from 0 to 1, but how do we scale really elastically from 1,000 to 1,500 GPUs very quickly in a given region. It's the same shaped problem.

Elastic Inference — The First Product-Market Fit

Okay. So you look at, say, Cursor Composer, right? They had a "we'll do RL on a model every couple hours." You guys have a whole version of RL inference gym and whatnot. When you look at workloads like that, you're basically doing train runs where you need to scale up, scale down every hour — thousands of GPUs, right? That's the example for "we do need it," right?

Yeah — well, actually, I'll take a step back and maybe talk about how people use Modal today. Because our biggest use case actually is elastic inference. And the thing we first found product-market fit with was inference for custom models. So we kind of stayed away from the LLM space.

We were serving companies like Suno for audio, Runway for video, robotics, comp-bio companies that train their own model elsewhere. But Modal is the best black box for deployment — scaling to however many GPUs you need as your traffic pattern changes.

We saw all of them actually have very unpredictable traffic patterns. Some days the company will do a launch and they'll need way more, and it's not just one model that they deploy — all these companies deploy lots of different models in different regions. So the autoscaling problem becomes even harder because you have to scale within a certain region, and those cycles are offset, so different times you need to scale up in different regions.

And that in and of itself is a huge category. There's a bunch of inference providers — Fireworks does this as a service, Together, whatnot, Baseten — that's kind of carved into its own niche for language models, at least right now.

Yeah. The thing that we have actually specialized in is the autoscaling aspect. Because we found that it's not universally true that everyone else can autoscale. And we've gone deeper into it on the tech side — we've incorporated GPU snapshotting into the product, so you can actually take the GPU state, like your torch-compiled model, snapshot it, and next cold starts way faster.

So going back to your question: that's why you need a lot of burstiness for inference. But then people also do a lot of on-demand training. Like for RL stuff, your rollouts are bursty, as you said. People also do a lot of batch jobs. We'll see a lot of companies, before they have a training run, they'll need thousands of GPUs to run encoding or something like that. And I think those things are much more bursty than — I agree that agents are not that bursty. Sandboxes are, except when you're doing RL. RL is insanely bursty.

Yeah.

Like when you're doing rollouts, you sometimes need 100,000 sandboxes.

Continual Learning & Frontier Customers

Yeah. I'm curious if you've seen early sparks of continual learning. There's some people, like our friends Engram, recently announced this. They're trying to do training. That also seems like a different workload, right? If you're doing training 24/7, there's a very weird dynamic of how you're using GPUs between people and whatnot — but seems like something you guys would work for.

As you said, we're fortunate to work with a number of customers at the frontier. And they are taking the primitives we have and trying to use them in very interesting ways, like continual learning. It's possible as the stuff gets better, some of that will be part of our offering as well, if more people need it. But we're just waiting to see how all this shakes out.

Going Deeper into LLM Inference — Deflash & Speculative Decoding

Is there a primitive that you added after sandboxing that was the next step in the story?

I guess we've been going much deeper into LLM inference, because we realize that some of the advantages we have with autoscaling — again especially in different regions and whatnot — are not present elsewhere.

And the place where we had a gap was: we weren't working on the model layer itself. Like, we were a black box. And we realized that we actually can get to frontier-level model performance by having great people who work on all of this. And we've been open-sourcing a lot of our work. Recently we shared our work on Deflash, which is a block-based speculator, and we've open-sourced all of it. So by using open-source Deflash you can get the same performance as you would with one of the proprietary providers.

I thought this was actually an interesting blog post as well. In here you make a claim about how effective speculative decoding really is. Anything you want to point out from this around what people should know?

Yeah, absolutely. The high-level summary — would it help to describe what speculative decoding is?

Yes. We've covered Eagle and all this, Hydra and all those things, but it was like two years ago. I think it doesn't hurt.

Speculative decoding is: you have a smaller model — called a draft model — predict tokens ahead of the bigger model. And then you have the bigger model verify all the tokens that are predicted.

The reason it's faster is: if you're predicting one token at once, you're kind of bound by memory bandwidth. But if you can batch the verification of the draft model, then you're much more efficient using compute, and it's faster. And as long as your draft model is producing a lot of tokens that can get accepted — which is called the accept length — you can get speed-up that's multiple times of the original model speed.

That's what we highlight here. People talk a lot about "we made these kernels faster" and whatnot — but improving kernels only gives you a few percentage points of improvement, and increasing accept length literally is a multiplicative decrease. Like 2 to 4x without much heads-on performance.

Yeah. I mean, you are running a second model, right? So maybe it's more expensive in compute — but I meant quality.

There's no drop in quality performance, because you're never accepting a token that — better or same.

Exactly. Right.

And so we've been working a bunch on Deflash, which is a block-based speculator. Instead of predicting one token at a time, it's predicting a block. We've been open-sourcing our work with it.

Auto Endpoints

The next thing for us here is helping people train speculators and custom models. It's something that traditionally is very FDE-driven, support-deployed-engineer-driven — like you work with customers and help them do that. And our vision for this is why we launched Auto Endpoints — we want to make frontier-level performance available to everyone.

We mentioned this announcement, we kind of teased it. The next thing we're launching is: basically, as you run an Auto Endpoint, we shadow traffic and —

Do you want to explain Auto Endpoints at a high level?

Yeah. So going back to your "Modal is — you touch the code" — sometimes people actually don't want to touch the code, and they want to get started with an endpoint that works and has all the great performance and scalability that Modal has.

We've made that easier with basically a way to create an endpoint from our UI, from the CLI, that has all of our optimizations that we talked about — like the Deflash stuff — already baked in. And there's full transparency: we give you the code, you can go run it yourself, and if you want, you can eject out into the full Modal experience. Which we see as people get sophisticated — they do want to tweak the models, they want to fine-tune stuff. You can still do all of that. It's not a black box.

And the next thing, as we tease later in the post, is: how do we give you value even beyond this in terms of having your draft models evolve as your data distribution evolves — again without having to talk to a person.

The Delta vs Rolling Your Own

I guess just to kind of understand it directly — you have the GPUs, you have an endpoint that's compatible, you serve open models. If someone was to do this themselves, what's the delta that you guys provide? You do a lot of open-source great work on effective inference — how does it compare to, say, I take the same model GLM 5.2 FP8, take off-the-shelf inference engine vLLM, SGLang, get compute of similar capacity, similar cost — what's the kind of delta that plugging into something like this offers, outside of the benefit of scaling?

It's kind of interesting because we've taken the approach of open-sourcing our contributions and upstreaming them. We work closely with the SGLang team. We actually want the improvements that our team comes up with to be there and open-source for others to use, even outside of Modal.

The benefit to us is: we have a team that has significant expertise, so if you do have something that is not there, our team can help you get that performance first.

The other thing is: with these endpoints, we are way more elastic — as you said — than anyone else. You have true scaling to zero, you have true burstiness. And in practice that matters a lot more to people than just finding the GPU and running model code on it.

Yeah, and I will say it's actually not that straightforward. What I said is easier said than done, right? I think, still, for the average person, still hard to just gut-check using different — there's quite a bit of combinations you can make there. The tradeoffs aren't really known at face value.

Yeah. I mean it's not just that. Running production-grade inference is a hard infra problem, even if you subtract out the autoscaling — controlling things like tail latency, making sure every request is delivered at least once, and whatnot. There's a lot of innovation you can do here.

What Won't Modal Do?

I think it's very interesting that you're starting to encroach on — as you become a full cloud, you're starting to encroach on other people's turf. What will you not do?

Well, we want to follow our users and make sure they get a platform that has everything that works well together. So right now we're kind of focused on the model lifecycle and the agent lifecycle. So both going from data prep to training to inference, and then also if I want to deploy a background agent — let's say sandbox, persistent storage, a whole bunch of other stuff.

We talked to Cole who did OpenInspect.

Yeah. And obviously RAMP Inspect also is on Modal.

Yeah.

So RAMP Inspect was a great example of a background agent that was really successful, because they were able to use some of the primitives like snapshotting and fast scaling to make this something that feels really reactive and works well.

Yeah, that's the new CTO of RAMP right there.

Yeah, Rahul. It was really really fun.

The Inference Inflection & 17 Cloud Providers

Yeah. I mean, I'm all very bullish. One of my reflections was also — I did not originally, because obviously when I met you guys you weren't that much in the GPU game, and now you're all about inference. And one of the points that I hinged on for Jensen's keynote at GTC this year was what we're calling the "inference inflection" — that in AI workloads or machine learning workloads it used to be, let's call it, 8:1 GPU to CPU, and now it's more like 1:1. Which is interesting, because of how much agents basically are blocked or call out to CPU-heavy stuff, the actual limiting factor swings back and forth from GPU to CPU a lot more than it used to be — all GPU and then occasional CPU, GPU-CPU. And now it's like just constantly, and you just have to co-locate everything.

Yeah. That's one of the things that — again, we see — is something appealing about Modal, which is: we've built this capacity pool that spans 17 cloud providers. So we're very good at running on various kinds of cloud capacity across the world.

You don't have your own data centers.

We don't have our own data centers. We just run across a lot of NeoClouds and providers.

Question. Yeah, you're running the math and you're like — what's the cutover point where you're like...

Yeah, it's a good question. I mean, part of it is: we see our differentiator in the software layer. And being capital-light and focusing on the software helps us move really fast. So far it's worked out well because there are so many other people building data centers that we're able to work effectively with them and focus on what makes us special.

Yeah. 17 gets you into like the local providers sometimes. Who's the most interesting one?

There are actually a lot more NeoClouds than you expect, and they all have various degrees — various levels of reliability. And that's why something we've invested a lot of time in is actually building our own reliability layer on top. So if the GPU falls off the bus or something happens, user workloads are not affected. And that actually lets us use a lot more capacity than you as a user would be able to.

Data Locality, Co-location, and the Perfect Agent Primitive

It's a useful thing to have. Because now everyone knows what layer you are, and you sort of optimize for being the "super cloud of all clouds."

Yeah, that's the idea. And so I guess when you mentioned co-location — that's another interesting thing where one thing we've seen is: people come to us when they want very specifically located CPUs or GPUs. Like they want —

Oh, they pin it in like EU.

Exactly. Or EU-US.

Data locality thing? Or performance? Or what?

It's either data locality or latency. Like you want your — they're running sandboxes in Modal — they want them to be right next to...

That's a — you know...

That is important in all those things.

And so, like, you've kind of accidentally — I don't know if it's accident, but like — you've built the perfect primitive for agents to express themselves. And then, it's almost very funny how every extra development just involves more file system, just involves more CPU — just the things that you already have. I don't know if there's any networking usages that are interesting, but you've also done some good work on networking.

Yeah, that's exactly right. We're sort of just taking compute, storage, and networking and building stuff on that layer for, again, the stuff people need.

Networking — Sidecars, I6PN, and RDMA

We see a few interesting network things coming up. One is: people actually want sandboxes — so we have a —

For like a Docker cluster type thing? Sorry — Docker swarm — what is it called?

Compose.

Compose-type thing.

Yeah. So actually, if you want Docker Compose, our sandboxes now support this thing called sidecar. So a sandbox is actually a pod of containers, and you can run multiple containers in the sandbox.

Also useful because — going back to networking — people want a lot of control over outbound networking from a sandbox. Might want to run man-in-the-middle proxy for logging stuff for RL, or controlling how egress can happen to a domain, injecting credentials. So we've kind of had to build a lot of that stuff ourselves.

But then also sometimes people actually want sandboxes spanning multiple nodes to talk to each other. Which is an emerging thing we're seeing. We have support for that for a different reason, and we'll see if that becomes safe.

Like just an open socket? Like NTLS?

We do support that, which is: you can expose a tunnel inside a sandbox and then either expose to public internet, or add a HTTP auth layer above it.

But we have this thing called I6PN, which we haven't talked about — this is like an overlay network using IPv6 addresses. So if Modal containers within the same workspace have this enabled, they can actually address each other using this private IPv6 address, and no one else can. It's like sort of private networking for containers.

We actually built it because we needed it as a primitive for our distributed training product. We have this other feature which is: you can add a decorator to a function and you get a cluster of GPUs, and they have RDMA networking, so you can run a dist training job that's truly serverless. And we need the overlay network for that. But then we've seen that people are using it for other reasons.

Yeah — what would people do with it?

Build primitives and let people figure it out.

They read the docs — "let me use that for something you never intended." This is literally not even in our docs page. People somehow found it, and they're using it.

I mean, the way you portrayed it with RDMA versus TCP — very well laid out. But just the transfer speed change at scale for RL, like — yeah, you have it built in. I'm sure someone found it to be a lot more efficient before you actually made a thing out of it, right?

Yeah. And not to split hairs, I guess: the overlay network actually is the TCP overlay network. The reason we have that is you need it to do the key exchange for RDMA before you set up the RDMA network on top of that. But then people found the TCP part.

Aside — John Ousterhout and RDMA

Can I tell you — this is like a big aha moment for me. So, you know, I reviewed 2,200 submissions for the World's Fair.

Yeah.

And then I got this from John Ousterhout, who — I don't know if you know John Ousterhout by name. He published — he's a well-known professor, published a lot of interesting software design books. And this is the talk he chose to submit — it's on RDMA at Imprint. And I'm like: you wouldn't think that this guy who is kind of an operating-systems guy would care about RDMA.

I mean it makes sense to me, because — cloud, right? — the way you move around your KV cache and how efficiently you can do it, how efficiently you move your weights from your training GPUs to your inference GPUs — and RL — there's a lot of degrees of freedom. And it is basically a systems problem of moving memory around, scheduling.

This shows you how primitive my understanding of networking stuff is. Is this like the domain of WireGuard as well? Not quite so? Adjacent?

Uh, so — explain everything. Sure sure. How do we move memory around GPUs?

Oh, sorry — yeah, that is memory. Sorry, I was talking maybe 5 minutes back about the private IPv6 addressing that you've set up.

Is it basically a VPN? Yeah, it's sort of like a VPN. And yeah, WireGuard is — you're right, it is in the same space. WireGuard is encrypted, and this is —

You don't need encryption.

Yes, it's not encrypted. That's the main difference. This is TCP, and we have eBPF programs that will reject or allow the TCP connection based on whether you're allowed to do it. Used to involve a full sidecar, but now you have eBPF in the Linux kernel.

Distributed Training & the RDMA Bottleneck

Yeah. Yeah. I don't know if this is a natural follow-on to the topic of my skepticism on distributed training — is that, well, people spend a lot of money on cables to hook up GPUs, and even that is not fast enough, and that's the bottleneck. Is your networking fast enough?

Yeah. So I guess you're talking about sort of fully distributed training, like a DiLoCo or something, which is cross —

That would be — yes, that's the extreme. Yeah. You're kind of in the middle. And then other people would have the Mellanox cables up in their actual data center.

When you run multi-node training on Modal, the RDMA — I think Mellanox / InfiniBand also uses RDMA — basically it's a way to bypass the TCP networking stack and transfer stuff much faster between one node to the other. And we have, I think, like 3 terabytes per second internal networking, which is the standard that's needed.

Okay, so I misunderstood what part of the stack was TCP. Okay.

Yeah.

I mean, very impressive work. So effectively you're extending sort of the Modal philosophy to the training cluster, like —

Yeah. And we're not going for obviously large-scale pretraining runs. The thing that we've built multi-node training for is — we see a lot of smaller-scale post-training. Like people are post-training medium-sized Qwen models so they can get higher quality on inference. This is a perfect fit for something like that.

Yeah. That is my impression of how a lot of these labs explore branches in post-training and then eventually merge whatever they find in.

Yeah. The other use case we've seen for multi-node training is: even if you have a big cluster, your researchers are still doing small runs. And having elasticity there matters a lot more. Like, this is actually the current limiting factor for auto-research, which is: you basically need to give your model some GPUs.

We have a blog post on auto-research, and Modal is — yeah, turns out to be a pretty good substrate for that.

Auto-Research

So my impression is auto-research means many things. Like — if anything coins right now, still science fair, right? Like, not actually — I don't know how many people actually doing this.

Yeah, I thought the same thing.

You would know.

We like — our internal both training and inference teams actually use this sort of shape quite a bit. Like we have this one internal repo called auto-inference, which is essentially: we've automated our own FDE efforts using this harness. The agent will just spin up a sweep of different things, it'll even run Nvidia Insight profiler, it'll tweak configs, and it'll arrive at the right thing. It'll change your GPUs from H200 to B200, and actually works really well.

Nice.

By the way, I enjoy that your FDEs are so technical that you have to do these things. It's very different from FDs from other people.

Yeah, our FDE team is essentially applied inference researchers or applied training researchers.

Someone told me they have to be able to build but they also have to be able to sell. Do they have to sell? Or are they — like, this is post-sale type of thing.

Being able to talk to a customer and engage effectively with them matters a lot — the same thing. But it's not really a sort of sales thing. We pair them with — we have solutions architects as well that are more on the pre-sales side.

More on Auto-Research

Okay, let's spend a bit more time on auto-research. This is a big focus for me for this year. Where does this go? Have people explored enough? There's all these beautiful charts of "improve, improve, then it sort of levels off a bit, and then you find the next thing." Is this basically one abstraction up from normal training? Is that how we think about it? Or do you think about it differently — like model-level training versus basically AI-driven hyperparameter search? Some people call it neural architecture search or whatever, right?

I mean, the stuff I've seen people do with it is nowhere on the architecture level. It's pretty much tweaking parameters. But it's basically a hyperparameter sweep that's guided by some sort of model intuition. So it's much more efficient than whatever other sweeper you would have.

Yeah. I mean, it's just a question of where you want to spend your compute. Because you can just throw infinite amounts of money on this and somehow you'll bang on Shakespeare.

Yeah. Internet monkey.

Yeah. I mean, so — very good for Modal. And I think it's also very important that agents can spin up other agents. They can spin up their own infrastructure. Very good for you.

How Good Are LLMs at Modal Code?

How good are LLMs at generating Modal code? Like, the benefit of existing pre-LLM is that you are in the data.

Yeah, they're actually surprisingly good. I think, like, pre-Claude 4, they were not. And then now they're able to one-shot stuff out of the box. We're playing around with releasing like a Modal Bench for the harder things that the LLMs cannot do yet.

What's an example of that?

I think the things that sometimes agents struggle with, without right guidance and a skill, is: how to use the rest of our observability. Like, something is failing — how do you look at the logs and then update the right thing? It's sort of reasoning about that.

But they're able to one-shot, like — yeah, you can just add a skill to it?

Yeah. So we have a Modal skill now — which is kind of actually why we built this Modal Bench: it's to find things like that so we can address them in our tuning skill.

Capacity, Reservations, and Airline Analogies

Yeah. No, I mean it's good. Are you facing any shortages? You know, we talk a lot about GPU shortages, but also CPU, also memory.

Yeah, we have had a lot of growth, which means that we've had to be much better about proactive capacity planning.

Yeah — which, by the way, is like an MBA's dream job. It's just planning this stuff. I think last time you and I talked about something maybe about this.

Yeah. We have a really competent team of people that we call — the role is called Compute Strategy. So if anyone listening here wants to work on —

Compute Strategy. Yeah, yeah.

I mean, the normies call it FP&A or something.

Well, it's not FP&A. There's a lot of interesting financial questions of like: what is the blend between one-year and three-year reservations? How do we forecast our own capacity? How do we basically — especially since our capacity is very fungible across different GPU types and different regions — you basically have to model a lot of it. And you also have to have an opinion on how the supply chain is going to evolve, and then you have to take bets based on that.

Tokenomics. Yeah. This is like probably not a real point, but I was trying to think about what other industries — we cannot be first to these kinds of problems. And what other industries have had this. And I was like: airlines with fuel. They have to hedge their fuel. And I think for a long time Southwest, because they made like a hero fuel bet, they were super low cost compared to everyone else.

Yeah, I had thought about that. We're having a fun time too, you know. Yeah. A lot of the compute business in general for us is also about being very good about capacity management. That is how you have great unit economics.

But also over time, how you can unlock more value for customers. Like, one of the things we're building now is a way for customers to — if they don't care about latency, get much cheaper pricing and they'll get results back in the next 24 hours or something. Like a batch tier essentially. And those are levers we have because we control the whole stack and scheduling, and whatnot, to give people a sufficient —

Yeah. I feel like they're not as popular. Like, the frontier labs have all those APIs — they're not as popular as they should be.

The demand that we see for something like that is actually not for LLMs. Although sometimes people want to run evals and do synthetic data prep, and there it makes sense. But it's from a lot of non-LLM companies — like people who are doing computational bio. They have really big batch jobs and they don't care about when they get it back.

Yeah. It's also like a cousin to the stopping problem of: will this finish in time?

Yeah. You can bound it. You can give people —

What's Next for Modal

Yeah. Yeah. I think what's interesting is: the next phase of Modal — what do people expect from you now that you're sort of established and you're well-known compute player among all these leading companies? You had an inference launch week and we talked a little bit about the launches. What else should people know?

We are building primitives that make our users' lives much easier. So, for example, with LLM inference — thousands more companies are going to post-train their own models and deploy open-source models for inference. So we're thinking a lot about what is the best product shape for that. And that involves everything from our training gym to endpoints that get frontier-level performance, again, without having to talk to anyone.

It looks somewhat different on other verticals. We're also seeing a lot of real-time audio-video stuff — which is why we're working on things like regional routing with fallbacks, so you can get GPUs that are as close to users as possible, so you get low latency for video streaming and whatnot.

And then on the agent side, we're still sort of working very closely with our customers because stuff is changing so fast in terms of what they need. And I think beyond sandboxes and persistent file systems, there's a lot of other things people need from this agent stack as they build production agents. So we're thinking about those other things that fit in there.

I want to ask what the other things are.

Yeah, probably share right now — I think —

Compute, Storage, Networking — Do Agents Need New Primitives?

Okay, so I do think a lot about the principal components of cloud, and you do talk about compute, storage, networking. Because so far, for me, it's fine — for I mean, the first couple generations of cloud it's fine. What's different — qualitatively different — about agents, that you need some new permission level? Like a lot of people, obviously — and I'll just kind of spew tokens at you until it sparks something.

Like, the new level now is whatever Claude Code does, which is "dangerously skip permissions" or "allow-list by command" or whatever, right? And sometimes they're like, "well, okay, well, we have this adaptive thinking mode where — just trust me, bro. I will make the calls for you." Is that it? Like, sort of LLM-mediated permission?

Now you're looping it with a goal and flooding row.

Yeah.

I mean, I'm skeptical of LLM-mediated permission for stuff that is at the sandbox level, because you do want hard boundaries.

Yeah, obviously someone can exfiltrate stuff. But maybe that's old-school thinking. Maybe we're the dinosaurs. Maybe the AI OS or the LLM OS is really — the kernel is a goddamn LLM. Like, it makes you feel uncomfortable, but that's what trusting the LLM is. Imagine a spherical cow perfect LLM, right? Let it — maybe — I want to test the boundaries, right? Obviously I don't believe that, but I want to see where — I'm that's the non-consensus.

Yeah. I mean, I think you always need hard guardrails. And you can pair those with softer guardrails, right? And ask an LLM and mediate it.

Managed Agents & The Ecosystem

Yeah. I get end with a couple of your commentary on the ecosystem outside of Modal. Managed agents — everyone has one. Gemini, OpenAI, Cloud. Very useful for you, but also it is their way of starting to edge into your space. What's going on?

Yeah. I mean, we're very excited to partner with Anthropic and some of the other foundation labs. Building is a great place to start if you're starting out building an agent.

But then when you get to building something more production-grade — like you're a company that's like RAMP that's building their own — RAMP also runs their accounting agent on us, part of their external-facing agent — you need a lot more control over your compute primitive. On things like: how do you process different files that the agent has access to, how do you snapshot and restore, how do you control the networking, maybe you want GPUs.

When you get to that point, you kind of want a specialized sandbox provider that gives you those things. And that's the role that we are trying to play. We don't really have an opinion on the harness — whether it's a cloud-managed agent and you hook it up to Modal sandbox, or you run the harness in Modal sandbox. We'll see where people converge with that.

Yeah. Do you have any opinions on the meta-harnesses? Just another layer on top of these things.

You mean like the OpenPie and —

OpenPie is one. I think Vercel had one which I can't remember the name of right now. Fred Shot had one. And then to me, most recently, was Databricks that had Omni-Agent. All these are sort of meta-harnesses — like, kind of pseudo-agent-cloud type things.

I personally have not played around with them and built into that.

I mean, everything's bullish Modal as long as it consumes more infra. That's why we're focusing on the infra layer. It's somewhere where our relative competence is, and also it's a hard problem to solve.

But what else you know — what else is coming up for us, or where do you see things going?

Yeah, I mean in general, it's clear that there's a huge shift happening. One thing that's not as obvious to people, because LLM inference gets talked about so much: we work a lot of companies that are doing things like drug discovery and computational bio — like the Chai Discovery worlds — big things are probably going to happen there. We work a lot of robotics companies that are actually putting robots in active deployments and getting good results out of them.

Is there air-gapped Modal? Is there a version that is like on-prem, air-gapped, whatever?

No, we're cloud only.

Okay. But yeah, I mean — so what you're saying is, because you're focused on primitives and they're good primitives, you find use cases and all these kinds of things. Actually probably diversifies you a little bit away from LLM all the time.

Yeah, absolutely. Our goal isn't to only serve the LLM-first market.

Replicate Post-Mortem — API vs Product

Just on the website — the audio, the bolts, on the bio images, yeah, I mean there's a lot here. There's Qwen, TTS, customizing — oh, Chatterbox — you know, there was a customizing Whisper. Yeah, yeah. This screen reminds me of a fallen competitor — Replicate. What's your post-mortem on what happened?

This is one thing we've kind of stayed away from is providing an API for models. Because I think providing model APIs — some of it ends up serving like a really hobbyist market, which is much less sticky. And we've always wanted to build for companies that are building products and need more flexibility that's not just an API.

Which — you can build an API for a model, and this is clearly what it is, but you can — but you're saying you can wrap it into a more fully functioning back-end that you run.

Yeah. So actually, all of our examples — it's not that "spin up this model, here's an API token, use it." They're actually all code.

Okay.

And so the point is that this is an example — starter code. But you can tweak it however you want. And if you're like a company building a product like computational bio, whatnot —

When Does a Wrapper Become a Product?

I guess I'm trying to tease out for listeners: when does it stop becoming "oh, you're just an API call and you're just a wrapper on an API" to becoming what you call a product? What is that layer like? Obviously more lines of code, but beyond that, what is the substance that people add that qualifies it to be something more?

I think there's a little bit of a selection effect of: a lot of companies who do want to get deeper into that level are probably building something that's more differentiated. An example is: with LLMs and friends, originally we worked with companies that were building their own post-training frameworks, or they were — RAMP, actually, early in the day, was training their own tokenizer and swapping out the tokenizer in Llama and whatnot. I'm not saying that was successful — in that case.

A better example is like — let's say, because Cursor uses Modal for — well, Cursor doesn't use us for training, MicKey on a pod — but they use Modal for all their inference. And that's because they have a completely custom model architecture, and that means they have to be at the code level and tweak things that are not "you know, it's an API."

Aside — Video Models as Agents

It's interesting as well. We had Ethan most recently on the XAI Grok team make a prediction that actually, the next tier in video model is a better model or agent that orchestrates video models — language-model backbone that can use tools and write code. Like, "yes, I can make my 6-second video or my 10-second video from Grok, but actually I want my 6-minute video, and I'm not going there through normal video-gen."

Yeah, that's interesting. I actually — so we have GPU sandboxes, and recently have seen a few companies doing sort of agents that do video manipulation.

Give it Photoshop, like — that's not — you need to give it Adobe.

Yeah. I hadn't put it together with — it would actually be a video production thing. In my mind, these things were going more towards editing.

Yeah. I think about this a lot, obviously. Sorry. Luma — Luma Agent is a version of this for video production, but you know, it's a one-off.

GitPod / Ona

I was going to get your quick takes on some other stuff that happens in recent news, and see if you have anything interesting. GitPod — very somewhat like, you know, different market, they're in like sort of the CI/CD market, but actually technically very impressive. I don't know if you've taken a real look at them.

Yeah. People on our team have talked to the GitPod team, and they're technically very strong. I actually — we're very bullish at Modal on the CI market as well, because as there's more agents — coding agents — they're going to run a lot more CI. And the primitives there can be much better.

I think there's a lot of wasted CI.

Yeah.

So is it just — let's filter: what is the highest-order bit here in improving CI for agents?

Well, there's a lot of wasted time in CI on preparing your artifacts, and getting you to the — basically, preparing your dependencies and whatnot. Obviously build systems help with that. But if you have primitives that are like memory snapshot and restore, can you just run CI more efficiently?

Oh, okay, interesting. Yeah. I mean, another form of on-demand compute.

Yeah, exactly.

Yeah. It needs the same, again, platform.

Yeah.

So — for those who don't know — GitPod rebranded to Ona. There was this whole thing. I got — I sort of semi-sounded the alarm at Cognition. I was like, you should take these guys seriously, because they're infra-red good. And then they joined OpenAI, and presumably we'll see Codex Cloud from the Ona team, which I think would be very very strong.

To me, teams like that that can set up the networking and the secure boundaries for like — your agents to have their own cloud each — effectively is what you're doing, kind of. And I'm just trying to draw the analogy or the differences. If you have studied them: what is the philosophical difference?

My sense is: maybe they didn't go after the right market at the right time. We, I guess, also got lucky with the agentic use case really taking off. And it's more of a sandbox-shaped thing than a "my setting is —"

Yeah. Sandboxes work.

Is sandbox. It's just like build-time sandboxes versus runtime sandboxes, and actually it turned out runtime was better.

Right. And the difference there is: runtime sandboxes have a different configuration surface of like how you configure images, how you attach storage —

Yeah.

Astral, Modular, and Language Choice

It's fascinating. Other people — Astral, also OpenAI. Also, like, Python tooling ecosystem — people. Are you still sort of bullish, building on top of Python? Also recently Modular also got bought by Qualcomm — just any of your takes there?

Yeah, I mean, we had Python as our first SDK language because that was the language that people did data and ML in. We actually now have Go and TypeScript SDKs as well. And our runtime is completely language — it isn't in Rust, but it's not tied to Python by any means.

We haven't seen — I think with inference and training stuff, people are still very Python. And the interesting thing with the agent stuff is: people use our TypeScript SDK a lot more, because they're not actually doing any ML.

I don't think we'll have to go beyond that super soon, because Python and TypeScript is still dominant.

The last two languages in the world.

Yeah.

That's it.

Well, English and prompting is —

English and prompting. I occasionally talk to people who try to build new languages. Even Bret Taylor, who's chairman of OpenAI — "we need a new language for LLMs." So no one has come across one, and I keep looking. Python and TypeScript are — you have a lot of data plus — but then also they are very imperfect as just as languages themselves.

Closing — Betting Everything on Agent Experience

Then my close is: I think Modal used to be a big bet on developer experience, and you've pivoted the team to agent experience. Is it, like — the way now — do you — can entire companies and unicorns, multi-unicorns, be built on just having better agent experience? Do you need something else?

It's a big part of our identity. It's not just the very tactical "how does an agent use the CLI?" But it's also: how easy is it to spin something up? What is your iteration time when you want to spin up a new service and you want to get something going in prod?

In practice, that matters a lot to people. And I think it will continue to matter. People are building stuff even faster. And if you give them ways to do it quickly and not have overhead — then —

I think the debate for me has been: do you do anything differently that is very fundamentally different for developer experience versus agent experience? You seem to be on the side of "they're like this — they're like —"

Cosine similarity of like 0.9 or whatever.

Yeah. I mean, pretty much.

The main shift for us has been, as I said: we built this benchmark, Modal Bench, to see where agents are lacking. And yeah — actually literally add surface area to a product if they're reaching for something. Like maybe this should just be a CLI.

They hallucinate their own features.

Yeah. And sometimes it makes sense: if they're reaching for this thing, it's product feedback — give it to them.

And then, actually moving — we used to only have logs and metrics in our UI, just moving all those things to CLI as well, so they're accessible in that form.

Simple as that. Cool. Thank you so much. This is a great update, and I can see why you guys have succeeded so much. It is really focus, but also really good execution.

Thanks. I mean, we have a long way to go.

All right. Thank you. Cool.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、把「DX 变 AX」的隐藏含义：这不是改名，是把语言基座换掉
表面上 Modal 只是把 SDK 团队从 Developer Experience 改成 Agent Experience，听起来像营销术语。但仔细看 Akshat 讲的：*"why would you have an agent read through hundreds of Kubernetes files and like write YAML that's not even typed"* —— 他们做的是一件更激进的事：**把基础设施折叠成一种"语言语法"**，让 agent 不是在"操作"云，而是在"写代码"。
这里有个反直觉的观察：过去十年 SaaS 的护城河都建立在"人机交互摩擦"上（Retool、Notion、Linear，都在优化人的注意力）。可是 agent 对摩擦的感知是完全不同的——它不会因为 UI 好看而更愿意用你，它只关心「函数签名可不可预测」和「错误信息能不能被解析」。**AX 一旦成为北极星，过去十年 DevTool 的护城河可能一夜之间被反转**——不是被替代，是被"重估

item.title: [Lex Clips] Sex in the Roman Empire | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最锋利的悖论：一个人同时是"最严厉的道德立法者"和"最叛逆的婚姻改革者"

真正让人愣住的不是查士丁尼娶了性工作者，而是同一个皇帝——他一边下诏认为同性行为会引发地震（一种把私人性生活直接接入宇宙秩序的因果观），一边让自己的舅舅专门修法，只为绕过"元老不得娶下等职业女性"的禁令。这不是虚伪，这是一种更深层的结构：当权力足够大时，法律不是约束权力的框架，而是权力用来剪裁现实的剪刀。你想娶谁——改法；你厌恶什么——立法。这个模式在现代政商精英的婚姻史、公司章程修改史里反复重演。查士丁尼只是把它做到了最坦白的程度，甚至在诏书里直接写"爱是没有边界的"——把私人情感强行注入公共法典，是极其现代的操作。

二、被忽略的洞察：罗马帝国其实是"高压条文 + 低强度执法"的原型

这段对话里最反直觉的一句话是 Kaldellis 那句"They're not actively going to track down, hunt down, monitor..."——帝国有严酷的道德法条，但没有配套的监控基础设施。这不是宽容，是技术性宽容 (technological tolerance)：不是他们不想管，是他们管不过来。这个洞察可以直接映射到今天：一个社会对"越轨"的实际容忍度，往往不由它的意识形态决定，而由它的监控成本曲线决定。

跨领域连接来了——这和 Foucault 的 panopticon 理论恰好构成倒影：福柯说现代社会通过全景监视把人变成自我审查者；而拜占庭恰恰相反，因为看不见，所以只能靠丑闻曝光才行动。这意味着 6 世纪的罗马人在日常性自由上可能比 21 世纪算法社会的人更"松"——只要你不搞出丑闻。这是个让人不太舒服的推论：所谓"进步"，某些维度上其实是监控密度的提升，而不是道德的松绑。

三、真正改变世界的不是查士丁尼，是 Tribonian 和他的委员会——这揭示了一个被系统性低估的历史机制

主持人问："这部法典还在影响今天吗？" Kaldellis 淡淡地说："When you study Roman law, you're studying Tribonian's committee." 一个 6 世纪的委员会，直接定义了后世 1500 年欧洲人对"财产"、"契约"、"人格"的想象——包括洛克、包括美国宪法里的产权观念、包括你今天签的任何一份租房合同。

这里藏着一个反常识的系统性真相：历史上最持久的影响力，很少来自"英雄叙事"里那个下决定的人，而来自那个下决定后被指派去做整理工作的委员会。查士丁尼打的仗大部分丢了，他重建的疆域一代人就崩了，但 Tribonian 编的书还在法学院的必修课里。

跨领域连接：这和现代开源项目的持久性悖论惊人相似——真正定义互联网的，不是那些明星 CEO，而是像 Linus Torvalds 主持的、Postgres 核心组维护的那种长期低调的编辑委员会。代码库和法典是同一种物体：一个持续被引用、修订、但结构稳定的规范性文本集合。这也解释了为什么查士丁尼身边那群"看不上眼的怪人"能成事——他直觉地理解了一件事：规范性工作 (normative work) 需要的是能坐得住、写得清楚的人，不是贵族。他招募的是拜占庭版的极客团队。

一个额外的、可能让你意外的观察：Theodora 的存在本身，可能是查士丁尼法典能覆盖"人格与地位"这么细致的一个隐藏原因。一个娶了社会最底层女性的立法者，比一个从未跨越阶级的皇帝，更能敏锐地意识到"法律地位 (legal personhood)" 是需要被明文界定的东西——因为他自己就用法律为他妻子重新定义过一次"人"。私人生活的越界，塑造了公共法律的精细度。这是历史里最容易被漏掉的一种因果链。

📝 转录文本

查士丁尼与狄奥多拉：一段颠覆阶级的婚姻

Justinian also marries Theodora, a woman who's a former sex worker, which is a controversial choice. By this point he's a senator and is holding all of these top generalships. And then he becomes emperor.

She was also very influential.

Yes. Very powerful — yes.

Very powerful under his… Yeah, during his reign.

So, the interesting thing about Justinian's choice of, let's say, associates — and that includes his wife — is that he seems to have just gone for people he thought were talented, or that would be good for him as a ruler, rather than their social class or prestige. And so, he accumulates around him quite a motley group of odd, sometimes disreputable people.

But they did what he wanted them to, and they were there with lasting consequences for world history. So, he has an eye for talent, and he doesn't hesitate to pick people that others might look down on if he thought they could get the job done. And these are some of the attributes that enable him to be as successful as he was.

So, that's his background. He's a Latin speaker primarily, but he knows Greek, too.

罗马法的重编：Corpus Juris Civilis 的诞生

So, here he is. Like I mentioned, the wars of conquest — he also overhauled the Roman law.

He appoints a committee.

Yep. As one does — [laughter] — to just take the whole of Roman law and condense it into what we today call the Corpus Juris Civilis, which is the body of Roman civil law. And this is a massive work of codification. It is our main source for Roman law. Like overwhelmingly, what we call Roman law is what Justinian's committee — and specifically this character called Tribonian, who was a very accomplished jurist — said it was.

And did that persist through time? Did that last?

Yes, still to this day. When you study Roman law, you're studying Tribonian's committee.

And so, in terms of our modern society, how much of an impact did that have — this codification, this stable law that ran the East Roman Empire?

Well, I mean, Roman legal concepts pervade everything from philosophy to political thought to actual Roman legal practice. It depends on what country you're in. So, in many of the countries of Europe, it is the current law — to some degree. I'm exaggerating, but legal scholars have to study Roman law. Even law students have to do that. Not so much in countries like the United States or Britain, which have the so-called common law system.

But ideas in Roman law about, for example, property rights, ownership, things like this — these were hugely important in formulating ideas, for example, about sovereignty in political thought. Yeah, it's one of the most important and influential acts of committee work ever done in Constantinople.

私法结构：人、物、诉讼的三分模式

So, going to Perplexity here and looking at some aspects of Justinian's laws, they include: a systematic structure of private law — persons, things, actions — strong protection of property and contracts, detailed family and inheritance, and a hierarchical status-based view of persons. These elements became the template for later civil law systems.

And some overall structures: tripartite schema, law organized around personae (legal subjects), things, and procedural actions. Essentially, who, what, and how of private law. And it goes on. A lot of things I'm reading here really sounds like what we think of as law.

Yeah.

Marriage.

Look, most of it's about property.

Yeah. Marriage is a legal status. Lawful matrimony required conformity to rules about age, citizenship, and connubium — or prohibited degrees, incestuous adoption relations, etc.

Oh, yes. Who you can marry, who not.

关于同性恋：地震、宗教与执法的悖论

What was their view of homosexuality?

Well, I mean, Justinian thought it caused earthquakes.

No, did it?

Yes.

Did it?

Yes.

Wow. Fascinating.

No — for him, yeah. So, for Christian emperors or Christian authorities, this is a reprehensible, immoral act. Depending, it could be punished. Justinian did try to punish it — pretty brutally. I mean, ultimately lethally.

However, this gets really tricky here. Because what counts as a homosexual act is not clear to everybody, right? And in addition to which —

[gasps]

— this is a society that generally will not go after, let's say, scandals or deviants, or however you want to call it, systematically. This is not like… there's no inquisition. There's no social police. If there's some sort of scandal and this comes out, then it's a problem. But they're not actively going to track down, hunt down, monitor, survey, interrogate people about their sex lives.

It isn't — despite having views that we may not agree with — sexuality was generally humane in the way of just kind of leaving people alone.

通奸、性工作与"改法娶妻"

I mean, that said — I'm just, I'm perplexed. You looked up Justinian Roman law view of sexuality. They're pretty harsh on like adultery and just getting freaky with it.

No, Justinian was more zealous in this matter, definitely.

Even though he married a sex worker.

Well, sex work wasn't illegal.

Right, but it's a bit spicy.

Yes. And in fact it was illegal for a senator to marry someone from such a disreputable profession — and he got his uncle to pass a law changing that rule so that he could.

Well. Yeah, love knows no bounds.

Actually, Justinian kind of says that in one of his laws.

Oh, well.

No, he clearly loved her. There is no question.

Love. I mean, you have to — like, this guy started as a peasant, surrounded himself [laughter] with all kinds of weirdos just because they were good at what they did, and it doesn't really matter class or not.

Yes, but there's a lot to be said against him too.

I like [him] so much. [laughter]

## 一、最锋利的悖论：一个人同时是"最严厉的道德立法者"和"最叛逆的婚姻改革者"
真正让人愣住的不是查士丁尼娶了性工作者，而是**同一个皇帝**——他一边下诏认为同性行为会引发地震（一种把私人性生活直接接入宇宙秩序的因果观），一边让自己的舅舅专门修法，只为绕过"元老不得娶下等职业女性"的禁令。这不是虚伪，这是一种更深层的结构：**当权力足够大时，法律不是约束权力的框架，而是权力用来剪裁现实的剪刀**。你想娶谁——改法；你厌恶什么——立法。这个模式在现代政商精英的婚姻史、公司章程修改史里反复重演。查士丁尼只是把它做到了最坦白的程度，甚至在诏书里直接写"爱是没有边界的"——把私人情感强行注入公共法典，是极其现代的操作。
